Title: Bunker
description: Possibly the first wargame with a hexagonal board, this game for two or four players comes with 20 pawns, 8 domed bunkers, 20 command cards, 4 x 6 sheets for minelaying records, 2 dice numbered 1-3 and 1 die with fields in white, black, blue and red. Each player has a starting area surrounded by barbed wire. Each player represents a different armed service: armor, air force, navy or artillery.
rules: Players place pieces and secretly record 3 minefields within their start areas. In turn they roll both dice. Color shows direction: white forward, black backward, blue sideways and red choice. One piece must move the exact number over open spaces unless ending on an enemy, capturing it. Landing on the red centre grants a card for immediate or later use. Player getting five pieces into the enemy's base wins, otherwise decided on points: 5 in enemy base, 2 per capture, 3 per survivor.

Title: Trade Winds
description: With WWII underway, this trade game focused on US trade with neutral Latin America.

Title: Bombs Away!
description: By itself, this is a dartboard printed with the overhead view of a city. It can be hung vertically and used like a regular dartboard. However, it was intended to be used with the Eagle Bombsight produced by the same company (listed separately in the database). Military targets are outlined in red, some letter-coded (e.g. F for factories, S for station, A for airport) and others obvious (docks and warehouses). Also shown are hospitals, a church and apartments.
rules: No explicit point values are assigned to the targets on the board. Hitting a military target counts for a point, hitting a civilian target loses a point and missing anything counts for zero. Highest points after an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Unterhaltungsspiele fur Soldaten
description: Even those on the front lines deserve some fun. This collection of common games was published by the German army for distribution to soldiers.  The paper envelope contains boards for Chess, Checkers, Belagerungsspiel, Wolf and Sheep, Solitaire, Salta, Halma, Dominoes and Lotto (Bingo). All the necessary pieces are contained in cardboard sheets.
rules: As per the individual games.

Title: Dictator
description: This is a game of colonial control rather than war between superpowers. The game uses lead miniatures: 8 Aviators (1/player), 12 submarines, 8 Battleships and 16 aeroplanes. The Aviators move around the circle by roll of the dice, collecting and losing money, armaments and political cards to be used in the game. The track is surrounded by 3 territories which hold the game's key resources- Manchukuo (Iron), Dutch East Indies (Oil) & Ukraine (Wheat). (Dan Cermak Collection)
rules: The goal of the game is to collect military forces so that you can control the 3 territories (1 at a time or if you are strong enough, all at once). The players move military units into and out of the territories on their turns, conquering weaker opponents (and taking their units). If you have the required military units in the territory and you have earned a propaganda card (by landing on the right space) you gain the resource of that territory. Having all 3 resources wins the game.

Title: Bomber Raid
description: Players take both bombing and defensive roles. Each player controls four bombers and one searchlight. Bombers try to fly from friendly territory across to the enemy side of the board. If they arrive directly over one of the target rows, they score points: from 10 for an oil depot to only 2 for bombing a city. Opposing is a cardboard searchlight beam with numbered perimeter squares to count movement.
rules: Player rolls one die each turn. Roll can be used to move a single bomber that exact number of spaces up, down, forward or back, or to rotate the searchlight beam by that many spaces along the numbered perimeter. A searchlight ending across a square with an enemy shoots it down, as does a friendly bomber moving to an adjacent facing space at the same height. Play ends after all bombers complete runs or are shot down. Player with most points wins.

Title: Kampf und Sieg
description: This chess analog has a colorful board with an overhead view of a varied landscape including a river and central town. One version came with wooden disks; another with metal playing pieces.
rules: The set-up areas are in opposite corners. Each type of piece has different move and attack capabilities. These include infantry, machineguns, artillery, tanks and aircraft. The river can be crossed only at bridges (except for air units). To win, a player must capture the opponent's Flag piece.

Title: Army Rummy
description: The game board features an image of both men and women in uniform playing the game in a military camp. The back of the board features another game called Lucky Roll.
rules: Cards are dealt out. First player plays lowest in a suit. New suit starts when one runs out. There are side pots for playing the Ace of Spades; King and Queen of Diamonds; King of Hearts; Queen of Clubs; Jack of Diamonds; and 10 of Hearts; and sequence of three. First to empty hand collects a chip for each remaining card from each other player. Players take all cards they played and compete for best poker hand to win that pot.

Title: Tanks in Action
description: This is simply a tank-warfare themed paper version of Battleships on a standard 10 x 10 grid.
rules: Standard Battleships

Title: Invasion (1941)
description: This is really just another chess-type game for two or four players on a 13 x 13 board. The board is bland, but its metal playing pieces are colorful (blue, red, black and gold). Each player gets 6 infantry, 2 tanks, 2 artillery, 2 pursuit planes and 2 bombers. The equivalent of the chess king is the Dictator, riding in a long open-topped limousine.
rules: Soldier moves 1 ahead, back or diagonally, but only captures diagonally. Tanks and artillery move up to 2, pursuit planes 3 in any direction and bombers 4 orthogonally only. Artillery, pursuit planes and bombers can attack over occupied spaces. The dictator moves only along the back row. A player is eliminated when the Dictator is captured. With 4 players, last team with surviving Dictator wins.

Title: Soldaten Kamaraden
description: This WWII game paints a jolly portrait of military life, whether in the army, air force or navy. Sure, there's drudgery like shining shoes and swabbing decks, but there's plenty of wine, women and song, along with playing cards, comedy skits, and showing off to civilians. The soldiers even seem to enjoy crossing a river in full kit and the field-kitchen grub is great. There is a second game on the back featuring a tour of greater Germany after the takeover of Austria and Czechoslovakia.
rules: Standard race game: first to the end of the 76-space track wins.

Title: Land and Sea War Games
description: The box includes two games. The first is an anti-aircraft dexterity game. The second is a roll-and-move game themed on tanks and artillery. Despite the "Sea" in the title, naval units appear nowhere in either game.
rules: In the anti-aircraft game, players drop or flip onto targets for positive or negative points. Highest total points wins. The roll and move game is unusual in that die rolls will move a player's piece in specific directions rather than by a fixed amount. First to reach the enemy base wins.

Title: Belagerungsspiel (Biedermeier)
description: This is a slightly later (circa 1820) German version of Belagerungsspiel, coloured by hand.
rules: See Belagerungsspiel (Napoleonic)

Title: Plane Parade
description: Players must identify planes from a silhouette that appears in a window, along with size, speed and other characteristics. If stumped, they can use a lever in the board to unveil more information. All of the information appears on wheels that are mounted underneath the board and spun to show one plane at a time in the window. There are two such disks, with 8 planes on each side for a total of 32.
rules: Player most successful at identifying planes and their specifications wins.

Title: Kampf zur See
description: This simple game offers nice graphics of WWI naval combat, including a sinking ship, a U-boat rescuing survivors. shellspouts, a line of battle and even a biplane dropping a bomb - still a very crude technique at that point in the war.
rules:
This is a very simple roll and move game. First to reach or exceed space 55 wins 2/3 of the pot; runner-up gets the rest.
There are various bonus and penalty spaces along the way. In particular, landing on space 30 (the sinking cruiser) eliminates the player from the game.

Title: Count Down
description: This builds a game around the complex process of preparing for a Saturn rocket launch. Each player has a "control pad" on their edge of the board that must be filled before launch. The cards have photos and historical details of people and equipment. The Saturn rockets are 4-stage plastic pieces. Costs are in Mios, or millions of dollars. There's even room for spying and sabotage.
rules: Players move by rolling dice, and must collect cards satisfying the nine conditions for a successful launch on their “control pad” as well as all four components of a Saturn rocket (Stages I, II, III and Space Capsule) - and of course the money to pay for them. Once set, they attempt a launch by rolling 6 dice, and must reach a total of 25 or more to succeed. They may try again on the following turn. First to launch successfully wins.

Title: Big Boom ... or Golden Age
description:
Between one and five players move Nation pieces cooperatively on an inward spiral toward the goal of a single world government through the United Nations, which will lead to a golden age of peace and prosperity. The remaining player represents the Atom Bomb and moves around a different “doomsday clock” circle. Components include 8 lettered Nation pieces, 34 Vote cards, 36 Goodnews cards, 36 Badnews cards and markers for the A-bomb and the number of its trips around the clock.

​
rules: Nations players roll two dice to move any of their pieces. A Nation landing on a "?" space allows all Nations players to draw and act on a Goodnews card. The Bomb player can die rolls either to move the bomb forward or to move Nations pieces backward onto a "?". This forces all Nations players to take and act on a Badnews card. Bomb player wins by completing a player-count number of circuits before all Nations reach the middle of the board.

Title: Combate Naval
description: This game shows the full range of naval participants in the European theatre of WWI: Britain, France, Russia, Germany, Austria-Hungary and Turkey. It was published in neutral Spain, which accounts for the inclusion of the Mediterranean naval powers. Each power is represented by a single warship containing dots numbered 2 to 12. The board is a brightly colored and dynamic melee that includes smaller boats, two submarines in the foreground and airships and planes overhead.
rules: Players take turns rolling 2D6 and a cube with a flag on each face. The rolling player places a marker on the number equal to the sum of the two normal dice on the ship whose flag was rolled. A player who rolls his own ship, instead pays that amount into the pot. A player who rolls a number already pays that amount to the target player. Eliminating a ship gives the shooter 50 from the pot; the loser is out and pays 25. Last survivor takes 2/3 of the pot, runner up the rest.

Title: Grandi Manovre, Le
description: This pre-WWII Italian title is a fairly sophisticated wargame. The detailed topographical map shows elevation, rivers, lakes and marshes, primary and secondary roads, and defensive wire and trench lines. Also marked are bases, airfields, and ammunition dumps. The metal pieces represent cavalry, infantry (including elite troops such as Bersaglieri, machineguns, field and heavy artillery, anti-aircraft guns, tanks, air squadrons and supply trucks.
rules: Each player in turn rolls 2D6. The total is the combined limit for both movement and attack. Most units move only one square per pip, but cavalry moves 2, tanks 3 and airplanes 4. An attack requires 2 pips. Artillery units can attack at range but have only 6 units of fire, after which they must be replenished by a truck convoy from the ammo dump. There are special rules for air, which must be able to return to base or crash, and may be intercepted by enemy air or anti-aircraft guns (potentially causing damage in the form of lost fuel pips). The winner is the first to occupy the enemy's two bases.

Title: Friegur
description: The title is an acronym "Feldgraue rangen in Ehren, gewannen unsterblichen Ruhm” (“Field grays fight with honor, gaining immortal fame”) and it was published by an ethnic German in Danzig just after Hitler came to power. The chess-like board is 15 wide by 14 deep, with blocks of 6 dark brown squares forming an X across the board. The playing pieces in red and black bakelite are huge (up to 92mm/3.6" high).
rules: Pieces include infantry, cavalry, tanks, artillery, airplanes, Leader and Flag. All moves are orthoganal (forward, back or sideways) by a variable number of squares. Pieces can only be eliminated by a combination of two attacking units. There are five ways to win: occupy five spaces of the opponent’s home territory; leave the enemy with fewer than five ground pieces; eliminate all enemy infantry; eliminate the enemy leader; or leave the enemy without a legal move.

Title: Union Quartette
description: This game is a trivia quiz on the events and personalities of the then-recent US Civil War. The deck is made up of sets of four cards. Each card has a title and three questions. The answer to each question is the title of another card in the set.
rules: This is an educational variant of Quartette (as in the popular Authors). Cards are dealt out to the players. In turn, they call on another player for a card that would help them complete and lay down a set. If successful, they call again; otherwise the turn passes. Winner of a round is the first to lay down all cards in hand. However, players must know the answers to the questions to ask for the corresponding cards.

Title: Year of the Rat
description: An almost real-time simulation of the 1972 North Vietnamese offensive, published in the same year.
rules: Standard odds-based hex combat game, with additional air power and naval bombardment rules.

Title: Battle of the Tanks
description: This extremely rare Milton Bradley game  is, as the title suggests, about tank on tank warfare. The board has a track six spaces wide. Starting from their own end, opponents move toward each other and try to eliminate the other's tanks. The game is intended to be played in matches of five games to determine the winner.
rules: Each player in turn rolls two dice and moves 2 tanks. One tank must move the number of spaces indicated by one die and the other tank moves according to the second die. A lone remaining tank may choose either die roll. Movement is orthogonal only and cannot be back and forward in the same turn. Landing by exact count on an enemy tank captures it. Each captured tank is worth 5 points, as is winning each game in the match. The player with the most total points wins.

Title: Central America
description: This massive complex game uses a conventional hex grid but enables simulation of the many aspects of civil war, including insurgency, counter-insurgency and foreign intervention. The counters represent 23 different countries and political factions, divided into two main factions: Communists and Allies. Scenarios range from some simple historical and hypothetical engagements all the way to World War III.
rules: There is a 64-page basic rulebook, a 40-page advanced rulebook and a 68-page scenarios booklet. In addition to the full range of armor and infantry units on the ground, there are helicopters for both transportation and attack, and air units capable of electronic warfare, standoff air attack, tactical bombardment and defensive close air support, as well as the optional use of cluster, incendiary and smart bombs.

Title: Battle for Hue
description: This is a tactical-level simulation of the bloody battle over the city of Hue during the 1968 Tet Offensive. Modern tactics meet medieval walls - with the Marines and ARVN troops not allowed to shoot at the Palace of Peace in the middle of the city. It has a very bloody combat results table that reflects the intensity of the fighting.
rules: Hex movement and combat.

Title: Contraband Control
description: This Canadian game presumed that WWII would be like WWI in the Atlantic: more a matter of blockading Germany than feeding Britain. The map covers North and South America, Europe, Africa and some of Asia. The seas are covered in a web of movement tracks, with red mined spaces, black "contraband control" spaces and four red circles in the Atlantic providing bases for Allied cruisers.
rules: One player tries to move freighters from the Americas, Africa and India into Axis ports. The other moves cruisers that try to capture freighters by landing on them. Freighters also can come to grief on mines and control stations. Winning conditions are not clear from the board.

Title: Vozdushnie Manievrei (Воздушные маневры)
description: This air combat game is focused on dogfighting and strafing the enemy's airfields. The board is covered with a network of flight lines that planes must follow. They must move forward either directly or along a curved line: no reverse movement or sharp turns are allowed. The playing pieces are red and white plastic. There also is a pair of cards with sets of five numbers. Each player secretly selects one line as his anti-aircraft locations.
rules: Each player starts with six fighters on any of the yellow circles, and moves one of them each turn by the roll of a die. Rolling a six grants an additional roll. Landing on an enemy plane shoots it down. A plane landing on an enemy AA site is shot down. A plane reaching any of the opposing red circles strafes an airfield, destroying any planes still there. A player wins by destroying all enemy aircraft.

Title: Gliding
description: This game shows "a wonderful sport" that became a critical military technology. As the rules note: "No noisy motors disturb the stillness". The board shows many gliders in flight. There are bonus spaces for altitude and distance records, and penalty spaces including forced landing, crashed and lost above the clouds.
rules: Up to 6 players race to be first to the end of the 80-space track.

Title: Combat
description: This pinball games shows soldiers and a tank on the ground, but features anti-aircraft missiles and a Convair F-102 Delta Dagger in the skies above. The F-102 was the first US supersonic aircraft to go operational, starting in 1956. Designed to intercept Soviet bombers, more than 1,000 were built.
rules: Standard pinball: Players use a spring-loaded launcher to send marbles around the top and into the playing field. They score points for landing in targets on the board, and in pockets at the bottom. Player with the highest total number of points after an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Military Fortune Tellers
description: The tarot deck goes to war. The deck has 4 suits (Hearts, Bells, Stars and Doves), each with 10 numbered cards and 4 face cards (Soldier, Sailor, Pilot, Nurse - the latter two female). Each card has two or more words along each of its four sides.
rules: The rules suggest laying out cards in two rows (with 8 each in the example). The first card is turned over and placed vertically, the second horizontally and so on. When the second row is begun, cards are laid first horizontally and then vertically (opposite orientation to first row). Players then try to divine a fortune by reading the words on the cards consecutively, picking one of those available on each card.

Title: Tactic-Tank
description: This self-published game by Eugène Petiot of Paris anticipated that the next war with Germany would focus on battles between tanks. His playing pieces show very early models: the Renault R-35 light tank for the British and French counters, and machine-gun equipped Panzer Is for the Germans. By the time war broke out, both sides had better tanks, but the Germans had figured out how to use them most effectively.
rules: Rules are not shown, but the layout suggests that a player captures an enemy piece by getting two friendly pieces adjacent to it.

Title: Cards (Skat Military)
description: This 32-card Skat deck includes numerous images of German land, air and sea operations and prominent military men. This includes the 42 cm Morser used to smash the Belgian forts at the outset of the war; the Chinese enclave of Kiautschau (Tsingtao); U-9 and U-21 with their commanders Otto Weddigen and Otto Hersing; and light cruiser Emden, which under Karl von Muller took 15 prizes and sank Russian cruiser Zhemchug and French destroyer Mousquet in 1914.
rules: Standard Skat rules

Title: Belagerungsspiel (Early Napoleonic)
description: Belagerungsspiel (Siege Game) is a classic asymmetrical move and jump game. It features a cross-shaped board with 33 points connected by orthogonal and diagonal lines. The top arm of the cross (9 spaces) is shown as the castle to be defended. The game was first published in Germany during the Napoleonic era has been published in many languages and with many visual themes since then. The generic game also is known as Festungsspiel (Fortress Game).
rules: The defender has two pieces, placed anywhere on the 9-space fortress area. The attacker has 24 pieces, placed everywhere outside the fortress. Attacking pieces may move along any path toward the fortress, but only into empty spaces. The defenders capture attacking pieces by jumping over as in checkers. The attackers win by occupying all 9 fortress spaces; the defenders by capturing all but 8 attackers.

Title: Up Against the Wall, Motherfucker!
description: Despite its outrageous name and tongue-in-cheek rules, Dunnigan designed it as a serious simulation of social conflict. One player runs the Administration, while the other represents the Radicals. The two sides struggle for influence across a range of 11 social subgroups, from the Trustees and Tenured Faculty to various student categories and the surrounding Harlem Community. Columbia U's Low Library is in the middle of the board, with tracks leading to each subgroup.
rules: Each side gets influence points used to push the other side inward (Administration) or outward (Radicals). Radical influence points start strong and decline. The Administration starts weak but grows. Each turn, Rads play first, distributing influence across tracks as they see fit, then Admin does the same. Tracks with both cause "combat". Contingency Cards give bonuses or penalties each turn. After 12 turns, VPs counted based on track positions.

Title: Blackout
description: This US game about protecting cities from bombardment was published while the UK was undergoing the Blitz but the US had not yet entered the war.
rules: Each space on a player's board has a die roll combination. A player rolls dice and places a black rectangle on the corresponding space. If there is no corresponding vacant space, turn passes to next player; otherwise, roll again. First to completely cover a board wins.

Title: Churchill Checkers
description: This simple game used British Prime Minister Winston Churchill as its selling point. The game is a kind of circular checkerboard printed on a wooden block. It is played with small wooden pegs with one end colored either red or blue. The pieces are stored in a hole bored into the side of the wooden block and stopped up with a small cork.
rules: Players start on opposite sides of the circle and move pieces as in checkers. However, they may jump opposing pieces either inward or sideways. Any piece that reaches the crown space in the middle of the circle becomes “kinged” and can then jump outwards as well.

Title: Sea Battle
description: This version of Battleships was published after war broke out in Europe but before Pearl Harbor.
rules: Standard Battleships.

Title: Guerra Aereo-Navale
description: This "Air-Naval War" game was published on June 10, 1940, the day Italy entered WWII. It has wooden pieces representing both targets such as a railway station, ammo dump and bridge, and attacking airplanes, ships and submarines (two per player) along with a single torpedo boat. The playing board has a red home area on one side and blue on the other. Pieces move along colored lines across the ocean between them. There is a small island in the middle.
rules: Players move one piece each turn, and must alternate between ships, submarines and airplanes. A die roll determines how far the piece can move. Players capture a piece (unit or target) by landing on it. Pieces move forward only until reaching the far side, at which point planes reverse course and ships reset to their starting spaces. Players get the torpedo boat, which can move in either direction, after losing three units. The winner is the first to destroy both enemy planes and half the ships.

Title: Battlefield
description: This pinball game features an aerial view of a target area that includes an enemy division, bridges, town, airfield, missile site, tank battalion and truck convoy, as well as individual soldiers and vehicles at the bottom.
rules: Players use a spring-loaded launcher to send marbles into the target area, scoring points according to where the marble lands. Highest total points after an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Hood's War Game
description: This card game was produced as an advertising vehicles for C.I. Hood & Co., makers of sarsaparilla and cure-all liver pills, as well as photo sets and paper dolls. The deck has 13 sets of four, each with a theme such as U.S. or Spanish leaders, battleships of each side, sea battles and American heroes.
rules: Standard Authors/Happy Families.

Title: Electric Patrol
description: Each player tries to lay a telephone wire from a Base in one corner to an Outpost in the opposite corner. Each player gets 7 plastic soldiers, in red and green. Soldiers start on a triangle of spaces in the home half of the board and on the Base (the latter carrying the telephone thimble on top. Each player has a circle of 8 metal contacts with a rotating lever that can touch them to complete a circuit. The board is marked with various obstacles like dense forest washed out roads and bridges.
rules: The Green player starts by moving his own lever one contact in either direction. He then moves the Red lever, counting each contact until a light flashes. This is the number of spaces one soldier can move, in any direction but in a straight line to the full count of spaces. Players then repeat the process in turn. Landing on obstacles may eliminate the soldier or delay progress. Passing over an enemy eliminates the piece. First player to get the telephone to the Outpost wins.

Title: Game of Defense (Pacific)
description: This game was hastily re-issued in a Pacific version after Pearl Harbor. The pieces and rules were identical to the original. Only the maps and objective cards were different. The only difference in the boxes was a small sticker saying "Atlantic" or "Pacific".
rules: Rules are the same as for Game of Defense (Atlantic)

Title: Modern Warfare
publisher: Ed Karl, Theodore Nitza (self-published by designers)
description: This game's units are large silver and gold metal soldiers, aircraft, tanks and artillery. The board has four jagged trench-type lines across each half of the board. Players place their pieces along one or more of their own lines.  The game uses a spinner for both movement and combat. Combat is affected by the type of unit and where it is placed. Two simpler games are included: Trench Warfare leaves out tanks and aircraft, and Hit and Miss is a straight-up shootout.
rules: The game begins with an artillery duel, first between single pieces and then by both batteries firing together until one side loses both pieces. Then tanks and aircraft fight until one side runs out. The winners retire to their reserve, leaving the final combat round to the infantry.

Title: Radio-Sende-Spiel
description: This game focuses on discouraging German players from listening to foreign broadcasts. The 100-space track starts in Stuttgart and winds around Germany and surrounding countries, ending in Berlin. It is most notable for humiliating players unfortunate enough to land on a foreign transmitter space.
rules: Players roll and move along the track. A player who lands on a transmitter in Paris, Moskau, Bukarest or Beromunster (Switzerland) must pay a pfennig into the pot, stand up, say loudly “I have committed a crime!” and go back to start.  A player landing on London, described in the rules as the “main enemy”, must pay five pfennigs to the pot, stand up, shout “God D*** England!” and is out of the game. First to reach the target at 100 wins the pot.

Title: Gulf Strike
description: This massive game models a potential conflict that actually came to pass years later. It simulates combined air, naval and ground operations in the Persian Gulf region, with 910 counters and four mapsheets. A second edition in 1988 added another mapsheet and 130 counters. And in 1990, a third edition added the Desert Shield expansion module, with 12 pages of extra rules and 260 new counters to reflect the actual invasion of Saudi Arabia and Kuwait.
rules: Hex combat

Title: Bomber Command
description: This brightly-colored board sees up to four players competing to bomb Berlin as quickly as possible. Each player moves on a quadrant of the board, leading to a pair of final tracks to the target in the middle of the board.
rules: Very similar to Snakes and Ladders, but each player has four bombers. Players take turns rolling a die. A 6 is required for a plane to leave its base. Along the way, pieces may land on squares that move them ahead or back. Each player keeps moving the first plane until it reaches the target and bombs Berlin in the middle of the board. Then the next plane may leave base, and so on. The first player to have all planes reach Berlin wins the game.

Title: Army Successful Career
description: This early woodblock sugoroku shows the potential career path for officers in the 19th century Japanese army.
rules: Standard sugoroku: first to reach the middle space wins.

Title: Strand War Game, The
description:
This mid-war game superimposes a translucent checkerboard pattern over a map of western Europe. It stretches from England to Germany in the north, and from France to Italy and Austria in the south. The playing pieces are paper on wooden disks. Playing pieces include infantry, cavalry and artillery. The two objective spaces are Bordeaux and Berlin. Each side has two fortified spaces that can only be captured by 
Artillery.
rules: The playing pieces start in opposing diagonals on the board and move only on the dark squares. Infantry and cavalry can move one space forward or sideways, and can capture an enemy piece by jumping over it to a vacant space. Artillery may move backwards as well, and may jump over two spaces to capture an enemy. Only Artillery can capture an enemy in a fortress space. The first player to occupy the enemy objective wins.

Title: On Guard
description: This is a simple spin and move game for 2-4 players. One player acts as the enemy Guard, while the others try to complete a reconnaissance of the enemy position. If a guard lands on a recon player, that player becomes the new Guard, and the guard player begins a recon route. Movement for all players is by a 1-4 spinner.
rules: First player to complete a reconnaissance route wins

Title: Im Schutzengraben
description: This game offers vivid depictions of trench warfare on the Western Front. The box shows German troops on guard. On the board, French troops throw grenades,  a British soldier works a trench mortar, Belgian soldiers defend the remaining sliver of their country and a German machine-gun unit fires toward the enemy. The art is by German impressionist and modernist painter Fritz Neumann.
rules: Up to six can play, three Germans and one each French, British and Belgian. The metal playing pieces start on their own or an allied space on the outer ring. Players roll 2D6, moving counter-clockwise until they land exactly on a space with an enemy flag, soldier, airship or plane. This allows movement inward to the next ring. Some spaces cause delay or retreat. Winner is the first to reach the middle.

Title: Jeu du Front
description: This game just puts a wartime theme on a standard board for noughts and crosses or X's and O's.
rules: X's and O's.

Title: Luftkampfspiel
description: The box shows a two-engine monoplane bomber with fixed landing gear and an open cockpit and forward gunnery position. The pursuing fighters are still biplanes, as are all the planes shown on the game board. The game pieces themselves, however, have moved a generation ahead. The five colored fighters in the mix are all twin-engine monoplanes, as is the larger silver piece representing the attacking bomber.
rules: The bomber starts from the airfield at the upper left of the game board. The fighters begin from the fields at the lower left and right. The fighter players try to intercept the bomber before it can reach the target factories in the middle of the board.

Title: L'Attaque
description: This classic wargame is still published as Stratego. The first edition was self-published by Mlle Hermance Edan in 1909, and a few years later Gibson in the UK bought English rights and published versions continuously through the mid-1970s. This was the first wargame enabling secret set-up and movement without a referee.
rules: Each player has 36 playing pieces on metal stands. The name and value of each piece faces the owner, with the back blank. The board is 9 spaces wide by 10 deep, with three impassable "lakes" in mid-board. Players take turns moving a single piece forward, back or sideways. If this ends adjacent vertically to an enemy piece, the player may announce an attack. Both pieces are revealed. Lower value is removed (both if tied). The Spy kills the General. Mines don't move but kill anything that attacks.

Title: Adler Luftwaffenspiel
description: This new “Europa” edition shows German dominance of Europe by 1941. The box bears 13 flags of conquered or allied countries: Germany, Italy, Romania, Hungary, Finland, Slovakia, Netherlands, Spain (friendly neutral), France, Belgium, Norway, Denmark and Bulgaria. Austria, Poland and western Czechoslovakia are not shown because they were absorbed into Grossdeutschland (greater Germany). Originally published as Adler Luftverdigungspiel.
rules: Players try to bomb enemy cities and defend their own. There are 12 tracks. Each player attacks on 6 and defends the others. Six dice of different colors are used to move bombers and fighters, fire flak and determine bomb damage. Planes shoot down enemies by landing on them be exact count. Game ends when all bombers have been shot down or returned to base. Points are scored for shooting down enemy planes, for bombing targets and for bombers returning safely to base.

Title: Dover Patrol
description: This first naval version of Gibson's L'Attaque features individually named warships - some historical and some imaginary.
rules: Similar to L'Attaque, but the enemy flag is in a harbor with walls, and a player must get to the flag and bring it back home to win.

Title: Conflict
description: The four-player board has interconnected land and sea areas. Pieces represent land, sea and air forces. Land pieces only move on land and sea pieces only on sea, while air pieces can move over and end their moves on either. There are "bridge" squares that allow land pieces to reach the central island and naval pieces to cross from one sea to another. Pieces are metal in yellow, orange, red and green.
rules: In turn, players roll two dice, each of which moves a piece that many spaces. Pieces can move horizontally, vertically or diagonally in any combination. Land and sea pieces capture an enemy by moving onto its square. Air pieces capture by moving over an enemy piece. Some pieces start in reserve, with one released by any roll of 7. A player eliminates another player by moving onto his home base. Last player standing is the winner.

Title: Victo
description: This game turns the bingo card into a tablet of inspirational wartime quotes. Most are from the past:  Patrick Henry’s “Give me liberty or give me death”; Admiral Dewey’s “You may fire when ready, Gridley”; Teddy Roosevelt’s “Speak softly – carry a big stick” and Woodrow Wilson’s “America cannot be an ostrich." Others are current:  Admiral “Bull” Halsey's “Hit hard – hit fast – hit often,” and War Production Board head Donald Nelson's “Production lines are battle lines.”
rules: As in Bingo, each player has a 5x5 card, but with a famous quote in each space.  There are 75 tiles each with a single quote. These are pulled at random, and players mark off the appropriate spot on their cards if and where it appears. First player to an agreed goal (e.g. complete a single line or fill the entire card) wins.

Title: Smash 'Em
description: Subtitled "Can the Allies be Defeated?", this is a simpler WWI game from the designer of the pre-war War Tactics, or Can Great Britain be Invaded?" The board is a plain brown on white with a 16 x 13 grid that covers the Western Front from Paris to Berlin and from Holland in the north to the edge of Switzerland. Playing pieces are thick cardboard disks.
rules: Players use ground and air units, including the "engine of destruction, the airship", with different movement and capture powers. In attacking, players must steer around both Holland and Luxembourg, which remained neutral. As in its predecessor game, the winner is the first to occupy all four squares of the opponent's capital.

Title: Unsere Wermacht im Manover
description:
Perhaps the last game to feature cavalry as a useful military force was the mid-1930s Unsere Wehrmacht im Manover (Our army on maneuver).
While the game box shows cavalry in action, the game board presages the imminent shift to armored warfare. The tank token represents only one of the 30 counters on each side, and a cavalry dragoon (mounted rifle) unit is featured in the foreground at the lower right of the game board. But crossing the central bridge is an early turreted tank.
rules: The game’s mechanism dates back to WWI, using the same format as the 1917 Kampf gegen Russland. Players have small wooden sticks with flat ends that are used to propel the cardboard disk playing pieces (like Tiddledy Winks). Players capture pieces by landing on top of them, and must be careful not to land in the river. This is shown on the map as a serious obstacle, and troops are shown using assault boats and a pontoon bridge as well as existing stone and wooden crossings.

Title: Commander in Chief
description: This is a turn of the century game on the familiar theme of rising through the military ranks, from Private to Commander in Chief. The central illustration appears to reflect the Spanish-American War, with the right-hand part showing a distinct resemblance to Frederic Remington's 1898 painting "Captain Grimes's Battery Going Up El Poso Hill".
rules: It is a simple roll-and-move game, with the winner being first player to reach Commander in Chief.

Title: Stukas greifen an!
description: This is an unabashed celebration of the tactical air power that was so important in blitz warfare. Most planes pictured are the gull-winged Ju-87 dive bombers (Sturzkampfflugzeug, hence Stuka). However, a Ju 88 is featured on both box and board. The planes are shown delivering destruction against land and ocean targets alike, with devastating effect.
rules:
The game board is cross shaped with 67 linked and numbered circles.  Five green planes start from spaces 1-5 and red planes from 62-66. Players try to move across the board and back without being shot down. Players roll a die and move one plane that many spaces. They shoot down enemies by jumping over. Searchlight and AA spaces help defend. Players score points for each enemy shot down and each plane that reaches the enemy spaces and returns safely.
​

Title: Light of Morning Sun
description: This game shows the first year of the Russo-Japanese War. In the first panel, Japanese Foreign Minister Jutaro Komura meets Russian Minister to Japan, Roman Rosen on Feb. 6, 1904. Two days later, the Japanese fleet launched a pre-emptive strike in three locations: Port Arthur, Vladivostok and Chemulpo Bay (second panel). The central end space shows news of the surrender of Port Arthur, which only happened on the day of publication.
rules: Sugoroku

Title: Jet Race Game
description: The box features the F-106A Delta Dart interceptor flown by Major Joseph W. Rogers on Dec. 15, 1959, when he won one of the last of the Thomson air trophy races with a world record speed of 1,526 miles per hour (2,456 km/hr), the first plane to surpass the 1,500 mph mark. The painting on the box is by famed American artist Charles H. Hubbell. The board has a 30-space track looping around a map of the United States. Pieces and 1-6 spinner are thick glossy cardboard.
rules: Each player gets 4 planes. First to reach the end of the track with all four planes wins.

Title: Stratego
description: This name was first registered during WWII in the Netherlands and published shortly after the war ended. It has become the most popular form of the game that started as L'Attaque in 1909, and continues to be published today.
rules: Standard L'Attaque.

Title: Road to Victory
description: This is a simple roll-and-move game for 2 to 4 players. The 100-space track starts and ends at Berlin. The game is notable for the timing of its publication, after the invasion of Poland but before Nazi Germany seized Denmark and invaded Belgium and the Netherlands. There is even a reference to the possibility of Romania joining the Allies. Also notable are two mentions of the Polish submarine Orzel, which escaped to England in 1939. Spaces 50 and 51 show it picking up survivors of a torpedoed ship and then escaping through the Skagerrak.
rules: The track offers a choice of routes. Players advance around the map by rolling a single die, with a roll of 6 causing the player to move backward. The winner is the first to return to Berlin and ensure the "triumph of democracy" by putting the Nazi leaders on trial.

Title: Sekai Rekyo So Yugi
description: The Battle Game of the World Great Powers offers an unusual Japanese perspective on WWI. Designed by Noburo Sato, it takes a metaphysical approach to the Great War. There are eight player nations: Britain, Germany, France, Russia, Austria-Hungary, Japan, Italy and the United States. Each has four pieces representing Armaments, Economy, Morals and Knowledge.  The goal is to get all four pieces to the center of the board, which represents the Ideal State.
rules: In play, it resembles Parcheesi, but for twice as many players. Each piece starts from the owning country’s corner space and proceeds by single die roll clockwise around the track of blue squares to its home track toward the center. A piece landing on a rival sends it back to start. If a third country then lands on the same space, the second country’s piece goes back to start. However, the first country then gets a die roll, and on a 1 returns to the space and banishing the third player's piece.

Title: PAX
description: This lavishly illustrated card game has players compete to collect enough countries around the world to enforce one of four types of PAX or world peace. Each of the 48 country cards has it outlined on a map, with its name, a region, a point value, possible PAX support and a brief description. Poker chips are used to bid for cards and count for scoring. There are four possible PAX coalitions: Pax Americana, Pax Europeana, Pax Atlantica or Pax Muscovita ("a peace dictated from Moscow").
rules: Each player is dealt 5 country cards and gets 100 points in chips. Cards are turned over and auctioned one at a time, with the winner paying into the pot. Play continues until a player can form a PAX set. If the deck runs out without a PAX, there are three rounds of trading. Winner gets points equal to the pot; others subtract difference between the starting 100 and what they have left. Start a new round and repeat until one player reaches an agreed total.

Title: Big Shot
description: This bagatelle (pinball) game has an odd combination of images, with the large central artillery piece and two smaller ones on the sides shown with very old-fashioned crenellated battlements! The spring-loaded plunger launches metal balls up the right side. In addition to the usual U-shaped ball traps, there are pivoted levers on each side to open high-value pockets and spinning dials with variable values toward the bottom.
rules: Highest score for any agreed number of shots wins.

Title: NSKK & HJ-Vehrkersspiel
description: This traffic rule game was published by the Hitler Jugend and the Nationalsozialistisches Kraftfahrkorps, a paramilitary wing of the Nazi Party. Players drive various vehicles around town, obeying the rules while completing transportation assignments. The vehicles are painted wood, and some come with riders. The board is an abstract layout of roads in a town center.
rules: Players each take a colored vehicle and draw cards to determine their starting location and assignment. When they complete the first one, they take another, and so on. Player to complete the most assignments by game end wins.

Title: Colonne Blindée, La
description: This land variant of Battleships was in Oct. 1943, while France was still occupied by the Germans. Its pieces are the same size, but represent an attacking tank force and a defending set of fortifications. The cardboard pieces are placed on large 10 x 10 boards, while small boards are used for plotting. Small red and green cardboard disks mark hits and misses on the plotting boards, while white ones are used to mark hits on the pieces on the large boards.
rules: Each player has three large (3-space), three medium (2-space) and 5 small (1-space) pieces. These are laid out on the large boards in secret. Players take turns calling out a single square. If the defender has a piece on that square, he must call the hit and name the piece. A hit grants the attacker another shot. The first player to eliminate all the opponent's pieces wins.

Title: Glydor
description: Unlike most games, the objective is to stay in the air the longest and avoid reaching the end of the 38 space track. The box shows a rather primitive glider with a pilot sitting in the open air. The board is unadorned, with colored spaces on a winding track across a deep blue background.
rules: Up to four players try to keep their gliders in the air longest. Move is by a spinner with results ranging from +7 to -2. Players may move either the sum of the numbers on opposite ends of the spinner, or by the difference between them. Three spaces force the player either up or down as in Snakes and Ladders.

Title: Waterloo
description: This game is based on Napoleon's final campaign in 1815. The map board covers northern France and southern Belgium.
rules: Players maneuver their pieces to try and capture those of their opponent.

Title: South American Travel and Trading Game
description: This is two games in one featuring trade and travel still possible within the Americas as WWII raged everywhere else. The map board covers the US, Central and South America and the Caribbean, with a network of routes connecting the cities. The game features a detailed cutaway diagram of the Flying Clipper Ship, described as the largest aircraft of its time, military or civil. It could carry 74 passengers and 10 crew with a range of 4,275 miles.
rules: In the trading game, players start by drawing two Buy and two Sell cards. They spin and move to the South American destinations wanting their US Sell goods, and then to the cities with the goods they need to Buy. All destinations must be reached by exact spin. First player to return to the US with the goods wins. In the travel game, players draw a ticket with a starting US city and 6 destinations. First player to reach all 6 and return is the winner.

Title: Air Cav
description: This game simulates tactical air combat, with playing pieces representing helicopters, vehicles and infantry squads. There are two maps, one for combat in Europe and the other for the Middle East. Each hex is 100 meters wide. There are counters for the United States, Britain, West Germany, Israel, the Soviet Union and Syria. Each game turn represents 2 minutes of combat.
rules: Hex movement and combat.

Title: Extended Broadside
description: This unusual game appears to be a huge, handcrafted, 4-player version of Battleships played on an octagonal board. It has large metal and wood miniature ships, metal planes, mines and islands, wooden and plastic markers. The board surface is painted onto a fibreboard base, with plain dividers.
rules: Ships may be played diagonally as well as vertically. Before play starts, each player announces the location of his island, minefields and planes. These, like the regular shots that follow, affect the same square on all other players' board segments. In turn, each player may fire a regular shot, a plane or a mine. A mine hit sinks a ship at once; a plane hit and any one other also sink a ship. Using a plane means missing a turn; a mine, 2 turns. A player may move a ship instead of firing.

Title: Dewey Game, The
description: This game was published to cash in on Admiral Dewey's fame after his victory at Manila during the Spanish-American War. Players collect letters to form the names of 14 American admirals of the time.
rules: Players draw a hand of 10 tiles and try to form names from the list. On each turn, a player draws a face-down tile and if possible starts or adds it to a name in front of him. A player may steal a face-up tile from another player if his own partial name is closer to completion than his opponent's. Completing a name grants a point value ranging from 10 - 100. Game ends when no further play possible. The player with the highest point value is the winner.

Title: Von Deutschland nach Amerika mit unseren Handels-U-Booten
description: This game celebrates the voyages of the German cargo submarine Deutschland to the USA in 1916, when America was neutral. The Deutschland at the time was the largest U-boat ever built. It made two trips in 1916, bringing cargoes of chemical dyes, medical drugs, gemstones and securities and returning with hundreds of tons of nickel, tin and rubber. The box cover and board feature artwork by renowned artist Sigmund Socholkowski.
rules: First to the end of the 80-space track wins.

Title: Liberty Flag Game, The
description: Each card in this 35-card deck features the flag and key statistics on a country. Conspicuously absent are Germany, Japan and Italy. Also missing are neutrals such as Sweden, Spain, Switzerland and Turkey. Included are all countries in the Americas, occupied countries in Western Europe, Britain and its Dominions but not colonies, the Phillipine Commonwealth (sic), the Soviet Union and Nationalist China. The cards form 17 pairs and a Statue of Liberty wild card.
rules: The full deck is dealt out among the players. Players first lay down any pairs in their hands. The dealer begins by drawing a card blindly from the player on his right, hoping to make another pair. That player then draws from the right, and so on round the circle. Play continues until all pairs have been laid down, with the player left holding the Statue of Liberty card being the winner.

Title: Wir fahren gegen Engeland
description: This submarine game is supposed to continue until every ship in the British navy has been sunk! Players follow one of two tracks around Britain. On numbered spaces they encounter (and sink) specific warships. There is a deck of cards, each with the silhouette of a warship along with its class, commissioning date, tonnage, length, crew size and a victory point value.
rules: The first player to encounter a given ship sinks it and takes the corresponding card. Subsequent players landing on that space get nothing. Other spaces have events that help or hinder players along the way. A player completing a circuit sets out again on either track. In the basic game, winner is the player with the most victory points; for advanced play, highest total tonnage sunk wins.

Title: Flieger und Flak
description: This is an airplane race with opposing players' anti-aircraft guns as obstacles. The board has a white winding around the outside of four coloured circles. The track has alternating red and black plane outlines showing direction of movement, and one light-coloured plane per circle. Each circle is filled with a web of white dots and lines, and four points from which each player's planes leave. Each player gets 4 planes and 3 guns, which can be placed anywhere in the home circle.
rules: Players roll dice to move. On a 4, the player must move a plane from his base to the colored dot on the track, and then rolls again to move that plane. A plane landing on a red outline of an opposing circle must enter the circle and try to eliminate a gun by landing on it. The defending player in turn may shoot down a plane next to it by rolling a 1. Planes leave the circle via the light-coloured outline. First player to get all surviving planes back to base wins.

Title: Vstrechnei Boi (Встречныи Бои)
description: This game will help you understand the features of a meeting engagement, and most importantly to develop qualities such as attention and intelligence", say the rules. Playing pieces are green and blue plastic soldiers, tanks and missile launchers. The board has a grid 8 squares wide by 12 deep over an aerial view of terrain including towns, roads, contour lines, woods, and a river, which have no  bearing on play. The middle two rows are shaded, representing no man's land.
rules: Each player gets 5 soldiers, 5 tanks and 2 missile launchers. In turn, each player may move one piece one square orthogonally. To capture, a piece must move adjacent to an enemy from a legal direction. Equal pieces can capture from any side; soldiers can capture tanks only from the side or back; tanks can capture soldiers only to tank's front. The missile launcher hits from anywhere on the board, once, and then turns into a tank. Winner is the first to reach the opponent's back row.

Title: L'Entente Cordiale
description: This 55-card deck celebrates the breakthrough 1904 agreements between Great Britain and France. Precise terms were mostly settling colonial boundaries, but the Entente set the stage for their WWI alliance. The cards are in six suits based on locations around a house, with 9 numbered items in each location. Each card shows the item and location in both English and French. Players must announce (in both languages) the name of each item as they place it.
rules: Whoever has the House/Maison card plays it first, followed by the 1 of any location (suit). Whoever has the 2 follows suit, and so on until all 9 have been played. The last player in a suit then starts a new one. The first player to run out of cards wins the game.

Title: Deutsch-Englischer Seekrieg
description: This game represents a clash between the English and German main fleets. Each player has a fleet of 22 wooden ships. This is made up of a single flagship, three cruisers, six destroyers, 10 torpedo boats and two U-boats.
rules: Starting positions are in opposing corners, as in Halma. However, movement and range for attacks vary with the piece. The player who eliminates the opposing fleet wins.

Title: Submarine Chaser
description: This game pits one player commanding the submarine against up to four players trying to sink it. The board is a set of linked concentric circles. The chasers start at bases around the outer circle. The submarine starts in the middle. Some arc and spoke segments show torpedoes.
rules: Only the submarine and a single chaser move in a given turn, and they move by only a single space. The chasers must force the submarine into the anti-submarine nets located at the top and bottom of the board. The submarine can eliminate a chaser by moving behind a torpedo that is pointing at a chaser immediately in front of it. The submarine wins by eliminating three chasers or killing two of them and then surviving for another 25 moves.

Title: Foxbat & Phantom
description: This game of tactical air combat in the 1970s takes place on a blank hex grid that acts as airspace. Each player starts with three planes and can choose from among 14 aircraft types. Different types come with different missions to be accomplished.
rules: Playing pieces are individual planes, with distinct movement characteristics and combat factors.

Title: European War Game Series (French Army)
description: This is one of a series of target-shooting games published during WWI. This game features targets representing the French army, on foot and mounted. It has a wooden spring-loaded cannon that fires wooden bullets.
rules: Target shooting game: player knocking down the most targets within an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Bomber, The
description: This is a particularly challenging dexterity puzzle. A cardboard bomber is pinned loosely to the bottom of the box. It has six slots in the rear of its wings. The player must try to get small wooden cylinders simultaneously into all six of the slots.
rules: Player to succeed in the least time wins.

Title: War Tactics, or Can Great Britain be Invaded?
description: This game was triggered by the pre-WWI German naval build-up, and is a call to action. “With this new game, you can immediately be placed in the theatre of war and become an Admiral or General, give your own orders as to attack and defence, and perhaps by this simple game you may be brought to realize the horrors which have to be faced, and the promptitude with which a decision has to be made.”
rules: The movement rules mix checkers and chess. Dreadnoughts, cruisers and torpedo boats at sea, and infantry on land, move a single space – forward, left or right, but never diagonally or backward. They destroy an enemy piece by jumping over it to an empty space. Submarines and “flying squadrons” move exactly four spaces at a time. They may not double back, but may change direction with each space into which they move. They kill pieces by ending their move on the enemy space, as in chess.

Title: Japanese Slide 'Em Puzzle
description: This colorful version of a slide 'em puzzle features the flags of the WWII combatants, 4 Japanese "meatball" tiles and the Japanese leader. The rules show starting positions and two target layouts.
rules: To be translated.

Title: U-boot-Spiel, Das
description: Asymmetric game where the German player tries to get his submarine into the British naval base at Scapa Flow

Title: Frontline Jeep Patrol
description: Players race plastic jeeps to deliver a critical message to a frontline unit and then return to their command post. The game has only 16 illustrated spaces and a 1 to 5 spinner built into the board. Situations include being hit with pre-registered artillery fire at a crossroads, skirting a minefield, getting caught in anti-tank crossfire, being strafed by low-level planes and getting protection from friendly artillery. Some images show combat in the Pacific.
rules: First to the end of the 16-space track wins.

Title: Batailles sur Mer
description: This may be the first wargame to use a “double-blind” system, in which each player has a separate playing board and they face away from one another. The map is of the Indian Ocean, but the opposing countries are not identified. Combined with the fact that the label on the box was placed over top of an image of a German bomber, this suggests wartime design and post-war publication.
rules: Players take turns moving secretly, and only give away their potential location by announcing an attack against a specific square. The number of shots per ship and their range and direction varies by the type of ship. However, any announced attack gives some indication of location. The game includes both red metal shell bursts to mark hits and dummy wooden ship counters to plot possible positions. A player wins be eliminating the opposing fleet.

Title: Flieger greifen an!
description: The diamond-shaped game board has a dense web of movement points laid over a generic map. Corners have images of airbase activities. Many spaces have flak or barrage balloons. There are two hexagonal target areas on each board: the small start and larger railway station. Players start with two each of scouts, fighters and bombers.
rules: Players roll each turn to move a single plane. Scouts must try to fly over the enemy base and return to base. Bombers must try to reach the heavily defended station and return. Fighters try to shoot down enemy planes by landing on them. Players get a point for each plane completing a mission and each enemy plane shot down. Most points wins.

Title: Be an Airman
description: A recruitment pitch for the Royal Canadian Air Force, the game was designed by Flt. Lt. O. Cathcart-Jones, and the box featured a portrait of Canadian WWI ace Billy Bishop, who by 1942 had become Air Vice Marshal William A. Bishop, VC, DSO, MC, DFC, head of the British Commonwealth Air Training Plan. It was approved by the Air Council and stated that all profits would go to the RCAF Benevolent Fund. The board's artwork features vivid images both of air training and air combat.
rules: After basic training, players at space 15 must choose one of three roles: pilot, gunner or observer. The full track has175 spaces, but the observer and gunner tracks are 18 spaces shorter, sending a message that while pilots get the most prestige, other roles will allow recruits to get into the scrap sooner. Otherwise, all have an equal shot at bombing Berlin, shooting down German planes and returning from the war as heroes.

Title: Weltkriegsspiel
description: This checkers-type board is 15 squares wide and 16 deep. All pieces move on the black squares. The four ranks at the bottom have Central Power markings, while Allied symbols adorn the upper ranks, with a 3-rank No Man's Land in between. The corners have images of Russian, British, French, Belgian and German troops in action. The inscriptions on the board reads: "We want to win! We will win! We must win!
rules: Move and jump as in checkers. The German player may get advantages given the more than 2:1 advantage in counters for the Allies.

Title: Through the Ranks?
publisher: Verlag und Eigenthum von Heinrich Kluge in Pillnitz a/Elbe
description: This unnamed and undated 19th Century German game board is divided into four triangular segments. Each player advances along the career track within that segment. The winner, of course, is the first player to reach the rank of General at the apex of his pyramid. The style and color of the uniforms show that the game was published after Germany's 1842 introduction of the Waffenrock (the first modern military uniform).
rules: First to reach the rank of general wins. Each of four players has a unique pyramid on which to play.

Title: Ato-Blitz War Game
description: This variant on the old L’Attaque has a very plain board, with yellow set-up areas and a green “No Man’s Land/Sea” in between. The cover is vivid, featuring a B-29 Superfortress, which carried the atom bomb to Hiroshima and Nagasaki. The wooden stand-up playing pieces have an illustrated angled side toward the owning player. They include infantry pieces with values from 1-14 and forces and weapons with special powers.
rules: Players set up secretly and move pieces one at a time. When one infantry attacks another, the values are revealed and the lower-value piece is removed from the game. Bombs don’t move, but kill any unit that attacks them except engineers. Tanks and airplanes kill any infantry unit including engineers, but lose to guns. Planes also kill tanks. The A-bomb acts like a moveable mine that destroys every piece in an adjacent square when it explodes – enemy and friendly alike.

Title: Trench War (French/German)
description: This unnamed game board has takes an unusual approach to the roll and move game. Two tracks run in parallel. One tracks the life of a German soldier; the other a French one. Both tracks run from the joyous start of war through its many hardships and challenges to a peaceful end. The captions for each image are in German, but the images are equally sympathetic to both sides. Interestingly, among the fierce combat pictures, soldiers of both sides are seen attending religious services.
rules: First player to reach the end of the chosen 60-space track wins.

Title: Black-Out
description: This game highlight the role of other air raid services in protecting civilians during the Blitz as players drive their cars across the center of London at night. The playing pieces are cardboard cars on wooden blocks. Movement is provided by a deck of cards showing the people and equipment needed to keep order during an air raid.
rules: Each player gets a mounted cardboard car that must make it from one corner of London to the opposite corner. Players draw a card each turn, and use them as needed to proceed across one or more of the white arrows that cross London's streets. Players continually must evaluate the best route as they draw new cards. First to cross London wins.

Title: Blitz: The Air-Raid Game
description: This is a war-themed variant of Reversi or Othello. The board has 60 squares representing properties including railway stations, shops, factories, armouries, an airport, a hospital and anti-aircraft defences. Each space has a different point value. The playing pieces are 60 wooden bombs, red on one end and black on the other.
rules: Players start with two bombs each, diagonally opposed in in the four middle squares. The red attacking player places a red bomb on any space where it will bracket a black bomb. The bracketed bomb is flipped over to red. The black player then does the same. Play continues until all 60 squares are filled. Players then add up the point values under their bombs, with highest total winning.

Title: Eagle Bombsight
description: This ingenious bomb-dropper was intended for use with the publisher's Bombs Away! dartboard. Players hold the device horizontally, looking forward at an angled mirror providing a view of the board below. They then use a toggle to drop each of four bombs. The bombs are wooden, with a metal dart point.
rules: As for Bombs Away!, the bombs score a point for each military target hit, lose a point for hitting civilians and get nothing for missing. Player with the highest total points after an agreed number of rounds wins.

Title: Put the Yanks in Berlin
description: This small box has a map of Europe on the bottom, stretching from England to Russia and from Scandinavia to Italy. There are four indentations surrounding Berlin, as well as white ones in Russia and Italy both representing the American air force (!), a blue one in England for the RAF and black ones in Norway and France. There are 7 small marbles, 4 yellow, 2 white and 1 blue.
rules: Players must try to get all four yellow into the spots around Berlin, the blue into London and the white into Moscow and Rome. Fastest to complete wins.

Title: Sabotage
publisher: Christian Gabriel Bach, Jørgen Milners, Viggo Torstensson (self-published)
description: Sabotage was designed and clandestinely distributed by a group of Danish resistance fighters during the German occupation of Denmark in WWII. It simulates an actual act of sabotage that took place against a transformer station at the Scandia Locomotive Factory in Randers. Erik Hagen, a doctor active in the resistance, is said to have sold the game to patients and used the money to support the cause. He was shot and killed on 6 June, 1944, while returning from a sabotage mission against the Globus Factory in Glostrup. The game board and pieces are printed on a single sheet of paper which players are instructed to mount on cardboard and cut out.
rules: The game is presented as a training simulation, and there are no fixed rules for winning or losing. It pits 1 or 2 saboteurs against 1 or 2 guards. Movement and gunfights are decided by throws of a single die. Saboteurs try to blow up as many machines and buildings as possible before being caught, killed or escaping. They first must find needed equipment like crowbars, guns and explosives on the black market. If guards discover the saboteurs and raise the alarm, the saboteurs have only a few turns to complete their mission and escape.

Title: War Puzzle
description: This is a puzzle rather than a competitive game, but shows the United States' desire for peace as Europe went to war. The cover graphic is a haunting omen of the agony and slaughter to come.
rules: The puzzle consists of a set of flat metal shapes. The pieces first must be fit together to form the word WAR. Then the player must re-arrange them to spell the word PEACE (with individual letters being smaller).

Title: Air Defense (Kroll)
description: This is a paper air-power themed version of Battleships on a standard 10 x 10 grid.
rules: Standard Battleships

Title: Tactics
description: This game shows the choices facing both belligerent and neutral nations as war breaks out. Of the four players, two are the North and South belligerent powers (at war with each other), while the East and West powers start as neutrals but may be pushed or choose to enter the war on one side or the other. Playing pieces are metal war ships and planes plus merchant ship disks. Cards grant submarines and air raids at designated locations. Board is divided into four seas each with numbered squares.
rules: Players move a piece each turn. The two powers at war score points by sinking enemy ships or trading with neutrals. The neutrals score points by trading with each other or with belligerent powers. Belligerents may attack neutrals, but doing so may cause the neutral to join the enemy power. The winner of the war gets bonus gold minus ships lost, and the player with the most gold wins.

Title: Weltkrieg, Der
description: This checkers variant was published early in WWI. The Allied flags on the cover include Japan and Russia as well as France, Belgium and the United Kingdom, but not Italy, which joined the Allies in April 1915. The German side shows only German and Austro-Hungarian flags. The board is a 15 x 21 square grid laid over a map of Europe. The playing pieces are wooden disks on larger cardboard circles. Each piece has the name of a city on it.
rules: Players take turns placing their pieces on the enemy side of the board with city information down. They then move pieces one at a time in any direction, and move faster by jumping over friendly or enemy pieces. When first moving a piece, its information goes face up, and stays up until it reaches its destination. The first player to get all pieces back to their home cities wins.

Title: Nicaragua!
description: This game models the campaign of the Sandinistas to take down Somoza and the subsequent unsuccessful attempts by the Contras to recapture power in Nicaragua. Players must use military forces to battle their opponents, but also carry out intelligence operations to uncover hidden forces and use psychological warfare and propaganda to demoralize enemy forces and build popular support. They must court support from both social groups at home and governments abroad.
rules: Game turns are quarterly, and the map is divided into zones called Departments. Any military conflict is resolved within individual Departments. Political conflict also gets resolved by Departments, each of which have icons representing the interest groups with significant representation in that region.

Title: Adler Luftkampfspiel
description:
The cover of the box features a German Me 109 downing a British Spitfire while escorting a pair of Heinkel 111 medium bombers. The cover of the rules also shows another Spitfire falling in flames, this one downed by an Me-109 accompanied by two Me-110s. The game introduces tactical air combat concepts including bombing, fighter escort, dogfighting and ground fire. It has plastic red and blue planes, cardboard flak guns and ships and wooden bombs.

​
rules: Each turn, players roll 5D6 and move a plane. Planes are numbered and must move in order. All bombers go before fighters. Landing on an enemy plane, flak or ship causes combat. For air-air, players roll all dice; margin of 3+ shoots down enemy. For air-ground, only attacker rolls; 18+ destroys target; plane shot down on <14. Points awarded for planes safely returned, enemy shot down, flak and ships destroyed and bombs dropped on enemy base. Most points wins.

Title: Schlacht bei Leipzig
description: This may be the earliest game ever published dealing with an actual historical battle. Up to seven players race to reach the city of Leipzig. Each player commands one of the attacking armies, with a player card having an illustration of the general in charge and a short biography. While not primarily educational, Die Schlacht bei Leipzig was the first game intended to help players understand and take lessons from the outcome of an historical battle.
rules: Each player advances a painted metal playing piece along a different track toward the city of Leipzig, and somewhere along the way faces an obstacle requiring a high roll that represents one of the encounters with French forces in the course of the campaign (the red diamonds topped with red dots and the name of a French commander).

Title: Nail Hitler
description: Help win the war by playing cards. Adolf Hitler adorns the central pot. Surrounding spaces take antes and are won by the play of specific cards or sequences. Chamberlain's Umbrella is a symbol of the former British PM known for appeasing Hitler in 1938. Coins there are claimed by playing 3 cards in sequence. No Man's Land coins are won by four in a row.
rules: Players put coins in the Hitler pot and surrounding spaces. Cards are dealt out among the players plus a “dummy” hand. The dealer may take the dummy or auction it to the highest bidder. The starting player plays the lowest card he has of a suit. Those with the next cards in sequence follow. The player who ends a suit starts a new one. The first to get rid of all cards in hand wins the Hitler pot. Losers pay into No Man's Land amount equal to number of cards left.

Title: Jutland
description: This is likely the earliest version of the game that became popular as Battleships. It is named after the one major British-German naval battle of WWI. It is a paper game that comes with 20 playing sheets.
rules: Players secretly place their own ships within a 10 x 10 grid. They then take turns calling out individual squares. If an enemy ship occupies that square, the defender calls "Hit" and the attacker plays again. First to completely eliminate the opposing navy wins.

Title: Siege of Tsingtao
description: This may be the earliest game in the world about a particular WWI battle of WWI, and shows a military first: an aerial attack on ships in harbor by sea-launched plane. The board shows the Farman seaplanes used in the ground-breaking naval raid on Sept. 5, 1914. Tsingtao was a German enclave in China, and Japan was a British ally in WWI. The graphics are by the artist Kawabata Ryushi, who was considered one of the “Three Big Figures” in the Nihonga style.
rules: This game is an example of furiwake-sugoroku. Players roll a die at the start to determine which path toward victory they will take. On an even roll, players follow the left path, which focuses on the naval actions. If the first roll is odd, players go right and follow the early land manoeuvers. The two paths join for the final assault.

Title: Stahl und Eisen
description: This game offers a stunning visual overview of Germany's military-industrial complex during WWI. The game track covers everything from mining to smelting, and then rolling into steel plates and fashioning finished products including artillery and warships. Game design is credited to J.B. Meier.
rules: First to the end of the 88-space track wins.

Title: Bomber Attack
description: This colorful game of aerial combat and bombing was issued mid-war. Notable among the planes illustrated on the board is a twin-boom P-38 Lightning, which only came into service that year. Also shown are civilians on the ground affected by bombing.
rules: First to the end of the 90-space track wins.

Title: Miltry
description: The board is circular, with interlocking paths leading toward a central fortress. Each player represents one of the five nations at war in the west: Germany, Britain, France, Italy and Russia. (Although this is an American game, the US was still neutral at time of publication.) The playing pieces are paper flags mounted with metal pins on wooden cylindrical bases.
rules: The game is for up to five players. Each player's four flags start on the outer ring of the circular board. The aim is to capture the fortress in the middle, but the movement and capture rules are not clear.

Title: Ferry Command
description: The game extols the virtues of the B-17 Flying Fortress as a transport plane rather than a bomber, carrying vital supplies across both the Atlantic and Pacific. Destinations are Murmansk, London, Gibraltar, Sierra Leone, Dutch Harbor, Pearl Harbor and Sydney. Cargoes are AA guns, Garand rifles, ammunition and medical supplies, but oddly also other planes (fighters, pursuit and medium bombers).
rules: Players draw a cargo and destination. They roll one die on the way out, and two dice on the return trip (because planes can fly faster when empty). Landing on a blue dash means “losing the beam”. Players are stuck there until they roll a 3. However, they may roll two dice in either direction, and can use a 3 on either die, or from the sum of the two dice (i.e. a 1 and a 2). The game ends as soon as the any player completes five round trips. Highest value of delivered cargo wins.

Title: Marschall Vorwarts
description: Its pieces are Napoleonic miniatures, but this game was published in September 1914 “to raise a memory of the wars heroically led by Germany and Austria a hundred years ago and today”. The board is 5 columns wide, with three ranks on each side. It is the first game to capture friction of war.  Pieces double and triple in value as they close with the enemy, but are correspondingly harder to move. Miniatures include infantry, cavalry and artillery. Leaders are Napoleon and Blucher.
rules: The rulebook is 32 pages long. Pieces vary in base value from 1 to 10. Players roll 3D6 per turn for total movement allowance (piece value x rank moved to). Players attack by column, adding piece values modified by rank. High total wins, inflicting casualties equal to difference. Elimination counts for rank-modified value; retreats base value only. One-column skirmishes and 3-column battles lead to "decisive battle" that totals the value of all pieces remaining on the board.

Title: Jeu de l'Histoire de France
description: Published in France while under German occupation, this game about French history ends with the German conquest in 1940. The final space shows Marshal Pétain, who became head of the puppet Vichy French government.
