rules: Each of the player cards has 10 rectangular spaces illustrating an event in French history and showing the date. Small cards with dates are pulled randomly and handed to the player with the right card. First player to fill a card wins.

Title: Dein KdF-Wagen
description: The board shows the entire process of developing Hitler's "people's car", from design through sales, assembly and delivery to day-to-day use, good driving and maintenance. The title comes from Kraft durch Freude (Strength through Joy). which the Nazis set up to bring middle-class activities like concerts, libraries and tourism to the masses. The KdF-wagen was priced at 990 DM, about the same as a small motorcycle.
rules: First player to save up, buy the car, learn to drive and get it home to the garage wins.

Title: Military Aviation
description: Images include men and machines of multiple nationalities: Zeppelins; a French tethered observation balloon; air to air combat; rudimentary bombing (dropping small bombs by hand); and reconnaissance by fixed-wing and lighter-than-air craft in support of both naval gunfire and German uhlans. Other images include an anti-aircraft gun and a searchlight as well as a portrait of Graf von Zeppelin.
rules: Standard sugoroku inward spiral

Title: Naval Engagement
description: The game board shows a naval fleet bombarding a shore battery. The most notable feature of the illustration is the inclusion of a Union Monitor-class ironclad.
rules: The attacking player has 10 ships and the defender 10 shore guns, each bearing a number from 3 to 12. Players roll two six-sided dice, and mark off the opposing ship or gun with that number. Duplicate rolls are wasted, and the first player to knock out all the opposition units wins. The game was re-released by McLoughlin Bros. in 1898 under the title Game of Bombardment.

Title: The War Game as Played by Volunteer Officers at Horse Guards)
description: This 2-page spread shows the unfolding of a training game for volunteer officers. The game is played across a large topographical map on a tabletop. Each team receives its orders and makes plans in secret. Orders are transmitted to an umpire, who then carries them out for both sides. The illustrations show everything from decisions on the scenario and battlefield through planning and play.
rules: Units are wooden blocks of various sizes and shapes, representing infantry, cavalry, artillery, engineers, leaders and even an ambulance. Each unit has a defined movement speed, firepower and range. Fire attacks are resolved with die rolls.

Title: Bizerte Gertie
description: Players are Buck Privates with a one-night pass in the northernmost port in Tunisia. Their goal is “to meet and date exotic maids waiting in the Park and stroll with them on the moonlit beach” - and avoid getting stuck with the ugliest date. The first three privates to pick up dates get Bizerte Gertie, Sally from Bali or New Guinea Minnie. The laggard gets stuck with Alice the Hound Dog.
rules: The player with Alice can swap her with another player’s date by landing on that player’s space. Players must make it through the “Yoo-Hoo” zone (where their dates may attract the attention of Officers), successfully enter the Play-a-Way Club (“no dogs allowed”) and then make it to the beach. The first to arrive there collects the kitty (and kisses).

Title: Propeller frei!
description: This is an early game of aerial dogfighting. Although issued shortly before WWII, the box still shows biplane fighters. The board is a 13 x 13 grid of movement spaces, joined by horizontal and vertical lines as well as arcs that form a circle within each 3 x 3 block.
rules: To be added

Title: Bombardo
description: This game was published just after WWI by the Lord Roberts Memorial Workshops. These were named after Field Marshal Frederick Roberts, 1st Earl Roberts, who won the Victoria Cross in India in 1857 and went on to become Commander-in-Chief of the Forces until 1904. Beloved by his men and a passionate advocate for disabled veterans, "Bobs" was killed in 1914 while visiting troops. The workshops provided disabled veterans with work and training, and made a wide variety of goods.
rules: This is a dexterity game where players spin a top within a circle of lightweight balls perched in a circle. Each player has a color, and the goal is to end each spin with the most balls of your color still on their stands.

Title: Grand National Victory
description: This game says "it presents to the mind of every Veteran as to the author of this game, a retrospective panorama of his struggles to save the Union…” It was sold door to door by unemployed veterans. “This is left for you to play tonight. The soldier or his agent will call in the morning for 10 cents, as it is earnestly hoped that you will patronize." The pitch was signed "A Boy in Blue.
rules: Players roll dice to move along the 100-space track from Washington to Richmond. Each space is marked with a battle, the name of a prominent officer or the name of a unit. They appear in chronological order. Union victories and good generals move the player forward; defeats and bad officers do the reverse. A player landing on a prison space stays there until another player reaches an exchange space, but it is possible to die in Libby Prison on space 99.

Title: Stuka: Das Packende Kampffliegerspiel
description: The board is fairly bland, with an illustration of a diving Stuka lightly etched behind an 88-space movement track.
rules: First to reach the end of the track wins.

Title: Boys' Warship
description: This 1917 game encouraged younger boys to think about the glory of joining the naval forces. The images capture a wide variety of naval activities, from building ships to sinking ships. There also is a seaplane taking off and examples of communications at sea, both through wireless and through signal flags.
rules: Standard sugoroku - first to reach the middle space wins.

Title: Jeu du Maquis
description: This game about the French Resistance was published shortly after the Germans fled Thonon in eastern France. It shows resistance activities including roadblocks, prisoner rescues, stealing arms and receiving paradrops, as well as grim milestones of German repression such as the massacre of the village of Oradour-sur-Glane by SS troops in June 1944.
rules: Players roll 2D6 for each move, and must move one of their four pawns by the full amount. When any of a player's pawns are on a white "refuge" space, that player can put the total into a "tactical reserve", to a maximum of 10 points. Any roll that would push the reserve above 10 cannot be added at all. Reserve points can be added in whole or in part to a future die roll (helping to avoid penalty spaces). First to reach the August 1944 Liberation space wins.

Title: Napoleon
description: This successor to Quebec 1759 and War of 1812 brought blocks to Napoleon's final campaign. It also introduced the impact of combined arms and added a tactical battle map. Any time opposing forces at a location have at least 3 pieces each, combat is resolved on this map.
rules: Rules for movement and attack are standard. The tactical battle map is used for larger units. Each player available units across 3 columns. Combat proceeds by column as usual, with an enemy pip eliminated for each 6 rolled. However, it the enemy is eliminated in any column, friendly units in subsequent rounds can flank an adjacent column and roll at double strength.

Title: Flight Formation
description: This small dexterity game comes in a metal box with a glass top and cardboard insert showing hangers at an airfield and the sky above. There are three plane-shaped indentations in the sky, and three corresponding cardboard planes. Players must try to get all three planes in their proper places (in order, a patriotic red, white and blue).
rules: Fastest to succeed wins.

Title: Down the Hatch!
description: This 3D game board has three parts: a target board inset in the box with various landing spots for marbles marked by color; a raised cardboard pyramid set in the middle; and a tower that covers the pyramid.
rules: Players drop marbles down the tower and aim to have marbles of their color stop in holes of matching color. The player with the most successful landings wins (single round or an agreed number of rounds). Cumulative score is marked with wooden pegs on the sides of the tower.

Title: Game of Battles
description: This small game box shows an American crew firing one of the French quick-firing 75 mm guns that the United States bought in 1917 to equip the forces it sent to the Western Front. It also shows a wireless operator working in the nearby dugout.
rules: N/A

Title: Seekrieg, Der
description: This WWI game shows a wide variety of naval activities culminating in a grand surface melee in the middle of the board. It is notable for its depiction of the extensive use of air units to support naval warfare. This includes an image of one of Germany's earliest seaplanes in space 10, a Friedrichshafen FF33.
rules: Typical roll and move. Players move metal ships along a 90-space trap, with bonus and penalty spaces along the way. First to the end wins.

Title: Ranger Commandos
description: Based on the Marine Raider assault on Makin Island, this game requires players to navigate rough seas to the island, cause havoc there, and then retreat. The playing pieces are wooden boats with a cardboard windshield, and holding a wooden peg that jumps out to move on land. Movement is by spinner, with different results depending on the phase of the game. The island is marked with paths leading to objectives such as the airfield, HQ and oil storage.
rules: The nighttime half of the spinner is used to move in; the inner ring for land movement; and the daytime half for retrieval. Once on land, players retrieve VP tokens from the objectives without landing on a sentry space. As soon as any player activates a sentry, all players must return to their boats within three spins or are out of the game. First player to return to base gets a VP bonus, and the player with the highest VP total wins.

Title: Air Raid Defense
description: This is a relatively small metal target game that uses a toy gun firing darts with rubber tips. Four targets, worth 50 - 200 points, are printed on the board near the corners. A central target is an airplane that flops down when hit.
rules: Player scoring the most points in an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Skat (Yenidze WWI)
publisher: Orientalische Tabak- und Zigarettenfabrik Yenidze (Yenidze Oriental Tobacco and Cigarette Factory)
description: This is a 32-card Skat card deck with illustrations of German leaders during WWI. It was published as an advertising vehicle for a German tobacco company.
rules: Standard

Title: Aviation (1st Edition)
description: This is Gibson's aerial version of L'Attaque. The board has no terrain. The 84 cardboard playing pieces have images on the side facing the owner and blank backs. They are mounted on metal clips as usual, with the pieces ranging from the Scout (1) to Air Marshall's Squadron (10). The Troop Carriers have a value of 4.5 and a critical role. AA Guns have ranges of 3-5 spaces. A piece is eliminated if attacked by a Searchlight within range of the gun. AA is killed if attacked by a Bomber (2).
rules: Players set up freely on their own side of the board, then in turn move one piece at a time, a single space orthogonally. When moved back-to-back with an enemy, an attack is declared, with the higher value piece eliminating the lower value one (both in ties). Troop Carriers can move diagonally, eliminate lower-value pieces, and are driven back to base by higher ones. A player wins by landing a Troop Carrier on the opponents Aerodrome.

Title: Campaign (1940)
description: The board is 25 squares wide by 34 deep. It is abstract, but with a clear resemblance to the French-German front . The forts reflect the Maginot and Siegfried lines along the Rhine. The river runs from neutral mountains in the south (Switzerland) to neutral flowering fields in the north (Netherlands). Tiptoeing through the tulips is not allowed in the game, WWII saw Germany invade the Netherlands rather than leave it neutral is in WWI.
rules: Each side gets four tanks, four artillery and six infantry pieces. The objective is to get one of each type across the board to the opposing base. Movement is complicated not only by enemy pieces, but by a river running down the middle and fortifications on both sides of the river.

Title: Bataan
description: The box cover has a vivid image of American troops valiantly defending against the Japanese invasion of the Philippines at the outset of the war. The game inside is simply a version of Hold the Fort, with a cross-shaped board of 65 dots connected by lines. There are 50 attackers who can only move against three defending pieces who eliminate attackers by jumping over them in any direction.
rules: Standard Grosse Belagerungsspiele. Attackers win by occupying all the spaces in the "castle" arm of the board. The defender wins by eliminating enough attackers to make this impossible.

Title: Chūshingura
description: This e-sugoroku (picture game) tells the classic tale of the 47 Ronin. These samurai became ronin (outcasts) after their lord Asano Naganori was insulted by a powerful official, Kira Yoshinaka. Asano lost his temper and slashed at Kira with his dagger. For drawing his dagger within the shogun's court, Asano was sentenced to commit seppuku, ritual suicide. His retainers plotted revenge, killed Kira and then turned themselves in, voluntarily committing seppuku themselves to atone for their crime.
rules: Mawari sugoroku is the most common version of this story-telling gambling game. Players roll dice to move along a clockwise spiral track from lower right to the centre. The first to reach the centre wins. It is similar to the many European variations of Game of Goose, where players advance counter-clockwise along a 63-space track starting in the lower left.

Title: Aero Navale
description: This is possibly the first boxed Battleships variant. The box is hinged. When opened, it raises a screen between the two players. Both in the lid and in the box, players have grids both for laying out their own forces and plotting their fire.
rules: This variant of Battleships adds divebombers and minefields to the attack mix.

Title: Viet Nam
description: This prescient game on Viet Nam was designed by Phil Orbanes while still in high school. It captures the essence of that war: ground combat, psychological warfare and world opinion.
rules: TBA

Title: Imperial Army Massive Victory
description: This game shows a wide range of events in 1937-38, as Japan fought in China but tried to keep the United States on the sidelines. In addition to battle scenes, it notes the establishment of puppet states in China, the accession of Italy to the Anti-Comintern Alliance, and Helen Keller's visit to Japan in 1937.
rules: Standard mawari-sugoroku: first player to reach the middle space wins.

Title: Neunzehnte Jahrhundert
description: This lotto game has 10 player cards illustrating all 100 years of the 19th Century. Each card has images of a notable person or event during one year of that decade. There is a deck of 100 small plain cards, each showing a year and the matching person or event. The accompanying 32-page booklet provides a year by year history covering both the illustrations and other events of that year. Also included are colorful portrait tiles that can be placed as appropriate instead of the year's small card.
rules: As in Bingo, each player pays into the pot for each card taken. The small Year cards are shuffled into a pile and turned over one by one. The player with the right decade board takes the year card and places it over the appropriate space. First player to fill a board wins the pot.

Title: Battle of the Coral Seas
description: While its title plays off the then-recent WWII naval Battle of the Coral Sea, it is actually an abstract game of naval combat played on an 8 x 10 grid. Unusually, ships sit on intersecting lines of the grid rather than in the spaces between them. The playing pieces are cardboard, and each player starts with 10 ships: one battleship, two heavy cruisers, four light cruisers and three destroyers.
rules: The aim is to manoeuver ships to bring their broadsides to bear on an enemy on an adjacent position. A larger ship can kill a smaller ship the lies directly ahead. In turn, each player may move one ship, either one intersection forward or a turn to the left or right.  The destroyers have the option of any two moves (two ahead, a turn and move, or turning directly astern). The battleship can attack at a range of two. Each side also has a land battery that kills any ship ending a move on its line of fire.

Title: Blockade
description: Two players each on an island compete to keep their economies supplied with four vital commodities: Food, Arms, Oil and Metals. The source for each commodity is a port on the board edge. Each player starts with 3 warships, 2 submarines, 2 planes and 3 cargo ships, as well as a stockpile of 5 of each. There are two spinners, one for movement and consumption, the other for battle results. There also is a 12-turn circular marker for each player.
rules: Movement is by spinner. The number spun can move a single unit or be split between two or more. Many results also cause the loss of a commodity. Cargo ships must reach a source port to load a commodity and get home to add it to the stockpile. Battles are triggered by moving adjacent to an enemy. Results are determine by a table cross-referencing the combatants with the number spun. As soon as any player runs out of a commodity, he has 12 turns to replenish or loses the game.

Title: Grunt
description: This game simulates the frustrating task facing American forces as they patrolled for elusive Viet Cong and North Vietnamese Army troops. The cover features the Huey Hu-1B helicopters that became the symbol of airmobile warfare in Viet Nam. The map is in black and white, and the counters black on yellow for the US and yellow on black for the Communist forces.
rules: The rules covered aspects of tactical warfare including airstrikes, helicopter medical evacuation, “body count” victory points, ambushes and booby traps. It includes a wide variety of scenarios in various types of terrain.

Title: Ration Board
description: Players roll dice to move around the board and collect the coupons they need in order to buy scarce items like coffee and sugar.
rules: There is a push-your-luck element to movement. Players announce a target roll. If they equal or exceed it, they move the number of spaces rolled. Otherwise they do not move at all that turn.

Title: Game of Defense (Atlantic)
description: A rough outline of the Americas, Europe and Africa surround a red-and-white checkerboard Atlantic Ocean. Each player gets 15 ships with red or white bases: 5 battleships, 5 cruisers and 5 submarines. Battleships move only on red squares in any direction, up to four per move. Cruisers move only on white, with a range of three. The submarines can move onto any square, but move only one space. There also are five objective cards and minefield results cards.
rules:
Players secretly choose one of five objective cards to start, and mark 10 spaces as mined. Players then move a single piece in turn. Battleships and cruisers capture other ships by jumping over them. Submarines capture ships by moving onto their squares. Any piece landing on a minefield must draw a card and suffer the result. A player wins the game by landing the required one or two ships on the chosen objective.

​

Title: Bombardement de la ligne Siegfried
description: As WWII dawned, the French expected a showdown with the Germans between their two fortified borders. The game ignores the possibility of a German attack through Belgium: the map, overlaid by a 10 x 25 grid, runs from the Swiss border only as far north as Cologne and Aix-la-Chappelle. Each player gets a playing board to mark shots and a smaller slate to mark his hidden defensive positions.
rules: Each player secretly places 4 machine guns, 3 anti-aircraft batteries, 4 artillery batteries, 4 aeroplane squadrons, 1 aviation park, 1 ammunition depot, 1 GHQ, and 5 mines. Machine guns, anti-aircraft, artillery, the aviation park and the GHQ are marked as 1-5 space lines, squadrons  as a triangle and the ammunition depot as a diamond. The mines are marked as an M in single spaces. Each player gets 30 volleys of 3 shots each. Player with the most hits wins.

Title: Verdun
description: This game illustrates the longest and bloodiest battle of WWI. Lasting 300 days, it led to 400,000 French and 350,000 German casualties, of which almost half died.
rules: The black and white mapboard shows the interlocking zones of fire amongst the many defending forts and batteries around Verdun. The attacking player must start from the southeast corner and try to overwhelm strongpoints one at a time.

Title: Salvo (Starex)
description: This is one of many 1930s paper variants of Jutland/Battleships, with some different rules.
rules: Players in turn fire a salvo of six shots. After the complete salvo, the defender must indicate the total number of hits and name of ship(s) hit, but not which squares did the damage. First player to sink all opposing ships wins the agreed pot. However, stakes are doubled if the winner has an unhit destroyer, tripled for an unhit cruiser, quadrupled for an unhit battleship and 11 times if the entire fleet is undamaged.

Title: Beachhead Invasion Game
description: Nine tracks from 4 to 15 spaces in length lead to a beach. Wooden pegs are used to mark three numbered warships and the progress of the landing barge along each track. The spinner has numbers from 1 to 15 and a "Back 2" segment.
rules: Defender spins, and removes any barge or ship peg corresponding to the number. All remaining barges move one step forward. All barges move back 1 if a ship is hit, and 2 if the "Back 2" space is spun. Attacking player wins when 5 barges reach the beach. Defender wins by knocking out 5 barges.

Title: Blockade Runner, The New Game of
description: The board has a map covering the Yellow Sea and adjoining territory, and is focused on the Japanese siege of Russian-held Port Arthur. The sea area is covered with an irregular network of dots and points. The game pre-dates the end of the Russo-Japanese War, since the site of the decisive battle at Tsushima is just off the south-east edge of the map.
rules: The Russian player tries to move pieces from dot to dot along connecting lines, trying to relieve the besieged forces in Port Arthur. The Japanese player tries to block the Russian player.

Title: Blast-Off!
description: Published immediately after Neil Armstrong's moon landing, this game challenges players to understand the physics of orbital movement as they reach beyond the moon to the planets. It also introduces limited fuel, which is used to modify the die rolls in order reach a planet.
rules: Players first launch a satellite and then a capsule. After the two rendezvous to make a spaceship, they head for the moon and then on to each other planet in the solar system. The plastic moon piece orbits the earth, moving each turn. The planets are fixed but orbit the earth rather than the sun. Each planetary trip starts from the moon and must reach the right orbit and planet location by exact count. Limited fuel can modify rolls. First to do the tour and return wins.

Title: L'Attaque (Gibson Import)
description: This appears to be the first version of the game published by Gibson for the British market. The box is stamped "Imported Goods". The pieces have the standard images but no metal backing. The banks are an unusual combination of blue and purple.
rules: Standard L'Attaque

Title: Stars on Stripes Football
description: Wartime publishers wrapped many games in patriotic garb. This conventional football board game has a cover featured Betty Brown, described as a University of Southern California majorette. She is wearing a starred top and a striped skirt. The photo, oddly for a football game, was said to be courtesy of the Ice Capades, a skating spectacle founded in 1940.
rules: Football board rules. Details TBA.

Title: War at Sea, Game of
description: The box cover has a detailed image of an American warship, similar to the Maine (with its offset turrets) but showing three funnels rather than the Maine's two. The playing surface is a checkerboard of light and dark blue squares, bordered by North, Central and South America from Halifax to Brazil. There are 24 metal miniatures: 6 battleships and 6 torpedo boats per player. The southern end of the board is a flap that opens to show the storage area.
rules: Players alternate in placing a single ship at a time anywhere on the map. In turn, each player moves a single ship. Battleships can move 1 in any direction; torpedo boats 1 orthogonally only. Either can jump over any adjacent ship to an empty square, eliminating any enemy ship jumped. Ships are larger than the squares; they occupy the one containing the ship's bow. Multiple jumps are possible. A player wins by eliminating the enemy fleet.

Title: Guerre-Eclair, La
description: This variant on L'Attaque was published shortly after the Liberation of France in WWII. Although its title would translate as "Lightning War" or "Blitzkrieg", none of the playing pieces are tanks. However, they do include aircraft and various types of bombs as well as fuel trucks to keep the planes flying. There also is a "magnetic fence" that somehow shoots down aircraft.
rules: As in L'Attaque, initial set-up is open within each player's closest rows, and with blank sides facing the opponent. Players move one piece at a time. When an attack is declared against an adjacent piece, both are revealed and the lower value one removed. Some pieces have special powers. Winner is the first to capture either the opponent's flag piece or its "Secret Plan" piece.

Title: Wehrschach
description: This chess-like game was developed by the German Wehrmacht as a training aid but became widely popular. Each side gets 18 playing pieces made of bakelite that represent various unit types. The board has black and white squares on an 11 x 11 board. It also has features including a main road, a river, walls and 2 lakes (which block all movement except air).
rules: The pieces have varied movement powers like chess. Unlike chess, a piece is not captured by moving onto it. Instead, the attacker must threaten it with two other pieces. There are multiple ways to win, including capturing the Hauptfigur (Chess King equivalent), occupying the enemy's territory, or killing all his infantry (pawn equivalents).

Title: Civil War Chess
description: This US Civil War game appears to be a variant of chess. It has a standard checkered 8 x 8 board, with 3 images at each end. The playing pieces are flat disks with lithograph images pasted on top of various types of troops including infantry, cavalry, artillery, a bugle boy and a general. The images are mostly of Union troops, with two battle scenes showing Confederates as defenders and one image of Confederate cavalry.
rules: Rules are not available, but the different troop types on the disks suggest they could be chess analogs (e.g. the General having the same moves as the chess King, with Infantry probably pawns.)

Title: H.V. Kaltenborn Game of Diplomacy
publisher: Trend Game Co., Highland Park IL
description: The colorful Monopoly-style board has flags in the spaces around the edge. The goal is to create the “greatest world empire” through threats and diplomacy rather than outright invasion. Players represent one of the six great powers of the time, and work with one Diplomat piece, 10 consulates, 5 soldiers, 1 bomber and 1 battleship. Players roll dice to move their Diplomat around the board, spreading influence and collecting Country,  Secret Treaty and Diplomatic Mail cards.
rules: Landing on a colony allows a soldier to be placed, and for a second time replaces the soldier with a consulate. A player landing on a space with an opposing soldier removes it. Landing on Switzerland or Cuba allows players to move across the Bridge of Nations and pick up a bomber or battleship, needed to capture “strategic locations”. The game ends when one player places his 10th consulate. Winner is the player controlling the most strategic locations, colonies and independent countries.

Title: Bomber uber England
description: This bagatelle (pinball) game shows a map of the UK, the North Sea and the northern European coast. England and France are shown in orange; Belgium and Holland in white; and Germany in green. This shows that the game was published after the German invasion of Poland and declarations of war by England and France, but before the invasion of the Low Countries in April 1940.
rules:
The board has 19 potential holes as targets and a grid of small bumps to redirect the ball. Each of the target holes is marked with a score.
The highest possible score, 100 points, is assigned to London and to Calais. Hitting neutral Amsterdam or Brussels subtracts 80, and hitting Germany is -100! Each player is allowed to shoot the ball 10 times, and the player with the highest total score wins.

Title: Gunkoku Shonen Kokki
description: This Japanese game features the flags of the world’s military powers during WWI, neutrals as well as combatants. The Swedish and Norwegian flags predate those countries decisions to dissolve their political union in 1905. The artwork is by Murada.
rules: Standard sugoroku. First to reach the Japanese flag in the middle wins.

Title: Warfare
description: This game depicts a battle in the second Boer War, probably that of Paardeburg from 18-27 February 1900. The title image is very similar to a photograph of troops from the Royal Canadian Regiment advancing up a hill at that battle. In that battle, the army of Boer General Piet Cronjé was surprised by British cavalry while crossing the Modder River and decided to hold its ground as the British 6th Division moved up. The game board shows some of these features, including the river and trenches on the Boer side, but is otherwise abstract. Also shown is a town, possibly the nearby besieged Kimberly.
rules: Each side gets 12 infantry, 6 cavalry and 3 artillery pieces. The goal is to capture the enemy's camp on the other side of the river.

Title: Marine-Schach
description: The game board shows a sea area, with harbors at each end, and an island in each quarter. Across the board, is a network of dots connected by lines. Each dot is designated by row (letter) and column (number). Each player gets 1 flagship (A, combat value 4), 1 heavy cruiser (F, 4) , 1 light cruiser (C, 3),  3 torpedo boats (T, 2), 1 minesweeper (R, 1) 2 land batteries (B, 4) and 8 mines (m) which serve only to block movement.
rules: All ships start in their harbor. Each turn, a player moves one ship either one or two dots in any direction. Ships may carry B to an island or m to sea, to drop during movement. When ships move adjacent, the one with the weaker combat value must retreat. A ship that cannot retreat to a space not covered by higher firepower is eliminated. A player wins either by destroying the opposing flagship, or moving a ship to the enemy's harbor.

Title: Air Raid
description: This pre-war (for the USA at least) game is very similar to German air war game Adler Luftkampfspiel, published in 1935. The board consists of two player areas separated by no-man's-land. Players try to move their bombers across the board to the far side of the opposing board, where they can drop bombs on enemy buildings and ammo dumps. At the same time, they must use their fighters either to escort the bombers or defend their own home board. The game has pieces for four players who then work in teams.
rules: Planes start from their own first row, move into the owning player's preferred formations. Bombers only move 2 spaces straight ahead or 1 space and turn. Fighters move 3 ahead and one less if turning. Points are awarded both for bombing the enemy and shooting down planes. The player with the most points wins.

Title: Flying Aces
description: This early American dogfighting game shows 2-seater monoplane fighters with fixed landing gear. The board is composed of circular pathways that the playing pieces must follow in the indicated direction.
rules: Each player gets 3 planes. Each turn, players roll 2D6. The total roll is the number of spaces to be moved, which can be split as desired among the 3 planes. When leaving base, planes must follow their own colored paths until they end, after which they can move anywhere. Opposing planes are captured by moving behind them and then jumping. A high enough roll may permit multiple jumps. Last survivor wins.

Title: New U.S. Merchant Marine, The
description: The game board is an unusually detailed Mercator projection of the world at the time, Players compete to pick up and deliver cargoes. The game was re-issued in 1936 with an updated map under the title Liners and Transports.

Title: Tri-Tactics
description: This is the ultimate evolution of Gibson's variants on the standard L'Attaque. Much of the map is land, but there is a central ocean along with a lake and river on each player's side. Units represent land, sea and air forces that interact in a wide variety of ways depending on location as well as unit types and values.
rules: The game retains the standard L'Attaque mechanic: single move, challenge, compare and eliminate the weaker. However, there is a wide variety of units with special powers against particular unit types. Combat results may be different depending on whether an attack takes place on land, over water or along a coastline. Winner is still the first to capture the opponent's flag.

Title: Deutscher Schutz dich Selbst
description: This game captures the public fear and anger that brought the Nazis to power in 1933. The board shows hordes of black airplane symbols surrounding Germany. The map shows the territories stripped from Germany by the 1918 Treaty of Versailles in vivid stripes. The game’s central space uses the new national flag brought in by the Nazis to replace the black, yellow and red standard of the Weimar republic. This black, white and red flag was in turn replaced by the Nazi party swastika flag in 1935.
rules: Players take on the roles of England, France, the USSR and Czechoslovakia. Each follows a unique spiral track toward the middle of the board. The winner is the first to reach the central space, which is labelled “Deutschland is schutzlos” (Germany is defenseless).

Title: Powers
description: Forget the raging WWII: here, bankers try to take over the world! The board has an external track with spaces for 72 countries, enclosing two maps of the world with a network of red dots. There is a deck of country cards, each showing the flag, statistics, value and grid location. The 48-card Powers deck controls initial movement also triggering special events, bonuses and penalties. Some countries and map locations also trigger bonuses and penalties.
rules: Each round, the Broker turns over a country card, which becomes the objective, and then Power cards for each player in turn. These direct movement along the outer track. When reaching or passing the Embarkation space, a player stops and then moves along the map tracks. The first to reach the location of the target country collects the card and its value. Next player becomes Broker and repeats. After an agreed number of rounds, the player with the highest total country value wins.

Title: National Game of Peace for our Nation, The
description: This bingo-like game is themed on keeping the U.S. out of WWII. Each player gets a card with 8 boxes across the top, each with a slogan representing American values above two rows of numbered dots. The bottom of the card has a "reserve row" of dots numbered 1 to 9. There is a deck of cards made up of single-digit numbers and "3-star" cards with slogans.
rules: Cards are turned over one at a time. Players put a token onto the corresponding number in the reserve row. Beginning with the 2nd card, players may combine two reserve tokens matching one of the two-digit numbers on the top row of their card, and cover those spaces. A “three-star” card allows players to place a token directly onto one of the dots under that slogan. Allow top-row dots must be covered before any second row dot. First player to complete a card wins.

Title: Black Falcon of the Flying G-Men
description: The game is based on a 15-part adventure serial called the “Flying G-Men” by Columbia. Three government pilots, one of them disguised as “The Black Falcon”, are on the hunt for an enemy spy ring headed by a mystery man called “The Professor”. Players move around a web of interconnected tracks across a map of the United States.
rules: Two to four players race around a map of the United States, vying to capture the most enemy spies. A spy appears whenever one of the players lands on an airport and pulls a Spy Card to show where the next villain will appear on the map. First to reach the space with the spy captures him.

Title: L'Alliance Franco-Russe
description: This game radiates France’s late 19th century joy at finally finding a friend among the Great Powers, and its determination to avenge its defeat by Germany in 1871. Each square also has a caption consisting of two lines of rhyming verse. Spaces 2 and 3 are devoted to the lost provinces of Lorraine and Alsace. The first has the caption “Hope for imminent sovereign justice, Lorraine,” while the next reads: “The sons of Alsace, to the three colors of France, have pledged their hearts.”
rules: Game of Goose

Title: Belagerungsspiel (WWI Morser)
description: This mid-war game box features infantry hauling a 7-tonne, 21 cm Morser 10 howitzer through the mud. The board includes images of artillery firing on both sides, along with infantry in trenches and advancing in the open field, French cavalry and German lancers.
rules: Grosse Belagerungsspiel: 50 attackers vs 3 defenders.

Title: Dice (Ancient)
description: My collection includes pre-Christian Era dice in various shapes, dimensions and materials, as well as a knucklebone also used for gambling. Materials used include bone, blue faience, glass and stone.
rules: Many dice games over the centuries.

Title: Sprung auf, marsch, marsch!
description: While this game about joining the army still shows cavalry and no tanks, the CH Clover logo shows that it was published after the takeover of the company by Max Herbart in 1939.  In addition to featuring cavalry both charging and in reconnaissance, images on the board include artillery, motorcycles, field phone, infantry in the assault and pontoon bridge-building.
rules: This is a simple race game. The first player to reach space 70 wins.

Title: Auf der Reichsautobahn durch Grossdeutschland (Neue Ausgabe)
description: This "new edition" was required in late 1939 by Germany's conquest of Poland that summer. Grossdeutschland now includes Austria, western Czechoslovakia and the bulk of Poland (split with the USSR). The Polish territory has been renamed as the General Gouvernement area.
rules: Players race to complete one of four colored routes across Germany, facing hazard spaces that cost a turn or force additional payment into the pot. First to complete the assigned route wins the pot.

Title: Trench War (Anonymous)
description: This untitled and uncredited board paints a grim view of life in the trenches. Human corpses lie side by side with a dead rabbit, and a dog finds a new toy: a fallen soldier's helmet.
rules: Roll and move along a track. First to the finish wins.

Title: To Berlin
description: This undated game is a comprehensive tour around wartime Europe. Players start in London, pass through France, Italy, Austria, Bosnia, Switzerland, Belgium, Holland and Russia before arriving at the belt of fortresses surrounding Berlin. The fact that Russia is shown as still holding Warsaw suggests it was published early in the war.
rules: Red squares are disasters, starred spaces represent fortresses and red-crossed spaces would appear to be recovery/hospital squares. No rules are shown on the board.

Title: Spin 'Em Target Game
description: This metal target shooting game has a wind-up mechanism that spins three target disks. High-value targets (200 - 1000 points) are on the disks. Other targets on the board are worth 25 points, while the central gun is worth 1000. The gun shown is from the same publisher but not this game: the original gun for this game fired corks rather than rubber-tipped darts.
rules: Most points after an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Aerial Attack
description: Players move metal airplanes that represent defending fighters. The illustrations show the attacking bombers as black and the fighters as red. The first 34 spaces on the 60-space track deal with the air battle. The remainder focus on events on the ground. The bonus and penalty spaces on the ground illustrate good and bad practices during an air raid.
rules: First to reach the end of the 60-space track wins.

Title: Attack
description: This mid-war game offers everything: infantry, mortars, artillery, tanks and airplanes. Players also plot the placement of minefields. Pieces start in the camp, ammo dump and airfield. The objective is to move across the field and capture or destroy either the opposing camp or ammo dump.
rules: Units are moved one at a time within their  allowed movement ranges. Units can capture multiple enemy units simply by entering their square. Alternatively, they can shoot within their range and destroy a single enemy. First player to capture an enemy dump or camp wins.

Title: Panzerkampfwagen vor!
description: The game board is filled with Panzer I tanks charging across a contested countryside. The light tanks are shown driving along dirt roads, heading down a slope, crossing a large ditch, smashing a line of barbed wire, concealing themselves in smoke and firing their twin machine-guns with abandon at defending infantry and anti-tank guns. It also shows that tanks broke down often and were vulnerable to anti-tank guns and airplanes.
rules: First to reach the end of the 90-space track wins.

Title: Denn wir fahren gegen Engelland
description: The title says it all: after taking Poland, "then we go after England". Both the box and the board show the waters around Britain flooded with U-boats and Heinkel 111 bombers, sinking English ships one after another.
rules: Simple race game: first player to complete the 100-space track wins.

Title: Mit 'Prien' gegen England
description: This game celebrates Gunther Prien, commander of U-47, who on Oct. 13, 1939, snuck into the British naval base at Scapa Flow and sank the outdated battleship Royal Oak. The event had little military impact, but was a major propaganda coup. When U-47 returned to Bremerhaven on Oct. 17, the crew was met by admirals and handed medals, then flown to Berlin in Hitler’s personal airplane for a parade through the capital, press conferences, speeches and lunch with the Fuhrer.
rules: Standard roll and move. The first player to reach Scapa Flow and then return safely wins the game.

Title: Pinpoint the Bomber
description: This game was published as a book that includes maps in both color and black & white. Players must copy and make their own playing pieces, compass, angle indicator and latitude/longitude finder. The book contains three games: Black is a solo effort to see how fast and accurate a player can be; Red is for up to 4 players racing to bomb Coblenz; and Green is for any number of players to find the required Pinpoints. Movement is by die roll or card draw and players must land on the target by exact count. Mission briefings in the book may include enemy fighter attacks or other events causing the player to head off track.
rules: The solo game is to reach the objective in as few turns as possible. The multi-player games are a race to reach the target first.

Title: Housing Drive
description: As Britain rebuilds its shattered landscape and welcomes veterans home, players compete to build new housing developments. The twist? The game assumes that such activity is a money-loser, and the winner of the game is the one who gets the job done with the fewest government subsidies!

Title: Guerras Modernas, Las
description: Subtitled “French-German Combat”, this checkers-type game has an 11 x 11 board. Both orthogonal and diagonal lines connect the dots. Each side gets 33 pieces: a flagbearer; two artillery; two air units (biplane and airship); 11 cavalry; and 17 infantry. Each player sets up on the three closest ranks: cavalry on the front rank; flag bearer in the middle of the second rank; guns on the corners of the back rank; aerial units next to the guns; and infantry filling in the rest.
rules: The rules are similar to checkers, but pieces have varying abilities. Infantry moves one space orthogonally, cavalry one space diagonally. Enemy pieces are captured by jumping over them to an empty space. Artillery can move across any number of empty spaces orthogonally (and then jump to capture). Air units can do the same in any direction. Capturing an enemy is compulsory if possible. Infantry or cavalry capturing enemy flag become artillery. Pieces can only move backward if in enemy territory.

Title: Great American Flag Game, The
description: A celebration of the American flag and all it stands for. The first edition was published in 1940, while the United States was still isolationist. Its cover shows historical achievements. The wartime edition, published in 1940, adds considerable military might to the front cover illustration

Title: Salute!
description: This game adds a physical twist to the standard "rise through the ranks" game. A small American flag is placed upright in the middle of the board. Whenever any player lands on a "Salute the Flag" space, everyone must jump to their feet and salute. Failure to act quickly enough is penalized by a two-rank demotion on the board. Players follow four different tracks through the Army, Navy, Marines and Air Corps.
rules: Standard roll and move. First to reach the Commander-in-Chief space in the middle of the board wins.

Title: Air Race
description: The box and board both feature game the Gloster Meteor, Britain’s first production jet fighter. A few flew late in WWII and some remained in service into the early 1960s. The board has a spiral 100-space track, with green bonus and red penalty spaces along the way. Playing pieces are colored plastic planes. A later edition featured a twin-tailed de Havilland Vampire, the first jet aircraft to cross the Atlantic Ocean.
rules: First to the end of the track wins.

Title: Soldiers
description: Despite the modern monoplane on the box, this shooter features stand-up soldiers in archaic dress uniforms and an elastic powered wooden cannon.
rules: Player to down the most targets wins.

Title: Vnimanie - Ataka!
description: This game comes packaged in a cardboard Soviet officer’s briefcase that has stitching around the edges and an image of a compass, pens, protractor and ruler on an inner flaps.  The playing pieces are cardboard. Each player has a small planning map for his side of the field . The playing board comes in four rectangular pieces that form an 8 x 8 grid over a map with roads, trees and a river. The backs of the map pieces have detailed diagrams of the weapon systems used in the game.
rules: Players set up their forces in secret by placing a fold-out “smoke screen” as a divider between them. Pieces have different move and capture powers. Eliminating the opposing army wins the game.

Title: Talita
description: This game consists of an envelope containing 4 cardboard cards. One one side of each card is a board for the Target Flight game. On the other are two boards for the Capture Ship game. The Flight game has start and end spaces numbered 1 and 22, and there are 20 plane outlines spread across a map of Europe. The ship game has outlines of three types of ship (blue warships, white merchant ships and black submarines) along with naval mines.
rules: These appear to be simple paper and pencil games but the rules are not clear from context.

Title: Conquete du Monde, La
description: This is the original Risk game, invented in France. The mapboard is identical except for the French area names, as are the corresponding reinforcement cards. The only significant difference is that this version has a fixed rather than escalating value for reinforcement sets. It took Miro two years to persuade Parker Bros. to publish the game.
rules: Players choose or draw starting countries. Each turn they can move armies into an adjacent area and fight any defenders. Players roll one die per piece in the combat, eliminating an enemy on a 6. Rounds of combat continue until one player is eliminated or retreats. Players produce new armies based on the number of areas and complete continents they control. Fights also grant combat cards Sets of the same color or weapon can be turned in for more armies. Last player standing wins.

Title: Luftkrieg
description: This game produced shortly after Hitler's rise to power did not proclaim Germany’s superiority. Rather, with its subtitle Ein Fliegerspiel zur belehrung uber die Luftgefahr (A game about the aerial danger), it bemoans Germany’s aerial weakness and urgent need to re-arm.  There are five intermingled tracks from Russia, France, Czechoslovakia and Lithuania. The strength of Germany's neighbours is shown, from Lithuania's 65 planes to France's 4,300 and the USSR's 5,000.
rules: Up to five players move along unique 63-space tracks, vying to be the first to bomb Germany and return to base.

Title: Kriegspiel Junior
description: This seems to be a WWI remake of the 1895 game Waterloo with a map of interlaced roads with black dots. Berlin and Vienna are at one end of the map, with Paris and Brussels on the other. Each city has a triangle of 3 grey spaces. The Rhine River wanders across the middle of the map, and two other cities are shown (Strassburg and Cologne) that have no impact on play. There are six fortresses, each containing a grey dot. Playing pieces are similar, with 8 infantry and 2 cavalry per side.
rules: The winner is the first to occupy 2 adjacent grey dots on an enemy city. In turn, players roll 3 dice. Each die can be used to move one piece that many dots. Two or even all three can be used to move a single cavalry. Doubles allow 2 pieces to move in tandem, and another roll. A piece cannot move through any other piece, and captures an enemy by landing on it be exact count. Pieces in forts and pieces adjacent to another friendly unit cannot be captured.

Title: Hold the Fort
description: This game shows American troops in post-Civil War blue uniforms assaulting an unidentified enemy on a hill top.
rules: Standard Grosse Belagerungsspiel

Title: Zeeslag
description: This naval game is an early Dutch version of Battleships. The board is a brownish grey, and the cardboard ships are outlined in black with an overhead view. The game also comes with markers to show hits and misses. This copy has a retail tag from Het Ijzerhuis, a hardware company founded in 1907 and still in business.
rules: Each player gets a 10 x 10 blank board and a fleet of one cruiser, two torpedo boats, two minelayers and four submarines. After choosing hidden locations for each of their own ships, players take turns calling out grid squares and marking them either with misses or hits by type. The first player to sink the other's entire fleet wins.

Title: Marine!
description: This game focuses on squad-level combat in Viet Nam across a series of scenarios. The map is a plain dark brown hex grid on light brown paper, and the counters are yellow-on-green for the Americans and green-on-yellow for the VC/NVA player.
rules: This hex combat game includes rules for airstrikes, naval bombardment, amphibious landing, off-board artillery, on-board rockets, mortars and machine guns, anti-aircraft units, helicopter transports and gunships, tanks, half-tracks, and a range of infantry units from Special Forces and Marines to Vietnamese security teams and civilians.

Title: Strategy: The Game of Armies
description: The cover art can only be described as garish. A caricature of a Roman god stands holding the entire globe, while airplanes buzz off his shoulder and tanks and guns march across the foreground. It is a dazzle of primary colors: yellow, red and blue. The board too is colorful if more functional. It is much larger (32 by 45 squares), and is divided into 62 “sectors” or groups of squares, each in different shades of white, pink, red or yellow. There also are terrain features.
rules: Each player gets 20 pieces: four guns and 16 infantry. All start in the Barracks next to each player’s capital. Players each turn can move one or more piece a total number of spaces equal to sum of 2D6. Guns are extremely powerful: moving into a new region kills all enemy pieces there; pieces may not move into a region with a gun. The goal is to get four pieces close enough to the enemy capital that they all can move into it on a single die roll.

Title: China War, The
description: This game includes three scenarios involving China: a full-out war with the Soviet Union; a renewed conflict with Viet Nam; and an invasion by Taiwan (still considered a possibility at the time).
rules: Hex movement and combat.

Title: Supremacy
description: This game puts a martial spin on Monopoly. Players roll dice to move around the board and buy control of strategic properties. Landing on a property owned by another forces payment, one that rises if the owner builds forts on it. The currency, however, is in military units rather than cash.
rules: Monopoly

Title: Tank Attack
description: An American tank must navigate past German infantry, machine gun nests and anti-tank guns to reach its objective. The small cardboard game puzzle comes in an envelope that advertises war bonds.
rules: The player shuffles the cardboard pieces within the frame to advance the tank to its objective.

Title: Battle Action
description: This pinball game features images of combat and technologies from late in WWII and the Korean War.
rules: Players pull back and release the spring-loaded plunger to launch a marble. They score points for landing either in targets on the board or at the bottom of the board. Highest total points wins.

Title: Air-Sea Rescue, Game of
description: This game showcases the RAF's Air-Sea Rescue Service, the combination of boats and planes used to spot downed pilots in the English Channel, to drop dinghies and supplies to keep them alive, and then to pluck them from the water and bring them home. There are various bonus and penalty spaces along the way, showing how airmen coped with the trials of ditching in the sea.
rules: The players get three counters. The downed pilot starts at space 1 and rolls to move up the 84-space track. At space 20, the player launches the search plane from 84. The player can use his die to move either counter, not both. The two must meet at space 44. When they do, the player begins moving the launch, also from 84. It must meet the pilot at space 60 and then bring him back to base at 84. First player back wins.

Title: Zeppelin Spiel
description: This game shows Germany's astonishingly broad network of bases and routes that it built up by the start of WWI. The board is a huge (88 cm x 77 cm) topographical map of Germany and bordering territories. It existing and planned zeppelin hangars, both military and civilian. Black flags sprout from the locations of zeppelin accidents that occurred during the development of this technology.
rules: Players roll and move along a 160-space track. The goal is to be the first to reach New York with a transatlantic airship voyage.

Title: Reichsautobahnen im Grossdeutschen Reich
description: The mapboard of this travel game shows Germany's autobahns after the Anschluss with Austria to form Grossdeutschland or Greater Germany in March 1938. The playing pieces are pressed metal cars.
rules: Players draw travel cards to determine their starting points, destinations and waypoints. The rules offer three variants, but generally they roll dice and compete to collect the most completed travel cards.

Title: Weltflug
description: This air race game features an Me 109 fighter on the box, which contains a variety of other common games also. There are four playing pieces, each with a colored plastic twin-engine plane mounted atop a wooden chess pawn. The board has a 12 x 18 space grid. The colored spaces in each corner are the starting spaces. The matching color in the middle is the goal. Getting there is far from straightforward.
rules: Starting in his own corner, the player must land on each of the other three corner spaces and then his own colored middle space. In turn players roll a single die and move exactly that number of spaces in a straight line. Landing on an arrow means the next move must be in that direction. Landing on a picture space requires jumping to the matching numbered picture. Landing on one's own home field allows jumping to a space adjoining the next field. First to the middle wins.

Title: Siegreich voran!
description: Victorious Advance" is another early WWII air combat game from Germany. The game board has an airbase on each side, separated by a landscape of woods and buildings. Each side has 1 command piece, 4 bombers, 10 fighters, 3 barrage balloons and 3 anti-aircraft units.
rules: Each player starts with aircraft on home base and placement of barrage balloons and anti-aircraft guns to protect that territory. Movement is by die roll. Players may move a fighter or a bomber. The goal is to bomb the enemy base while protecting the friendly base. Fighters shoot down bombers or enemy fighters by landing on them by exact count. Bombers succeed by reaching a target square. Points are awarded for bombs dropped and enemy aircraft downed.

Title: War
description: This is a simple roll-and-move game, apparently self-published by Frank Prindle of San Francisco shortly after the Spanish-American War of 1898. The board appears to show a stylized view of the entrance to Santiago Bay, looking northeast to the San Juan and Kettle hills. The image shows naval action in the foreground, including a sunken Spanish ship, with land forces moving on both sides of the bay. The objective space (100) shows an American flag waving over a cloud-topped hill, presumably San Juan Hill.
rules: Players start at space 1 at the mouth of Santiago Bay and roll dice in a race to reach the 100 space. Various spaces along the way offer rewards and penalties.

Title: Tactics II
description: This improvement on Charles Roberts' self-published breakthrough game Tactics was the first published by the Avalon Hill Co. It retains many features of earlier chess-type games, including a square grid over a map board with various terrain features, and pieces with differing movement and combat abilities. His big change was to allow a player to move all his pieces on a turn and then carry out resulting attacks.
rules: The pieces are marked with movement points that show how far it can move in a turn, and strength points that determine the odds in a battle. The combat results table has columns for different odds, and a die roll determines the result within the appropriate column.

Title: Flotilla
description: This abstract naval game has a couple of interesting features. First, there are multiple maps (two for the standard game and three for the deluxe version). Each map has a triangular grid. The maps are laid on top of a cork board, and the playing pieces are mounted on pins that stick into that board. Second, combat is resolved with a 12-sided teetotum, which provides odds-based results. If two equal ships fight, the color of the letter is all that matters. If two different ships fight, the letter determines the winner. The teetotum has 6 Bs for Battleship, 4 Cs for Cruiser and 2 Ds for Destroyer. A battleship therefore has a 3:1 chance of beating a destroyer, 3:2 against a cruiser and even odds against another battleship.
rules: Each player starts with one battleship, three cruisers, five destroyers and one submarine. The submarine fights at even odds against any other ship. The submarine can move only one point, while the battleships and cruisers can move two and the destroyers three. In each turn, a player may use a maximum of 10 movement points, split amongst his ships as he sees fit within the limits of each ship type. Combat is triggered by moving onto an enemy ship's location. The losing ship is removed. In the advanced game, ships have numerical strengths and the highest value wins. Players may attack with multiple ships to gang up on a larger foe. The land areas are marked with ports having values of 10, 20 or 30. The winner is the first to capture 100 points worth of enemy ports.

Title: GHQ
description: This game shows how the Allies expected the 1940 invasion of France to go. The game acknowledged recent developments in military technology, with both sides having leg and motorized infantry and armor. The board has a web of points joined by lines covering France, Germany, Britain, Belgium, Holland and even Switzerland. The game suggests that the Allies are just as likely to take Berlin as to lose Paris.
rules: Each turn, a player rolls a die to see how many units can be moved. Infantry units move only to an adjacent space; motorized infantry by two spaces and armor by three. Successful combat is based on outflanking the enemy. Capturing an enemy piece requires occupying either two adjacent spaces to take an infantry or three adjacent spaces to take armor. The Germans win by capturing Paris and Dover; the Allies by taking Berlin and Munich.

Title: Blackout
description: The board encased in a box shows a city street with three bombers lining up to do their dirty work. There are 8 indentations in the board over certain windows and streetlights. Players must try to juggle the 8 balls in the box into all 8 indentations to black out the street and save the city.
rules: Fastest to succeed wins.

Title: War and Diplomacy
description: This game has a deck of 32 letter cards with the letters A, Y, R, M, N and V: 8 each of the vowels and 4 each of the consonants. These letters can form four words: Army, Navy, Many and Vary. The first two are considered "war" words and the other two "diplomacy" words. There also are 17 "war pennants" and 16 "diplomacy or truce flags". These are placed in separate piles, face up, and claimed as the game proceeds. The rules say the game is best played by four, with opposites being partners.
rules: Dealer passes out 7 cards per player. Player to left of dealer leads a letter. Others must play different letters, resulting in one of the four words Whoever played the first letter gets either a 2-point (truce) or 3-point (war) card. A player unable to make a legal play instead plays any card, which causes the round to "bust", giving no one a point card. Game ends when either the truce or war pile runs out. Player or team with the most points wins.

Title: Convois, Les
description: The war may be over, but players still need to use jeeps to escort convoys safely across a bleak countryside to the warehouse. Each player gets five metal jeep pieces.
rules: First to reach the warehouse with all five jeeps wins.

Title: H.M. Squadron or Naval Manoeuvres
description: This ironclad-era naval game is more about showing the flag to win hearts and minds than bombarding and capturing cities. Two tracks of hexagonal spaces run around England, Scotland and Wales. Black lines connect coastal cities to hexes on the two tracks. Each hexagon is in one of 6 colors: blue, green, red, brown, white and yellow. One die has faces in the six colors; the other, numbers from 1-5 and G for Gale. There are four painted metal ships as playing pieces.
rules: Players place their ironclads on starting hexes off Penzance and move clockwise or counter-clockwise on the inner or outer track. In turn, they roll both dice: for example, Red 4 means moving to the fourth red space along the track, making it possible to go halfway round the board on a single turn. Passing a city allows placing a token or removing an opposing one. A player placing two tokens on a city captures it permanently. Game ends when all are captured. Player with the most cities wins.

Title: Anno 2000
description: The best thing about this game is its gorgeous 1950s science-fiction artwork, both on the box and on the board. Some features of early space flight are fairly realistic; the evil space robot not so much. The game was originally published in Germany under the title Mit Sputnik in dem Weltraum, and later issued with different titles in multiple languages including French, Greek and German.
rules: It's a standard roll and move as most players try to leave earth, explore space and be first to return home safely, but one plays the evil robot who wins by eliminating the other players by landing on them. The robot has a whole network of exclusive movement tracks to help it capture the unwary space traveler.

Title: Volkerkrieg, Der
description: This game published early in WW! portrays it as the "people's war". Players choose to enlist as infantry, artillery, cavalry or pilots of airships and planes.
rules: Standard roll and move. First to reach the end of the 100-space track wins.

Title: Dominoes (Napoleonic PoW)
description: Many prisoners of war during the Napoleonic era eased boredom and earned money by carving games and other objects out of discarded soup bones. This set of dominoes is contained in a box with two sliding lids also used to keep score for cribbage. It was made by a French prisoner and sold to an English buyer.
rules: Standard dominoes.

Title: Kosmonavti (Космонавти)
description: The first two Russians in space, Yuri Gagarin and Gherman Titov, are featured on the cover of this Bulgarian game. The game board goes beyond their exploits and assumes that players will soon be able to travel to the moon and other planets. Some images on the board like spacewalking are fairly realistic; others a bit futuristic. The main game is one one side of the board. The back has a second, simpler one.
rules: In the main game, players first do a low orbit of earth before moving onto one of the planetary trajectories. Once they reach a planet, they must orbit until they can get to the landing track. Having landed, they take off, return to a high orbit and then and aim for another planet. Once they have landed on all four, they return to high orbit until they reach a landing track that brings them back to the surface. First one home wins. The second game has a simple spiral track.

Title: Sino-Japanese War
description: The art provides a vivid history of Japan's battles in China during 1937, from the Marco Polo Bridge incident in July through the conquest of Beijing, Shanghai, Bautou, Nanjing, Xiamen, Jiujiang, Canton (Guandong) and Hankou.
rules: Standard mawari-sugoroku: players roll dice to advance along the track. First to reach the objective space in the middle wins.

Title: Rubicon, The Game of
description: This is an abstracted game of colonial warfare with objectives set in Uganda. Most pieces start in Asia (really Arabia) and cross to Africa trying to capture the most valuable parts of Uganda. To aid their crossing of the Red Sea, each player starts with three pieces in Fort Lugard, which was located just outside Kampala.
rules: Players start with 3 pieces each in Fort Lugard and 9 in Asia. They move alternately, one piece at a time, advancing by one space or jumping as in Halma. Movement in Asia is point to point. Pieces cannot cross to Africa until a friendly piece has moved from Fort Lugard to an African port. The checkered board in Africa includes impassable rivers. When they reach Uganda, they place the piece on the space with the highest value available. Highest total points in Uganda is the winner.

Title: Feindliche Flieger in Sicht!
description: This air defence game was published in 1938 by Klee. Its owners, brothers Leopold and Moritz Bromeisl, hoped it would show that they were patriotic Germans, but it did them no good. Like many other Jewish business owners at the time, they were forced to sell at a pittance. The edition shown here was published about 1940 by the new owner, who had removed the Klee "clover" logo.
rules: Standard roll and move. Illustrated spaces show both air battles and good and bad safety practices for those under bombardment. Landing on these spaces means bonuses or penalties. First to the end of the track wins.

Title: Jeu de la Victoire
description: This game presents a very graphic French perspective on WWI. The attack runs chronologically from the attack on France and Belgium to the armistice and handover of Alsace and Lorraine. As well as battles, spaces show German atrocities such as the execution of nurse Edith Cavell,  the torture of prisoners and the use of civilians as slave workers, and French civilian sacrifices. It is a game that says “we won”, but at a horrible cost.
rules: Standard Game of Goose: first to complete the 63-space inward spiral track wins.

Title: Kampfgeschwader vorwarts!
description: This game features the stolid Junkers Ju-52 transport plane in a bombing role. The board has a 20 x 22 square grid over a generic map. Each player has two forts, two bomber bases and two fighter bases, and starts with 4 fighters, 6 bombers and 4 flak guns.
rules:
In turn, players move a single piece. Fighters move 5, bombers 3 and flak 1. A fighter kills a bomber or another fighter by landing on its space. 
A bomber hits anti-aircraft artillery in the same way. Flak kills all enemy planes within its 1-space range and protects friendly planes there. Players get a point for each kill and for each bomb run over the enemy base. Most points wins.

Title: Vozdushny Boi (Воздушны Бои)
description: The cover features a top-down view of three Sukhoi Su-7B (NATO Fitter) fighter-bombers. The board feature an aerial view of mostly rural terrain as seen from an altitude low enough to show individual houses. While the board is laid out on a grid, the movement locations are actually at the corners of each square. The playing pieces are in red and yellow plastic.
rules: Each player has a squadron of six fighter bombers and another 10 escorting fighters. The objective is to shoot down the enemy’s planes and bomb the opposing airfields. Attackers also have to avoid air defenses that include both anti-aircraft guns and missile sites.

Title: Vsegda Gotov (Всегда Готов)
description: The electric Q&A game features set of cards about Soviet accomplishments. Each shows a range of images that must be paired with descriptions. Most notable is a card showing Soviet achievements in space, from the original Russian rocket scientist to the Soyuz space station.
rules: Most correct answers in an agreed time frame wins.

Title: L'Attaque (wooden export edition)
description: This edition has pieces with wooden stands and backs rather than metal, and appears to have been Hermance Edan's first attempt to export her game.
rules: Standard L'Attaque

Title: Guided Missile Air Force Game
description: This is the Air Force version of a game that also had Naval and Army versions. The board is inset in the box, and has small holes representing specific targets. Raised platforms on the sides allow each player to mark targets hit.
rules: Players take turns spinning a small top across the board, and score points for having it land in specific holes. Player getting the most total points after an agreed number of spins wins.

Title: Jeu des armées, Le
description: This game was published just before Germany invaded Belgium in 1940. The box art appears to show Belgian defenders under heavy attack by unmarked planes that appear to be German Dornier Do17 light bombers. The board is a 16 x 20 grid laid over a three-colour map. The playing pieces are wooden in four shapes. While a single tank is shown on the box and a few small tank outlines on the map, the game does not appear to anticipate the critical role of armour in the coming German blitzkrieg.
rules: Each side gets 24 infantry (cubes), 8 artillery (rectangles), 2 motorized divisions (cylinders) and 6 bridging units (triangles). Infantry moves one orthogonally; motorized infantry can move any number of unblocked spaces in a single direction; artillery moves only one space diagonally. Players win either by destroying the enemy army or by capturing their opponent’s key objectives: government, broadcasting and arms production. Movement is affected by a variety of terrain and obstacles that can require support by either artillery or bridging units.

Title: Militaire
description: The 54-card deck includes seven each of Lance Corporals (worth 5 points), Corporals (10) and Sergeants (15). There are four each of the officer ranks: 2nd Lieutenant, Lieutenant, Captain, Major, Lieutenant Colonel, Colonel, Brigadier and General, each showing the insignia of their rank. The Sergeant Major is a wild card that can be used as any rank.
rules: Players start with a hand of seven cards. Each turn they draw and discard one card. Players try to collect sets of 3 or 4 of the same rank or runs of consecutive ranks. The first player to collect either two sets or runs, or a single run of 6 or 7 cards, wins the round. He scores the value of the cards in his set(s), minus that of any unused card in hand and collects a bonus for each set. Bonus values range from 10 for a 3-card run 100 points for a 7-card run.

Title: Nuclear War
description: This is a highly cynical game about nuclear confrontation. There are population cards, bomb cards, delivery system cards, secret cards and a spinner in the box that affects each bomb result. The aim is to wipe out all other powers either by attracting their population or, more likely, by killing them. Propaganda cards attract people from enemies, but are useless once bombs start flying. There are a handful of anti-missile cards for defense.
rules: Starting population is determined by player count. Each player in turn draws 7 cards, immediately playing and replacing any Secret cards, and then placing two cards face down on the table. On each subsequent turn, player turns over first card and adds another - so always must commit plays two turns ahead. Attacking requires a bomb with a suitable delivery vehicle. Spinner adds effect to bomb drop. Last player with any population left wins.

Title: Quax der Bruchpilot
description: This wartime game was based on the Dec. 1941 comedy movie of the same name, in turn based on a story by Hermann Grote. The title translates as either "Crash Pilot" or "Test Pilot". The movie is set in the pre-war years. The hero, Quax, is an ordinary man who wins free flying lessons in a newspaper contest. Despite many misadventures, he somehow becomes a good pilot. The game board features photos from the movie.
rules: First to the end of the 50-space track wins.

Title: Belagerungsspiel (Franco-Prussian)
description: This version of Belagerungsspiel is themed on the Franco-Prussian War of 1870-71. While published in victorious Germany, however, it was intended for sale across Europe, with the box cover including the title in German, French, Dutch and Hungarian. It therefore shows French artillery troops in correct uniform firing the guns at the fortress. However, the troops are firing what are clearly Krupp breech-loading siege guns – the very ones recently used against the French in the siege of Paris!
rules: Standard Belagerungsspiel rules: 24 attackers, 2 defenders.

Title: Get in the Scrap
description: Players compete to collect scrap and deliver it to appropriate furnaces to support war production. The game booklet a Scrap Quiz that teaches players recycling tidbits. For instance, they learn that while a single discarded farm tractor could be turned into 580 machine guns, it took 7,700 aluminum pots and pans to make one fighter plane.
rules: Players first move three small “truckload” pawns to a scrap dealer. This turns into a “carload” of scrap to be delivered to each player’s choice of three destinations: an Open-Hearth Iron Furnace, an Iron Foundry or an Electric Furnace.

Title: Dennis Wheatley's Blockade
description: This is more of a naval and economic affair than its predecessor, Dennis Wheatley's Invasion. The playing pieces are identical to those of Invasion, but represent surface fleets, submarines and air forces. Most of the board is ocean, again marked with a grid for movement. And again the four nations have fake names: Cockayne, Leoland, Adlerreich and Ursinia. The first two share an island in the middle of the board; the latter have a land border and occupy the northern reaches.
rules: To be added

Title: Democracy
publisher: John M. Kosnis & James Rice Jr.
description: This bingo-like game is very much about ensuring the preservation of democracy by preparing for war. Each player has a card showing the game’s 48 available “defense units” organized into 8 categories: soldiers, tanks, artillery, signals, fighters, bombers, officers and industry. There also is a metal slider to record money. The corresponding tiles are held initially on a Priorities Board. Players move around a Monopoly-style board with 11 spaces per side and a large spinner in the middle.
rules: As players spin their way around the board, they try to accumulate all six pieces in one of the eight categories. Each spin results in a 1-12 move plus a choice of four tiles. When players move into some spaces on the board, they get money or are allowed to take another tile from the Priorities Board or other players. Other spaces inflict penalties. First to complete a category wins.

Title: Volldampf voraus
description: This may be the last naval wargame published before the launch of HMS Dreadnought in 1906 revolutionized the design of large warships. In this game, each player has three ship types: ships of the line, large cruisers and small cruisers (in English terms, pre-dreadnought battleships, armoured cruisers and protected cruisers). The ships are metal. The game was patented in 1903 and produced till at least 1909. In 1908 twenty games were purchased by the German emperor for the German navy.
rules: Each of the two players has a fleet consisting of six ships: two ships of the line with three ship towers, two large cruisers with two towers, and two small cruisers with a single tower. There also is shore-based artillery. Players move along curved circles between ship locations, which limit the degree to which ships can change facing. A ship is hit if it is caught in the crossfire of two enemy ships or artillery. A hit causes a ship to lose a single tower, destroying a ship with only one left. The winner is the player who destroys the enemy fleet.

Title: Phase Line Smash
description: In this solitaire game, players take the American role against Hussein in the liberation of Kuwait. The campaign’s ultimate outcome is not in doubt, of course, but winning the game is not easy. To win, the player must better the historical result, which saw the US VII Corps chew up 4,600 armored vehicles and 10 divisions in four days, while taking only 239 casualties.
rules:
The player activates one unit at a time for one of three types of possible attacks that must be coordinated with artillery and air strikes.
