The right combination can blow a hole in the Iraqi line and roll up a flank in a single turn. Losses to friendly fire are possible, and the game engine will attack an exposed flank. Managing the flow of supplies is critical.

Title: In der Gross-Stadt
description: This game sends a message of calm and normality amid the chaos of the Great War. The board is a detailed street map of Berlin, and the game track offers a thorough tour of the capital’s many cultural attractions. The only hints that a war is on can be seen in some of the close-up images of the featured little boy as he tours the big city, such as one showing him marching with his umbrella alongside a rank of soldiers on parade.
rules: Standard roll and move. First to the end of the 130-space track wins.

Title: 5th Column Spy Hunters, The Game of
description: 5th Column Spy Hunter is a point-to-point movement game for 2 teams. The board is a map of the United States that features dots for both cities and major military bases.
rules: 5th Columnists score points for achieving goals such as stealing documents from a military base by ending their moves there. The Spy Hunters get points for frustrating the 5th Columnists, and the winner is the first team to 15 points. ending their moves on designated spots (for example: air naval or army bases to steal documents), the Spy Hunters get points based on failures by the 5th Columnists. The winner is the first team to 15 points.

Title: Blast 'Em
description: This is an elaborately packaged variant on the venerable Battleships. A pebbled-cardboard binder holds separate folios for the Battle of Britain, Battle of Midway and Battle of the Solomons. Each uses the standard 10 x 10 grid, but has a different pattern of land areas within the ocean. The player's targets have different names and shapes than the standard game.
rules: The Battleship and Submarine must be marked entirely in sea areas. The Land Battery and Tank Force must be placed on land areas. And the Air Squadron (three spaces in a vee) can be placed anywhere on the grid. Otherwise standard: players take turns calling out spaces for hits or misses until all of one player's pieces have been destroyed.

Title: Bomber Ball
description: This is a very elaborate bomb-dropping game. There is a target board in the bottom of the box and two 3D cardboard ships (one an aircraft carrier) intended to be placed on the floor. The bomber is a large flat cardboard shape with four holes for bombs along its fuselage. Holding the bombs in place is a cardboard panel that can be drawn back, allowing one bomb to drop at a time. The game comes with both marbles and softer chalk balls as bombs (which make it easier to see where a bomb has hit).
rules: Highest score after an agreed number of bombing runs wins.

Title: Heitai San
description: This pre-Pearl Harbor Japanese game was aimed at second-graders. The movement track shows soldiers in a wide variety of roles, including infantry, artillery, tanks, cavalry, transportation, medical and engineering. Unusually, it also features a beach assault by Special Naval Landing Force troops, naval bombers and warships, bridging the traditional rivalry between army and naval forces.
rules: Standard sugoroku. First to reach the Banzai space in the middle wins.

Title: Electric Air Age Spotter
description: This multiple choice knowledge game has players inserting metal points into planes and a matching fact.
rules: Player to answer the most questions successfully wins.

Title: Wartime Easy Money
description: This is a substantially redesigned version of the 1930s business game Easy Money. Players still move around the board and buy properties, but there also is an inner circle of events. Most of the actions involve citizen support for the war effort. Even when passing go and collection income, players give half of it back to buy War Bonds.
rules: Scans of the two pages of rules are included in images.

Title: Junior Bombsight Game
description: The box features a Dauntless dive bomber in action. The board is inset in the box, showing an aerial view of ground targets. Players use a three-tube bombsight made of heavy cardboard. Two of the tubes provide a binocular sight, with cross-hatch plates for aiming. The third parallel tube is used for the bombs. These are white marbles that the players drop one at a time through the tube.
rules: The targets inset in the board have assorted values, and are hinged to provide visible evidence of a hit. The player scoring the highest total points after an agreed number of bomb drops wins.

Title: International Game of Spy
description: This game takes the hidden set-up mechanism of L'Attaque/Stratego and turns it into a 4-player contest. Each player has two target spaces (Palace and Coat of Arms) within a triangle of the board. Much of the border between players is blocked by lakes, mountains or forts, forcing much of the action toward the middle. Pieces have values of 1-10, plus some with special powers, mounted on small cardboard circles.
rules: Each player sets up secretly. Players in turn move a single piece one space. If they move into a space occupied by an opposing piece, they can ask "Who's There?" Both pieces are revealed, with lower or tied value removed. Combat is optional and more than one piece may occupy a space. Secret Police cannot be lost, but captures only an opposing Spy. Winner is first to occupy either target space of any player.

Title: Marine Spiel
description: This game offers a detailed view of a turn-of-the-century German battleship. It has an unusual board. When first unfolded, it shows a side view of the ship. Then two more panels fold down from the deckline to show the playing board: a red-on-white schematic of the ship's interior. The artwork is loosely based on the pre-dreadnought battleship Kaiser Wilhelm III, commission in 1898.
rules: Players roll dice to move through a detailed schematic of the ship’s compartments. The 46-space track runs from a bunkroom in the bow to the engine room in the stern. First player to the engine room wins.

Title: Siege de Sébastopol
description: This is a lavishly finished French version of Belagerungsspiel featuring illustrations of combat during the Crimean War. An aerial view of the fortress and attacking fleet can be seen behind the images of Russian defenders at top right. Attacking troops include infantry, cannon and mortar. Many of the metallic features in the images are burnished with a shiny golden ink.
rules: Standard Belagerungsspiel rules: 24 attackers versus 2 defenders.

Title: Spot-a-Plane
description: Plastic planes fly over a fictitious landscape, advancing only when their owning player correctly identifies a plane by its silhouette on a card. The game track is 54 spaces long, ending in "Mission Accomplished". Landing on a red or blue dot requires drawing from corresponding decks with potential bonuses or penalties.
rules: In turn, players draw from a deck of cards with silhouettes on one side and full details on the other. They study the silhouette and then cover it. Then they look at a chart showing all the planes in the game. They make a choice, then reveal the card side with details. If correct, player moves forward by a number on the card. First to reach the end of the track wins. Advanced rules require identifying the plane without using the reference card.

Title: Assault, Game of
description: This is an early English-language version of the classic Belagerungsspiel. The board is a lithograph pasted onto wood. It comes with a set of wooden playing pieces shaped and painted like Napoleonic soldiers.
rules: See Belagerungsspiel (Napoleonic)

Title: Kop the Kaiser
description: This game celebrates America's entry into WWI. The abstract board has US military bases around an outer circle, with paths leading to Europe and eventually to Berlin in the middle. Each player represents a branch of the American forces: Infantry, Cavalry, Artillery, Marine Corps, Engineers, or the Aviation Corps. The board shows Patent Pending but no designer or publisher.
rules: Players start from their service branch's US base. They first roll their way all around the outer circle “dodging submarines”. Once back at base, they head to France and the Wotan Line (the northern part of the Hindenburg Line), then through Belgium, across the Rhine and into Germany. First to   enter Berlin and capture Kaiser Wilhelm wins.

Title: Battle
description: This is a game of movement across a network of dots rather than squares. board is 11 dots wide and 14 dots deep. A jagged river runs across the middle of the board, forming a critical barrier. Each player gets 11 men, two tanks and two anti-tank guns, as well as a pontoon bridge. A player has a choice on each move: either place a piece onto the friendly side of the board, or move a piece that is already there.
rules: Pieces can move as far as they wish forward, backward or sideways, but not across the river. Once the first piece is placed, others may only be placed adjacent to one already on the board – friendly or enemy. A bridge can be placed vertically or horizontally across a river space. Once placed, it cannot be moved. Pieces may not move into enemy territory except across their own bridge. Infantry capture by surrounding. Guns and tanks kill 2 and 3 spaces away. Eliminate the enemy to win.

Title: Chicago! Chicago!
description: This game by Jim Dunnigan is based on the clashes between protesters and police surrounding the 1968 Democratic presidential nominating convention in Chicago. Anti-war protesters try to disrupt the convention. Police, as Mayor Daley put, try "to preserve disorder".
rules: Demonstrators win points by successfully harassing police. Police win points by dispersing the crowds. Police may also resort to assaulting demonstrators, but if this results in someone being shot, in which case they lose the game. Each type of conflict in an area is resolved on a separate combat results table.

Title: Soldaten Quartette
description: This Quartette deck displays the colorful uniforms still used at the outset of WWI. It includes sets representing Central Powers, Entente and neutral countries. Cards show uniforms of infantry, artillery, cavalry and special units or generals.
rules: Standard Quartette (Authors)

Title: Great European War Game, The
description: This WWI target shooting game comes with targets ranging from elegant officers on horseback to a British Tommy in the trenches. Players use a metal and wood spring-loaded rifle to fire the bullets.
rules: Player to knock down the most targets in an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Child Soldiers
description: This WWII game encourage kids to consider army careers. Each player becomes on of the five children on the lower right and pursues a distinct training path that leads to one of the five service branches: Infantry, Artillery, Signals, Armor, and Air Force. The game was included as a centerfold pull-out in a children’s magazine published by the Asahi Shimbun newspaper chain. The absence of any naval roles suggests that the game was army-sponsored.
rules: This sugoroku includes five player paths. First to complete the chosen path wins the game.

Title: Moi, y'en a vouloir des sous
description: This game is based on a satirical movie that lampoons capitalists and socialists alike. Sparked by the 1968 Paris riots, the hero is an accountant who gets fired, goes to work for his union-leader aunt, persuades the union to invest in a bicycle business that becomes wildly successful and then goes on strike against the union to be allowed to quit as CEO. The cover features well-known revolutionaries, including Lenin reading the Wall Street Journal.
rules: As in Monopoly, players roll to move around the board. They may purchase businesses they land on, but also vie for control of unions that can be used to trigger strikes on opposing businesses. Event cards may provide financial bonuses or penalties as well as symbolic ones: being forced to hold one's fist in the air until the next turn. As always, player with the most money wins.

Title: Jeu de l'Oie (de Gaulle)
description: This Jeu de l’Oie (Game of Goose) features General Charles de Gaulle while telling the story of the French wartime experience. The first space shows the German invasion of 1940 as a jackboot descending on France. This is immediately followed by the promotion of de Gaulle to Général de brigade (a two-star general in the French military). Space 62 shows a tri-colored French cock standing on a Nazi flag followed by Gaulle being handed a bouquet of flowers to mark the dawn of the 4th Republic.
rules: Standard Game of Goose layout. First to reach the end of the 63-space track wins.

Title: Belagerungsspiel (WWI Small)
description: This is a small version with illustrations of Germans advancing on a French fortification. It is most notable for the poignant image on the lower right showing a (presumably surrendered) French soldier giving first aid to a wounded comrade as the Germans advance past him.
rules: Standard Belagerungsspiel.

Title: Gallant Japanese Army
description: The game shows a wide range of Japanese military activities including the work of scouts, railways and shipping, naval bombardment, a beach assault, combat engineers, headquarters, guarding a railway, communicating by motorcycle messenger, carrier pigeon and telegraph, cavalry, anti-aircraft guns, airplanes dogfighting, bombing and strafing, logistics, and tanks and infantry in the assault.
rules: Standard mawari-sugoroku: first player to reach the middle space wins.

Title: Guerre Moderne, La
description: This roll-and-move game was published early in WWI, and shows the full scope of military operations on and over land and sea.
rules: First to reach the final space wins

Title: Army Air Corps Game
description: This game takes players through the three stages of US pilot training during WWII. By the time it was published in 1942, the Army Air Corps had already been expanded to the US Army Air Force. The board has a 38-space track illustrating the many elements of pilot training from the orientation flight and basic flying maneuvers through dogfighting and night flying to gunnery, tactics and precision bombing. The board has a built-in spinner with three rings corresponding to the stages of training.
rules: Players do not move along the track. Rather, they spin in turn and place a wooden block of their color on the corresponding space on the track. They use the inner red ring until all red spaces have been filled, then the middle white ring until that is filled, and finally the blue ring. The first player to fill all the spaces wins. Duplicate spins are wasted, but there are opportunities at each stage to fill in missing spaces without spinning the number.

Title: Belligérants, Les
description: This brightly colored board represents a variety of terrain features: brown (normal), blue (water), green (forest), grey (dangerous), white (hospital), and black (annihilation), with various effects. Pieces are wooden pawns of varying height, with identity symbol on top and value on the bottom, from soldat (100) through HQ (1000). Some have special powers vs. others. Movement is by a die numbered 1-5, with a devil stamped on the sixth side.
rules: The red and yellow players set up in opposite corners of the board. In turn, they roll the die and move a single piece that many spaces in a straight line in any direction. Spaces must be empty. Ending on an enemy piece of lesser value captures it. The "devil" allows a player to choose 1-5, to take over and use many enemy pieces (e.g. planes, tanks, guns) and rescue friendly units. Game ends when one player's army has been eliminated.

Title: Air Combat Trainer
description: The youth arm of the National Aeronautic Association aimed to teach kids how to fly fighters and bombers. The playing board was an open sky showing both horizontal distance and altitude, with a slightly curved horizon. Cardboard pieces show friendly and enemy planes by model. Player aids included mission spinners for both the attacker and defender, and a triangle to calculate release points for both high-level and dive bombing.
rules: First player to complete the assigned mission wins, whether on defense or offense.

Title: Dave Dawson Victory Game
description: This game is based on the War Adventure Series of books about the escapades of Dave Dawson and his sidekick Freddie Farmer. Players follow one of four movement tracks across a map of Europe. Each represents a different service (Army, Navy, Air Force or Marines) but the risks and encounters are identical.
rules: The game has an unusual mechanism for determining moves. Instead of rolling a die, players flick a checker up a scale on one side of the board, which must bounce back. The number it lands on determines how many spaces the player’s piece advances. Red spaces send players back two spaces. Encounter spaces flip cards for minor victory, major defeat, enemy defeat and counter-attack. First to reach the Victory space wins.

Title: Empires
description: Empires is played on a world map, with up to six players all starting in the middle of the Atlantic Ocean. The six Powers are the United States, Great Britain, France, Russia, Italy and Japan. Parts include 6 pawns representing each country, 20 Markers for each player, 48 resource cards and 48 armament cards with values of 1 or 5 representing Air, Navy and Army units. The map includes starred territories, land area colonies with a value from 3 to 6, and blockade spaces.
rules: Players move from point to point around the map. When moving into a vacant space, they gain one resource for a territory, and place a marker to take control of a colony and get resources equal to its value. Resources are converted into armaments at 3 for 1, with armament cards drawn randomly. Once all colonies are taken a colonial war begins. As players land on opposing colonies, they choose an armament class and attack. Highest strength wins the battle and takes the colony. The loser gives up resources equal to the colony's value. Once all armament cards have been drawn, the first player back to start triggers a winner-take-all battle based on total armament values.

Title: Air Defense (Target)
description: This metal target game uses a spring-loaded coastal defense gun that fires small silver balls. The targets are five planes mounted on the back stand. They flip over to show a parachute with a point value when hit. The point values are 200 (center), 300 (near right/left) and 500 (edges).
rules: Player scoring the most points after an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Over There
description: This game is focused on patrolling in No Man's Land. The box presents a rather idyllic view of life in the field for American forces, but it is very much a game of fighting in the mud.
rules: The board has five lines of advance. Each player has one piece per line. Players spin and move any one piece that exact number, forward or backward, not both. They capture an enemy by landing on it by exact count. Each line has one or two shell holes. A piece in a shell hole cannot be captured. Capturing an enemy piece ends the game on that line. The capturing player can move his piece to any other line. The first player to capture all of the opposing pieces wins the game.

Title: Flying the Beam
description: While not involving combat, this game was designed by a pilot to simulate "blind flying" conditions. At the time, a transmitter near the airport would send out a radio buzz indicating the correct landing track. Veering left or right of the lane would trigger the Morse code letters A or N. When on track and near the airport, the signal would fade altogether. For wartime production, the planes were made of a wood-based cellulose composite.
rules: Players choose an approach lane, and each turn make a spin that determines their move. When they successfully enter the "cone of silence" around the airport, they roll for landing instructions. First plane on the ground wins.

Title: Flagship
description: This variant of Checkers is played on a 9 x 9 board, and pieces are placed and move only on the dark spaces. Each player’s home rows therefore have five available spaces in the back rank and four in the second rank. Each player gets seven destroyers, one flagship, one aircraft carrier and wooden rings to turn destroyers into flagships on reaching the enemy back row.
rules: The flagship moves like a checkers king. It moves one space in any diagonal direction. It takes an adjacent enemy piece by jumping over it to an empty space. Destroyers act like normal checkers (forward only), gaining flagship powers if they reach the final rank. The game has small wooden rings that get put onto the destroyer pawns to show their new status. The aircraft carrier may move as many spaces in a line as it wishes and captures by jumping over to a space immediately behind the enemy.

Title: Victory in Europe?
description: This unnamed board shows American troops in action on the Western front, from embarkation for transport across the Atlantic to the heroic return home. Notable technologies shown are the Waco glider and the Motorola "handie-talkie".
rules: Standard roll and move: first to return home at space 60 wins.

Title: Panzerschlacht
description: This is one of the first games to focus on modern tank design and armor tactics. This is one of many chess-type games that emerged in the late 1930s, but it has only two kinds of playing pieces: light tanks and heavy tanks. The board is elongated. It measures only 12 spaces wide, but 20 deep. Each player starts with eight heavy tanks in the back row and 10 light tanks in the second row.
rules: Both kinds of tanks only move diagonally, forward or backward. However, they shoot and kill enemy pieces only directly forward (across the alternating squares). A light tank moves up to six spaces, and can then kill an enemy tank up to three spaces away. A heavy tank has a maximum move of four spaces, but also can shoot an enemy up to four spaces away. The dark green barriers represent tank traps and other barriers. Playing pieces cannot move onto or through them but can shoot over them.

Title: Jutlandia
description: This naval game features metal ships. Each side has a flagship and two other battleships, one aircraft carrier, 4 cruisers, 5 destroyers, 2 submarines and 2 squadrons of aircraft, along with coastal batteries, a fort and a base flag. The checkerboard playing surface is 9 squares wide by 8 deep, with a 2-deep home base with rows of 5 and 4 spaces.
rules: Not available

Title: Benedicto, or Beat the Blitz
description: The name of the game comes from its publisher, the makers of Benedict Peas, who then added the alternate title "Beat the Blitz". The board is colorful, but the game's most interesting feature is the ad-filled back of the envelope that contains it. Its four-verse ditty boasts of the company's long reliance on British peas, a big plus now that "the war has come - and foreign peas, No longer fill our docks and quays.
rules: The game is described on its envelope as a thrilling race against air raids, time bombs, traffic jams, tank traps and diversions. However, it actually is a very ordinary roll and move from the pea patch through the factory and into the home. First player to reach the home wins.

Title: Bomb the Navy
description: This simple target game uses cardboard ships as targets and rolled marbles as projectiles.
rules: Most targets down in an agreed number of rolls wins.

Title: Combat (WWII)
description: This Battleships variant was published shortly after Pearl Harbor. The standard 10 x 10 grid is superimposed on a map of the Pacific theater running from the US west coast to Japan, the Philippines and Australia. However, the locations on the map have no bearing on the game.
rules: Standard single-shot Battleships rules. The forces are weaker than usual. Each player gets only 1 aircraft carrier (4 boxes), one bomber (3) and two scout planes (2).

Title: Marsch den Luftschutzraum
description:
As in Britain during the Blitz, this game was intended to teach German children how to protect themselves from bombing raids.
The cover illustration is grim. Two weary nurses carrying a stretcher into the shelter. A scared mother with her infant in arms follows them down the stairs. A civil defense worker in a gas mask sprays water on a hot spot. The aggressive and optimistic tone of early war German games is gone.
rules: Standard roll and move. Good and bad civil defense practices are reflected in bonus and penalty spaces.  For instance, those who read The Siren magazine (space 11) have the latest information on air protection and move ahead. Leaving clutter, failing to help out or forgetting to keep pails of water handy penalize players. First player to reach space 70 wins, but must immediately shout “Air Raid Protection is Necessary!” or be sent back to space 60.

Title: Reichskarte
description: This 32-card Skat deck was produced by German tobacco company Yenidze. The face cards reflect a who’s who of the empire’s wartime leadership. The King Cards feature portraits of Kaiser Wilhelm II, Crown Prince Wilhelm of Prussia, Crown Prince Ruprecht of Bavaria and Grand Duke Albrecht of Wurttemberg. Others include Kaiser Wilhelm’s wife Auguste Victoria, Chancellor Theobald von Bethmann-Hollweg, General Paul von Hindenburg, Grand Admiral Alfred von Tirpitz and Ferdinand, Graf von Zeppelin.
rules: Standard Skat rules.

Title: Hallo "Uberfall!
description: This Nazi-era version of Belagerungsspiel is titled "Hello - Raid!" and features images of a quick and successful police response to a bank robbery.
rules: Standard Belagerungsspiel

Title: Falklands War, The
description: This game's map uses a standard hexagonal grid, 36 spaces by 30. It covers May 1 to June 29 in 30 turns, two days per turn. Land units have anti-aircraft as well as ground combat and movement factors. Air and naval units have different attack factors for anti-air, surface and anti-submarine combat, as well as a defense factor and either movement (ships) or range (aircraft).
rules: Combat occurs in sequential rounds: Air to Air and Surface to Air; then Air to Surface; Surface to Surface and Submarine; Naval Bombardment; Ground to Ground. Game is played in three phases of variable length from initial blockade to resolution. Phase advances on a D6 rolled each turn. A move into Crisis Resolved ends the game. Players get points for enemy units destroyed and for occupying the key locations in the islands.

Title: War in the Falklands
description: This is a fairly small game with only 150 counters, but it covers the actions of all the land, sea and air units involved. The map is divided into 14 areas. All the ships involved have individual named counters. Air units are identified by type. British units consist of Harrier jump jets, Vulcan long-range bombers and Sea King helicopters. Argentinian air forces include Mirage and (Israeli-made) Knesher fighters, and Super Étendard and Skyhawk attack aircraft.
rules: The rules cover air to air, air to sea, air to land, ship to ship and land to land combat, which are resolved each turn in that order. Combat values   range from to 12, most range 1 but circled (missiles) range 2. Players must roll equal to or higher than combat value on 2D6 to hit. The game is played for a maximum of 12 turns. Players get Prestige (VPs) for areas controlled each turn and for sinking opposing ships.

Title: Grossdeutschlands Stadtespiel
description: The gameboard shows a map of greater Germany after the 1939 conquest of Poland. The rules booklet has the names of 30 cities within the country with a short rhyme; the board shows only a coat-of-arms beside each city. The names and rhymes are read out before the game starts. The game comes with a large number of letter tiles, mostly black with 30 red ones. The reds represent the starting letter of a city name.
rules: All the letter tiles are placed face down. Players take turns drawing a single tile and placing it in front of them. As soon as a player draws a red tile, he places it face up and then adds as many of his black tiles to the city name as possible. Other players then have the opportunity to add as well. The winner is the first player to be able to get rid of tiles in hand after the last tile is drawn.

Title: Carrying the Tools to Britain
description:
Canada's national broadcaster put this out in 1942 to show Canadians just how much they were supporting the British war effort.
The map is full of symbols showing what was produced where, and the back of the fold-out board has lots more info about both the growth of the Canadian forces (from 15 fighting ships to 300) and goods sent across the Atlantic, from 300 million bushels of wheat to 13 million pounds of honey.
rules: Players start on Canada's West Coast, and the first to cross the country and then the Atlantic to London wins. However, there are multiple routes that branch and intersect. Landing on intersections or some red dots requires taking detours or following specific routes.

Title: War in Europe, The
description: This Japanese view of WWI in the West features a vivid stylized map of the European continent. The game can be played by two players or teams of up to three players each. The game provides six cut-out counters at the bottom, with Germany, Austria and Bulgaria representing the Central Powers and Russia, France and Britain the Allies.
rules:
The Central Powers start top left and Allies bottom right. Each has three possible routes across the board. The air route is shortest but has the most hazards; the land longest but safest. Once a piece arrives at its objective (Brussels or Hamburg), it must try to enter the enemy fort to win the game. It succeeds on a roll of 1 or 6. Failure sends it to red hazard spaces that are hard to escape.



The goal is to get all three pieces of one side to the starting fort of the opponents.

Title: On Guard
description: This is a simple roll-and-move game for 2-4 players. One player acts as the Guard, while the others try to complete a reconnaissance of the enemy position by moving along their individual tracks. If a guard lands on a recon player, that player becomes the guard, and the guard becomes a recon player, starting from the beginning. Movement for all players is by a 1-4 spinner.
rules: First player to complete a reconnaissance loop wins.

Title: Camouflage, The Game of
description: The 56-card deck consists of assorted weapons of war. There are five each of cards illustrating an Airplane Gun, a rhomboid Tank, three kinds of artillery (Mortar, Howitzer and Field Gun) and four kinds of infantry weapons (Machine Gun, Rifle, Revolver and Bomb, meaning hand grenade).The deck also includes 11 Camouflage cards.
rules: One card is dealt face up to each player, plus one to dealer's left. This player spins and "shoots at" the player identified. The shooting player takes all defender's cards unless covered by a Camouflage. Play moves to the left. A new player dealt a Camouflage card gets protection, but loses his spin. A player dealt a Howitzer card takes all of the other stacks not camouflaged. The player with the most cards when the deck runs out gets 10 points. Winner is first to 50 points.

Title: Historiesch Spel van Waterloo
description:
This is the earliest game to show specific Napoleonic battles. The spiral track traces Napoleon's later campaigns and has spaces showing the burning of Moscow in 1812, Napoleon's exile to and escape from Elba, and his final battle at Waterloo in 1815. It was published in the Netherlands just one year after Waterloo, and the central illustration shows one of the results: the marriage of the Prince of Orange (later King William II of Netherlands) to Anna Pavlovna, sister of Tsar Alexander I. The marriage, which took place on
on February 21, 1816, at the Chapel of the Winter Palace in St Petersburg, was a symbol of the alliance created after the Congress of Vienna.
rules: First player to reach the central space wins.

Title: Jagd auf Kohlenklau
description: Players move along a track that shows both wasteful and beneficial actions to support wartime production.

Title: Voennaya Taina (Военная Таина)
description: This cartoon game aimed at 7-9 year olds features the son of a peasant farmer battling “bourgeoisie” powers represented by caricatures of Germany, Japan and the United States (General Douglas Macarthur with pipe and cane): all just errand boys for a fat banker. The boy is entrusted with a military secret, captured, tortured and executed for refusing to talk. This inspires the triumph of Communism.
rules: Standard roll and move: first player to reach the end of the 32-space track wins.

Title: Unsere U-Boote Neuestes
description: This game has pieces stamped with the actual numbers of six of Germany's highest scoring U-boats by 1916. U-21, for instance, was the first submarine ever to sink a ship with a self-propelled torpedo when it downed British cruiser Pathfinder on Sept. 5. 1914. The game also is remarkable for fine artwork on both cover and board. The game board also reflects many historical U-boat victories, including U-9's sinking of the "Live Bait Squadron" (cruisers Cressy, Aboukir and Hogue) on Sept. 22, 1914.
rules: Players roll and move along a 100-space track, from Helgoland around the UK and Ireland and back home. There are 20 English flag stickers wrapped around pins, and these are placed on 18 black spaces along the track. Each represents a historical sinking of a British ship. The three pins placed on space 6 represent the Live Bait Squadron sunk on Sept. 22, 1914. The first player to land on a black space collects its flag, and the winner is the one who returns home with the most flags.

Title: Russisch-Japanische Krieg, Der
description: Metal soldiers and ships are featured in this German game about the Russo-Japanese war. They move along a series of adjacent tracks intended to reflect both the ground and naval aspects of the war.
rules: First to reach the end of the track wins.

Title: Kriegs-spiel
description: This is one of several games published in Germany after its triumph in the 1870-71 Franco-Prussian War. It features vivid illustrations of close-quarters combat among infantry, cavalry and lancers. The back of the game sheet has a version of Belagerungsspiel themed on the same war. An image of Prussian infantry and jagers (green jackets) burning a French town is especially striking.
rules: This is a relatively complex gambling game. Full rules to be added.

Title: Canards du Camp, Les
description: C. Brunlet, a French prisoner at a camp in Senlis, designed this game based on his experiences and published it in Switzerland after escaping.  The title is a pun: "Canards" is both a pun on the Game of Goose and a word for the endless rumours that circulate among captives. It revels in the absurdities of camp life and comes with a booklet explaining the stories behind the images.
rules: Standard Game of Goose: first to the end of the 63-space inward spiral track wins.

Title: Jungdeutschlands Schlachtenspiel
description: This modular board game allows players to recreate both tactical exercises and historical battles. The boards are white with a square grid. To set up, players place wooden terrain features (houses, water, woods and forts) and deploy troops according to the scenario. The historical battles portrayed range from the Seven Years' War to the Franco-Prussian War. Diagram and set-up for the 1870 battle of Gravelotte are shown here.
rules: Players alternate moving a single piece. This may move in any direction including diagonal by one space, or multiple spaces by jumping over an adjoining piece to an empty square. Multiple jumps are possible in a single move. Players may jump over friendly or enemy pieces, with enemies being removed. Battle continues until one force has been wiped out or surrenders.

Title: Feldherrnspiel, Das (Blocks)
description: This is a German version of L'Attaque featuring WWII-era weapons and units. The unit identities are printed on labels glued to the slanted side of each wooden piece. The opposite vertical side faces the enemy.
rules: Standard L'Attaque rules, with hidden set-up, alternating movement and challenge, combat resolved by eliminating the weaker piece (both if tied).

Title: Sixth Fleet
description: This simulation of potential naval warfare is set in the Caribbean. US and Soviet players control surface ships, submarines and air units, and try to locate and destroy opposing units while keeping their own units alive.
rules: Hex movement and combat. Each turn has separate sub, surface and air phases, but players may take these phases in any order.

Title: Commonwealth Navy
description: This is a naval target-practice game. The target ship is in the middle of the board, with 11 spaces numbered from 1 to 5. The metal playing pieces are ships that move around the edge, taking a shot from each of the 32 spaces with colored dots or red-bordered squares around the rim.
rules: A player's ship starts on one of the colored dots, and moves one space clockwise each turn. Each player then spins the teetotem and counts that number of spaces following the inward arrow from that space. If the result is a space with a number, the player score that value. When all players have completed the circuit, the player with the highest score wins.

Title: Squadron Insignia
description: The deck of this game features the logos of early-war American naval air squadrons. These include the squadrons based on the carriers Enterprise, Yorktown, Saratoga, Wasp and Ranger.
rules: Similar to Happy Families/Authors

Title: Battle Checkers
description: This game celebrates the dawn of a formal global alliance against the Axis in the Jan. 1 Declaration by United Nations at the Arcadia Conference. The box cover shows the flags of alliance members spread across a map of the world. The squashed-diamond board has the names of friendly and enemy nations as starting spaces. Pieces are plastic infantry, guns, tanks, submarines, ships, fighters and bombers.
rules: Checkers-type move and jump rules. Allied pieces start on the U.S., U.K., Russia, China, Canada, Australia, Greece and the Netherlands Indies. Axis pieces start on Germany, Italy, Japan, Austria, Hungary, Rumania, Bulgaria, Finland and Indo-China. Infantry, tanks and guns move and jump forward only. Tanks do the same in any direction. Submarines, battleships and Flying Fortresses move one, but can jump 2, 3 and unlimited spaces to capture. Win is by elimination of all enemy pieces.

Title: L'Empire Francais
description: This card game from Paris publisher Miro (which later published La Conquête du Monde, the first edition of what became Risk) offers another take on the extent of the French Empire at the outbreak of WWII. The box includes a quote from then prime minister Edouard Daladier's speech in Algiers on Jan. 6, 1939, in which he declared that France's colonial activities were always based on love, and that it acts only to "bring people into the light".
rules: It is a set-collection game, with sets comprising one card with a map and main exports of a colony and the others bearing photographs and key statistics. Colonies in the deck include: Indochina, Tahiti, New Caledonia, New Hebrides, La Réunion and its enclaves in India in Asia and Oceania ; Syria and Lebanon in the Middle East; Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Cameroon, Togo, French Somalia, French Equatorial Africa (Congo), French West Africa (Ivory Coast and Mauritania) and Madagascar in Africa; and St. Pierre and Miquelon, Martinique, Guadeloupe, and French Guiana in the Americas.

Title: Gloria-Victoria
description: This was a training game published early in WWII by the German army. The board was bland and the pieces cardboard, but adapts chess-type movement to a game themed on tactical warfare. The title comes from a popular marching song, and the cover image shows happy troopers heading for the fight.
rules: This chess-style game has pieces representing infantry, machine gunners, storm troopers, panzers (tanks) and artillery, supported by a fighter plane, a bomber and a flak (anti-aircraft) gun. Each piece has different powers of movement and attack. The board is an 8 x 10 grid, half in blue and half in red. The only terrain features are a shell hole and a downed bridge on each half, both of which block movement.

Title: Feldherrn-Spiel, Das (Cards)
description: Uniforms and military strength of the 8 major combatants in WWI are featured in this card game. The deck is divided into 8 sets of 5, with each national set including infantry, navy, artillery, air force and specialty units (such as Russian Cossacks and British Highlanders). Each card also lists an aspect of that country's military strength.
rules: Standard Authors.

Title: Bomber Greifen an!
description: The box has spectacular art. The board is an aerial view of countryside, marked with a square grid, 18 wide by 26 deep. The middle four rows are shaded darker, representing the front lines. The game pieces in this copy are wooden: biplane bombers; high-wing monoplane fighters; and guns with a barrel mounted on a block. They are very roughly carved and glued together.  The game is not dated and may have been published after the end of the war.
rules: Each player gets six bombers, six fighters, six anti-aircraft guns and one “Long Max” artillery piece. This was a very large (38 cm) gun originally intended for use in battleships, but that was adapted for use in WWI as a heavy siege or coastal defense gun on land. The planes start in irregular airfields in opposing corners. Guns can be placed anywhere but cannot move. Players then take turns moving a piece and either shooting or bombing.

Title: Trap a Sap
description:
This small dexterity game comes in a glass-topped box. The background art shows infantry and a tank at the bottom, while part of a battleship is seen in the middle, with two burning Japanese planes overhead. This clearly references the kamikaze threat facing American ships as they moved ever closer to Japan.

​
rules: Players try to juggle three beans through a small hole into a cardboard enclosure.

Title: V Ataku! (В Атаку!)
description: This game for high-school students has a 15 x 20 grid across a board with generic landscape. There are three key features: a red-bordered six-space headquarters that must be captured, a river across the middle that slows movement, and four yellow squares on each side that allow players to place reserves that cannot be attacked. Each side starts with 21 pieces: 8 infantry, 4 tanks, 4 artillery, 3 missiles and 2 airplanes. An illustrated divider allows hidden set-up.
rules: As in chess, each type of piece moves differently and destroys an enemy piece by moving onto its square. All moves are orthogonal. The first player makes a single move; his opponent makes two; the first player makes two; then the opponent makes three. Thereafter each player's turn includes three moves, enabling a variety of assaults, probes and counter-attacks. Destroy all opposing forces to win.

Title: World War 3
description: This game simulates a possible global but conventional war between the United States and the Soviet Union starting in the late 1970s. The map is global, hexes are 500 miles/800 km wide and units are armies and fleets.
rules: Standard hex movement and combat with combat results table.

Title: Trench Football
description: This dexterity maze combines trench warfare with Britain's favourite game: football (or soccer in North American terms). Each of the enemy "players" is a caricature of a notable German leader. A brown trenchlike maze winds its way around the green board. Players juggle a small metal ball past each member of this German team all the way to the Kaiser Wilhelm goal mouth. The box is wooden with a glass top.
rules: Player to reach the goal in the least time wins.

Title: Belagerungsspiel (1935 Panzer)
description: Shortly after Hitler took power, this board shows prototype dive bombers, early panzers and a flamethrower in action. The tank commander is shown still using flags as signalling devices, rather than the radios which became standard features in German armor by wartime.
rules: Standard Belagerungsspiel (24 attackers, 2 defenders)

Title: Achtung! Feind Hört Mit!
description: As the rules put it: "In this game you have to track down a traitor who is about to escape across the border with his briefcase containing important information about our fortifications." One player is the spy; the others take pieces representing the army, air force, police, border guards, workers and Hitler Youth. The two-section board is a map of a town with streets connecting 21 circular spaces.
rules: The spy starts in the hotel at lower right and wins by reaching the border post at upper left. The other pieces start at various locations around town. Each piece moves to an adjacent space in turn, starting with the spy. Each piece must move unless completely blocked. The other players must cooperate in surrounding the spy so that he cannot move, but the player who closes the trap wins the game.

Title: After the Holocaust
description: This game shows the United States struggling to recover 20 years after a nuclear war. The game combines military, economic and political strategy, as players try to rebuild from the ruins.
rules: The rules make it difficult for players to survive to the end of the game, much less win. The game last 10 turns, and each turn has five phases: Production; Trade; Consumption; Political/Military; and Finance.

Title: Trap the Jap in Tokyo
description:
This small box (only 4.5 inches/11.4 cm square) illustrates the Allies’ big-picture strategy in the Pacific. Both the game’s lid and the bottom of the box shows a map covering the entire war zone from eastern India to Alaska and Australia. 
The board  (below right) has a total of nine indentations: four white circles, four blue circles and one red, and the glass-covered box has small marbles in matching colors. 

​
rules: Winner is the player who can get all the balls into the correct holes in the least time.

Title: White Squadron, The
description: First published in 1896 and as late as 1919, this card game celebrates America's growing naval power. Each card shows an individual ship with a short description. The deck includes many of the vessels that played key roles during the Spanish-American War, including six from Admiral Dewey's Asiatic Squadron and eight that were present for the Battle of Santiago de Cuba.
rules: Authors

Title: L'Aero Navale
description: This boxed Belgian version of Battleships was published on the eve of WWII. As the box opens, an illustrated screen pops up between the two players. The grid is an unusual 15 spaces wide and only 6 deep. In addition to the ships, there are airplanes and minefields.
rules: Battleships variant

Title: Atomic Submarine: Up Scope
description: Cardboard ships on wooden stands are set up on a triangular board. There is a heavy wooden periscope mechanism that also triggers the launch of spring-loaded torpedoes.
rules: Most hits wins.

Title: Air-Attack
description: This may be the earliest war game with an odds-based combat result table. Each player gets 6 planes, which can be used to bomb enemy objectives or fight enemy planes. Each player has three 10-space objectives. Before play, each player inserts six black anti-aircraft pegs and 10 target pegs into a secret objective card. Players then spin to move, using the outer 2-12 ring for movement and the inner 1-8 for combat. Movement points can be divided as desired between planes and in any direction.
rules: Players bomb by ending movement on an objective space, scoring a point if it is a target space, and losing a plane if an AA space. Combat occurs whenever enemy planes come adjacent. Each player can count all planes adjacent to the one triggering combat, with total number on each side determining the column on the CRT. Score 1 point per target bombed, 2 points per enemy plane downed, 10 points for eliminating all enemy planes.

Title: Admiral Pampero
description: This is a game of maneuver for up to five players, representing Germany, England, France, America and Italy. The sea areas represented on the map include not just the Baltic and North Seas, but also the Mediterranean and Red Seas, the North and South Atlantic Oceans, and the Pacific Ocean as far as Samoa, and a very squished Indian Ocean leading to Australia.
rules: Number of playing pieces is asymmetric: 30 each for Germany and England, 25 each for France and Amerika, and only 10 for Italy. Rules booklet is visible in German museum collection, but not legible.

Title: Electronic Intercept
description: In this battery-powered game, one player tries to reach and bomb the enemy base within 10 turns; the other player tries to shoot down the attacker. The plastic play unit has a board that can be lit from below, and controls for the attacker on one end: twisting the knob to move forward or backward, sliding to move sideways. The defender 4 SAM sites that can be placed anywhere at start, and places a red plastic interceptor on the board each turn. Sounds and lights add to the action.
rules: After initial placement, the Attack Jet may secretly move up to 4 squares in any combination of directions, counted orthogonally. The defender then has two chances to place the Interceptor. If placed on the attackers square, it is shot down. The attacker scores 5 points for each row advanced and 100 for reaching the enemy base. Players change roles and repeat for as many rounds as desired.

Title: Beleirings-spil
description: This is a Danish version of the German Belagerungsspiel.
rules: Standard Belagerungsspiel (24 attackers, 2 defenders)

Title: Our Defenders
description: This WWII morale booster is a competitive jigsaw. The game has pieces that form images of individuals from different arms. There also is a spinner inset in the box surrounded by the same images. Each player chooses an image to complete. They then take turns using the spinner, and whoever is working on the puzzle it points to gets to add a piece.
rules: First player to complete their puzzle wins.

Title: Liners and Transports, Game of
description: This re-issue of the 1918 New US Merchant Marine and International Salesman has a different map showing post-WWI changes in boundaries. Most notable is a hazard space marked “Terrific Storm: Total Destruction” just south of the Japanese home islands. Two of the closest cities shown are Nagasaki and Hiroshima.
rules: Players move around the map picking up and delivering cargoes. Player finishing with the most money wins.

Title: A Line in the Sand
description: The map board is divided into irregularly shaped areas rather than a hex grid. There are relatively few counters, and they are larger. Some counters are designed to lie flat; others are cardboard and are folded into stands to hold them upright.
rules: Area movement and combat

Title: War, or Generals and their Battles
description: This card deck has 61 cards are divided into 15 sets of four, plus a wild card. Each set consists of an American General and three of his battles. The general card has a short biography and each battle card includes a brief description. The capture of Burgoyne and his army of 5,791 men on Oct. 17, 1777, for instance, notes: “Among the captives were six members of the British Parliament, 42 pieces of brass artillery, 5,000 muskets and immense quantities of stores.”
rules: Standard set-collection rules as for Happy Families or Authors. The 61st card is named simply “Officer”. It acts as a wild card, allowing a player to call any card from memory to fill a book. It also acts as a tie-breaker, counting as a single book unless the number of books is tied, in which case it counts for 16.

Title: Squadron Scramble
description: This rummy-type card game doubles as an aircraft-recognition training aid, approved by the National Aeronautic Association. The 52-card deck is divided into 16 sets of 3 plus 4 wild cards. Each set shows an individual aircraft from three directions: overhead, side view and front view. The deck includes 2 British, 2 German, 3 Japanese and 9 American planes.
rules: Each player is dealt a hand of 7 cards. They immediately lay down any sets of three in their hands. Then in turn they draw one card from the remaining deck and discard a card. They may lay down sets as they are drawn, but must discard except on their final play. The first player to empty his hand wins the round. The four wild cards can be used in any set, may be taken from a played set by the player with the missing card, and count for penalties if still in hand when the game ends.

Title: Marine, Jeu de la
description: This game lays out a history of the French navy that ends with the 1855 Siege of Sevastopol. Featured individuals include François d'Orléans, Prince de Joinville and his 1844 bombardment of Tangiers, and René Duguay-Trouin, who led a major victory over the English at the Battle at the Lizard (1707) during the War of Spanish Succession.
rules: Standard Game of Goose.

Title: Zoom
description: The 36-card deck has images of warplanes from around the world, from late-model biplanes to early-war line aircraft and prototypes such as the Lockheed XP-38 and Boeing XB-15. Each card bears a number from 1 - 9 in corners colored light or dark blue, yellow or red.
rules: For 3-6 players, cards are dealt out face down, then players take turns simultaneously turning over their top cards. If any numbers on the cards match, the first player to call "Zoom" collects the discard piles of all matched players. A miscall of Zoom forfeits the callers discard player to the next correct winner. Player to collect all cards wins. Two-player rules are for standard card game "War".

Title: Oh welche Lust Soldat zu sein!
description: This pre-WWII German recruiting game (Oh what a great soldier you are!) offers a cheery view of the training process from raw recruit to the smiling trained private on the box. Once past the medical inspection, players get uniforms, learn to march, parade, shoot and shine shoes (and may get caught sneaking back into base after a night on the town).
rules: Standard race game: first to the end of the 100-space track wins.

Title: Two-Ocean Navy
description: This brown-paper version of Battleships is named after the Two-Ocean Navy Act passed by Congress after the fall of France in 1940. It authorized massive new spending to expand the U.S. Navy, including construction of 18 aircraft carriers and 15,000 naval airplanes. As a result, the United States began gearing up for the Pacific War long before Pearl Harbor.
rules: Standard Battleships on a 10 x 10 grid.

Title: F.P.1 Antwortet Nicht
description: Floating Platform #1 was a gigantic aircraft carrier in a 1932 movie of the same name, basedon a novel by Kurt Siodmak (better known in the U.S. as the creator of The Wolf Man). Its premise was a floating landing field that would provide a stopover for transatlantic travel, complete with hotels and shopping centers as well as runways and hangars. The pieces features a tiny model of the F.P.1, along with six much larger painted airplanes.
rules: Players try to reach and land on FP1 in order to complete a transatlantic voyage.

Title: Sinai
description: This strategic-level game covers the Arab-Israeli wars of 1956, 1967 and 1973. The mapboard includes all of Israel and the Sinai as well as parts of Syria, Egypt, Jordan and Saudi Arabia.
rules: Hex Combat

Title: Belagerungsspiel (Dice Game)
description: This is a different kind of Siege Game, with a large board showing a fortress marked with various combinations of two dice. The attacker rolls dice to eliminate parts of the defence. The defender does the same trying to eliminate the attacking artillery pieces.
rules: Players roll alternately. They put markers on target spaces that match their die rolls (e.g. 1-6 or 5-5). A roll with no matching open combinations misses. The attacker must hit a larger number of spaces to defeat the fortress, but the defender misses more often, with some combinations useless from the beginning.

Title: L'Attaque (Gibson Deluxe)
description: This is a very early Gibson International Series edition. It also is an example of Gibson's Deluxe version, which includes the pebbled metal backs on the playing pieces that were part of Hermance Edan's first edition in France. This copy is in excellent shape with the metal backs still shiny and the artwork crisp and clear.
rules: Standard L'Attaque

Title: Sturmpioniere
description: A detailed view of the work of combat engineers is provided by this game. The metal playing pieces show various skills: wire-cutting, mine detection and removal, armored assault, flamethrowers and river crossing. There also is a series of illustrations of these activities in boxes that surround the rules printed inside the lid of the game box.
rules: It is a roll and move affair in which each player follows an identical course through their individual triangle of the board toward a central bunker objective. They must pass through a series of obstacles, each of which requires a different pioneer specialist. Each player advances along a track on one of the 4 triangles that form the full board, with a wooden bunker in the middle.

Title: Sahara Patrol
description: The French Foreign Legion takes on North African Arabs in this spin and move game. It has an unusual board with pop-up cardboard forts, and transparent green and pink flags are used to mark possession of locations. The spinner is backed by a waving French flag.
rules: Players start from their respective corners. In turn, they spin and may move one or more pieces by a total number of spaces equal to the number spun. Players capture a fort by landing on it by exact count. They eliminate an opposing piece by landing on it. Once captured, a fort cannot be recaptured and blocks enemy movement along its track. Winner is the player to occupy the most forts.

Title: Three Guardsmen
description: This is a version of the large Belagerungsspiel board themed on the Three Musketeers.
rules: Standard Grosse Belagerungsspiel

Title: Adler Luftverdigungsspiel
description: This complex bombing/air defense game was designed by a Luftwaffe officer and quickly re-issued as Adler Luftwaffenspiel. The map is an overhead featuring targets at both ends, with tracks for fighters and bombers between them. Players get wooden planes, six of each type. They roll a combination of dice for movement, attack and damage control. Bombers must survive fighters, flak and barrage balloons to bomb and return safely.
rules: Bombers move with one black die; fighters with up to 3 white dice. A fighter landing on its matching bomber shoots it down. When a bomber is on one of 6 numbered flak spaces, defender rolls a red die, and shoots it down by matching the number.  A bomber reaching target rolls a multi-color die that determines damage type. Defender gets to roll that die three times to match and repair the damage. Players get points for shooting down planes, bombing and returning safely. Highest point total wins.

Title: America: Kate Smith's Own Game
description: Kate Smith was a huge radio star with CBS during the 1930s and 40s, best known for her rendition of Irving Berlin’s God Bless America, which hit the charts for the first time in 1939. She hosted a series of radio marathons that brought in war bond sales of $600 million (more than $10 billion today).  The inside of the unashamedly patriotic box is arranged to show an American flag, with a sheet of stars over the rules next to player tile racks in bright red and white.
rules: The144 tiles include 48 states (in four regions); 48 state capitols; 30 primary industries; 10 “favorite sons” and 8 American flags (which act as wild cards). Players start with a hand of 12 tiles and draw and discard each turn.  They try to assemble and lay down one of 8 possible combinations. Values range from 5 for ahand with 3 states from each region to 30 for one with three states, each with their capitols, primary industry and favorite son.

Title: Wir fahren und fliegen gegen England
description: This paper game from a German magazine shows both air and naval combat against Britain. Thetrack that wanders across the North Sea to Scotland, down the Irish Sea, along the Channel, over southeastern England and finally home. What makes the game a little unusual for its type is that players cut out and use one of five different playing pieces: a scouting seaplane, dive bomber, fighter, cruiser or U-boat. The 22 red event spaces on the track have a different
rules: First to complete the 100-space track wins.

Title: U-One
description: This post-WWI title features a one-on-one duel between a U-Boat and a merchant ship. It has a four-panel board in heavy card stock showing the Western Approaches (the waters around Southwest England, the French coast and the Channel Islands. There two playing pieces are metal, and take up three squares on the board.
rules: Each player in turn rolls one die and moves that many squares. The count is from the ship's bow and moves are orthogonal, so turning a ship is gradual. The merchant player starts from the western edge and must reach port in either Southampton or Le Havre to win. To sink the ship, the submarine player must have the merchant directly in front of its bow, and then roll a number equal to or greater than the distance between its bow and the nearest square of the ship.

Title: Cargo for Victory
description: This mid-war game focuses on the challenge of moving vital Allied goods by sea in the face of enemy surface ships and submarines. Cargo ships are drawn randomly, with each showing the nature of the cargo, its starting point and its destination. Each player tries to deliver those loads while using his surface ships and submarines both to protect his own cargo ships and to sink enemy ships and submarines.
rules: In turn, ships and submarines may move one space in any direction. However, cargo ships can move faster by jumping friendly ships and submarines as well as enemy cargo ships. A submarine destroys any enemy piece by jumping it. The destroyed piece is removed and set aside for scoring. Ships that reach their destinations also count for scoring. The game ends when the last cargo ship is sunk or reaches its destination.

Title: Right or Wrong?
description: Subtitled "How to Act during an Air Raid", this game was published by the Shemen consumer goods company in Haifa in what is now Israel but was then controlled by Great Britain. Haifa and other cities were bombed multiple times during 1940-41 by Axis planes based on the island of Rhodes.While most of the bombing was done by Italian planes, the game focuses on German aggressors including twin-engine and Stuka dive bombers emblazoned with the Nazi swastika.
rules: Like air raid games published in other countries during World War II, this roll-and-move game illustrates both good and bad practices for avoiding casualties from enemy bombers. Good behaviour illustrated includes preparations such as taping windows, sandbagging entrances and keeping essential tools and supplies handy ahead of time, obeying orders to head to shelters when warning sirens sound, and coping with the damage both during and after the raid. First player to reach the all-clear space (45) wins.

Title: Liberty Game, The
description: Players roll dice to move from the United States to WWI Europe and back. The track meanders across France, Italy, the Balkan States, the Ottoman Empire, Russia, Austria-Hungary, Germany Denmark and England.  Colored dots represent challenges to be overcome.  These include a submarine, battleship, torpedo boat, zeppelin, airplane, infantry regiment, cannon, rapid-fire gun, officers, trenches and forts, all illustrated at the bottom of the board.
rules: Winner is the player to collect the most points by defeating the various challenges. Rolling one of the indicated numbers captures the that objective for a variable number of points. Failure can send a player's piece to one of the four hospitals, marked with red crosses in France, Greece, Russia and England.

Title: Parachute Jump Game
description: A colorful box cover with a game board full of intersecting lines. Players jump from their planes and try to make safe landings on the ground.
rules: Up to four players start at the jump positions at the top. Every time they land on a green intersection space, they must change tracks, to the left or right as appropriate. Touching down on one of three field or land scores points: 50 for 1st down to 10 for 4th. No points for other locations. A player who crosses a red line near the ground without ever landing on green dies, as parachute fails to open. Players make four jumps. Most total points wins.

Title: Jeu de Poilus
description: This game is a quiet tribute to the "poilus", the ordinary soldiers at the core of the French army. The board itself has only a couple of images of war: attacking French troops across the top, and a cameo of a single tank in the lower left corner.  The large gold-lettered label on the deep red back of the board has just the title and an inscription: “Dedicated to the children of France in memory of the Great War”.
rules: Standard roll and move along a 133-space track. Players start as a private second class, gradually getting promoted through 13 ranks and eventually to Marshal of France. Playing pieces also pass through many of the war’s battles: the First Marne in 1914, Yser, Champagne, Somme, Verdun, and Second Marne in 1918. Along the way, players may win medals or be demoted, and more importantly may be wounded, captured or even killed on the field of honor (which forces the player out of the game).

Title: U.S. Army Game, The
description: The 53-card deck is divided into four suits by service: Infantry (blue), Cavalry (yellow), Artillery (red) and Engineers (green). Card values are by rank, from Private (1) to General (12). There also four black (unsuited) cards illustrating military technology: Machine-Gun (5), Armored Motor-car (10), Air-Ship (15) and Tank (20). Finally, there is a President and Commander-in-Chief card.
rules: Cards are dealt evenly. Player to dealer's left leads. Other players must follow suit if possible. Technology cards act as a miniature trump suit, and the Commander-in-Chief card beats all others.

Title: Rough Riders, The
description: The box cover features a very recognizable image of Theodore Roosevelt leading a line-abreast charge up San Juan Hill in Cuba. The game is a simple roll-and-move game. Up to 10 players can start from the Camp at the bottom of the board and race up intersecting winding tracks toward victory at the top of the hill. The game features the first spinner designed to mimic two normal dice, with a 1-6 inner ring and an outer ring with 6 boxes for each inner segment.
rules: First to reach the summit wins.

Title: Fortifications, Le Jeu des
publisher: Gilles Jodelet, sieur de La Boissière
description: The game details the innovations of Sebastien Le Prestre de Vauban, who revolutionized the art of fortification after the end of the Franco-Spanish war in 1659. It was designed by one of his fellow Royal Engineers, Gilles Jodelet, sieur dee La Boissière. Each of the spaces names, describes and illustrates an aspect of his designs. It was dedicated to students at the Royal Military School. This edition by Daumont & Crepy was published in 1712.
rules: The game can be played as a standard race along a 53-space track to the completed fortress in the middle. However, each of the spaces also shows a miniature playing card, and the game was designed to be cut up into a deck that could play standard card games or be used for educational purposes. For example, see the copy of a later game by the same designer, Le Jeu de la Guerre, in the French National Library at https://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/btv1b105274765

Title: Naval War, The Game of
description: This game offers an unusually gruesome graphic on the box, and an even more unusual map on the board. The focus of the board map is on Cuba and its surrounding islands. However, the board also includes a map of the Philippines, plunked to the northeast of Cuba.
rules: Red Player starts in Tortugas and Blue Player in Cadiz. Each gets five ships. A 1-8 spinner determines movement. In turn, each player must move one ship by the exact number shown. A player's ship must land on an opponent by exact count to capture it. Routes shown in red and blue are exclusive to the respective players. Gold routes can be used by both. To win, a player must capture all opposing ships.

Title: Aviation
description: This is an aerial-themed version of L'Attaque. This 1st International Edition features the Bristol fighter, a 2-seater first flown in 1916, but which remained in service into the 1930s. The game then invents a more powerful "Battle Plane", suggesting that size and armament would be more important than agility in controlling the skies. The plain board is 8 spaces wide and 12 deep.
rules: Pieces in vertical stands are set up secretly on each player's side of the board. They have values from 1 (Scout) to 10 (Air Marshall's Squadron). Each turn, a piece moves forward, back or sideways. If it ends facing an enemy piece, the owner may declare an attack. Higher value eliminates lower value. Ties destroy both pieces. Goal is to capture the enemy aerodrome.

Title: Jeu d'Assault
description: This French version of Belagerunggspiel features the 200th Regiment of Infantry. This was one of a dozen new regiments recruited for the 1895 invasion of Madascar. It was 400 men strong when it was formed in Lyon. By the time the fighting ended in September, it had ceased to exist as a fighting force. Of the 15,000 strong invasion force, 25 died in combat and 5,000 died of disease.
rules: Standard Belagerungsspiel

Title: Belagerungsspiel, Grosse (Glass Panzer)
description: This version of a Grosse Belagerungsspiel came with unusual playing pieces: models of Panzer I tanks made out of glass and painted grey on the top and sides.
rules: Standard Grosse Belagerungsspiel

Title: Kohlenklau Quartett
description: This is a lavishly illustrated deck of cards that shows the many ways in which coal contributed to wartime military and civilian needs. There are eight sets of four cards each plus the Kohlenklau (Coal Thief) card. Each set illustrated a theme on the importance of coal: Coal for Fighting; Coal Plastics; Coal Extraction; Coal Processing; Coal Burning; Coal in Industry; Coal Conversion; and "Wonders from Coal" (other uses including medicines, propellants, dyes, candles and soap).

Title: Coast Defense
description: This is a large metal target game using a toy gun with soft tip darts. There are six targets painted around the edges of the board worth 50 - 200 points. Three other targets are pop-outs. When hit, they trigger a plane above them to fall down and reveal a parachute with a value of 1000 points. Hitting the central plane also rings a bell.
rules: Player scoring the most points after an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Echanges France-Colonies, Jeu des
description: This trading game was published while the Germans occupied much of France and Vichy France administered the rest. It sought to raise French spirits by reminding players that France still controlled a vast empire of 24 colonies spanning Africa, Asia and the Americas.
rules: Each of the 2, 4, 6 or 8 players takes an equal number of colonies to start. The goal is to completely exploit one's colonies by providing to each one a ship, an aspect of good government such as a school, hospital, engineers or religious missions, and then collecting and exporting the colony's two main products. Counters are drawn at random by each player in turn and either used for the player's own colonies or traded to another player.

Title: L'Attaque (Int'l Series)
description: This is the first edition published by Gibson after it acquired international rights to the game from inventor Hermance Edan.
rules: Standard L'Attaque

Title: Das U-Boot-Spiel
description: This is a tactical game about U-47's successful raid on the British naval base at Scapa Flow in October 1939. Both the box and the board are rather plain, and the pieces are small wooden blocks and cylinders.
rules: The single attacking submarine must evade multiple defenders to reach the harbour.

Title: Air-Scouts!
description: The cover of this race game features a Bristol Blenheim light bomber in its reconnaissance role, overflying a squadron of warships firing. The board has an unusually long 201-space track, from the home aerodrome to the enemy base and back. There are various bonus and penalty spaces along the way.
rules: Standard roll and move: first player to return to home base wins.

Title: Morskie Manevry (Морские Маневры)
description: This Cold War game has a chess-like board, but with terrain that narrows the field significantly in the middle. Playing pieces are in white and yellow plastic. Each player gets two battleships, four submarines and four torpedo boats.
rules: Each piece has different powers. Destroy all enemy forces to win.

Title: L'Attaque (Five Bridges Edition)
description: This final Gibson edition of L'Attaque has a new board with five bridges across an impassable river between the players. It was not enough to keep the title going as Stratego became the dominant game of this type.
rules: Standard L'Attaque, but with different terrain restricting movement between the two sides of the board.

Title: Dennis Wheatley's Invasion
description: This is caricature of war in Europe, with Angleland, Franken, Danereich and Ruslavia fighting for dominance. The map is a network of dots that abounds with silly place names (e.g. Donner and Blitz, Spit and Polish). The play is complex, involving land, sea and air units.
rules: Players roll 2D6 in turn to see how many pieces they can move, each by one space. An army pushes back an adjacent enemy; two attacking from different spaces create a corps that can only be affected by another corps. Fleets push back similarly, but two fleets within 2D6 spaces of an enemy can eliminate it. Air units attack at range to disable a fort, sink a fleet or capture an enemy air base. To win, a player must occupy all 4 spaces next to an enemy capital.

Title: Nouveau Jeu de la Guerre (?)
description: This unnamed French board is notable for its strong focus (both artistically and in the rules) on the field hospital at lower left as well as the tent with the red cross near the middle. The 1860s saw the foundation of the international Red Cross in Switzerland and its spread to the United States by Clara Barton during the Civil War.
rules: Standard Roll and Move. First to reach the top of the hill at space 99 wins. Landing on a space with H sends a player to hospital until another player arrives there; an A space takes the player to the ambulance. Other red and blue spaces cause payments into and out of the pot which is taken by the winner.

Title: Neues Exerxierspiel
description: This rare military-themed race game comes in a wooden box with wooden pieces. It uses an unusual die with faces are marked: R, G, H, L, K and E, which stand for rechts, gradaus, halt, links, kehrt, eilmarsch, meaning right, forward, stop, left, turn around, hurry. This die determines which direction each player's soldier must move. The game clearly was published after the Crimean War, as the bottom of the box has a regular Belagerungsspiel themed on the siege of Sevastopol.
rules: Each player puts money in the pot, and then roll the die in turn. Rolling R or L means moving up a green line to the right or left. G means moving one ahead on a red line, and E means two spots forward. H means stay in place, and K forces the player's piece back by one along a green line. There is an odd payout. Whichever player first reaches an uppermost red point gets a quarter of the pot (25%); second gets half of the remainder (37.5%), third three quarters of what's left (28%) and last the remainder 9.5%).

Title: Gliding
description: This game shows people enjoying the new sport of gliding, a technology that was to find important wartime uses. Up to six players move colored metal gliders set on stands.
rules: First to the end of the 80-space track wins.

Title: War of 1812
description: This game and Quebec 1759 moved the idea of hidden set-up to historical battles and wars, and by introducing the element of luck to combat resolution. Instead of stand-up cardboard pieces with fixed values, each piece is a wooden block, with one large side blank (facing the opponent) and the other showing the unit's identity and strength. Strength varies from 1 - 4, and the current value is shown by the number of pips at the top of the piece.
rules: Players take turns moving a single group of pieces from one point to an adjacent point. When opposing pieces occupy the same point, a battle ensues. Each player rolls a number of dice equal to his strength points, and eliminates an enemy strength point for each roll of a 6. Either side may retreat rather than firing, ending the battle. Otherwise it continues until one side has been eliminated.

Title: Jimmie Allen's Air Battles
description: This paper version of Battleships was published as a promotional item by The Skelly Oil Co. Jimmie Allen was the star of a 1930s radio serial called The Air Adventures of Jimmie Allen, which was sponsored by Skelly Oil. Instead of ships, the five targets in this game are a scout plane (2 squares long), fighter (3), bomber (4), observation balloon (5) and dirigible (6).
rules: Standard Battleships but with 4 simultaneous shots per player.

Title: Kampf gegen Russland
description: The game box shows a German assault as it reaches the Russian trench line. The board offers an aerial view of the fighting, including artillery duels, cavalry probes, airships, rail cars, bridge assaults and the never-ending task of digging trenches. The playing pieces are cardboard disks, 50 per army: one Commander-in-Chief, two Generals, 3 Officers, 1 Flagbearer, 19 infantry, 11 cavalry troops, 1 drummer, 1 trumpeter, 4 artillery, 2 machine guns, 1 “warcar”, 1 baggage wagon and 3 air units.
rules: The game is more about dexterity than strategy. Each player uses a wooden stick with a flat end to try to “flip” his pieces so that they land at least in part on top of an enemy piece. The enemy piece is then eliminated. Any disk landing in the water is lost. A disk can only cross the river via one of the two bridges. A player wins by eliminating the enemy.

Title: Invasion America
description: This large wargame is a hypothetical invasion of the United States and Canada by three fictional forces: European Socialist Coalition, South America Union and Pan Asiatic League. The game map covers all of North America with each hex about 80 miles/130 km wide.
rules: Hex move and combat.

Title: Soldier Boy, Game of
description: The box shows a very young lad carrying an American flag into battle. The board has an inward spiral running from enlistment through the many ranks to Commander in Chief. Corner illustrations show troops in training and in battle, as well as an "enemy prison camp". Spaces along the way trigger penalties and bonuses.
rules: First player to reach the end of the track wins.

Title: East is Red, The
