description: This game simulates a hypothetical invasion of Manchuria and northern China by the Soviet Union during the 1970s. There are scenarios for both a hasty attack and a well-planned full invasion.
rules: Hex movement and combat.

Title: Bombers Aloft
description: This game is a three-dimensional anti-aircraft shooter using a multi-purpose spinner. The bombers move counter-clockwise around a circular board with 30 spokes. In turn, each bomber makes three spins. The first determines which of the four lanes it will move to. The second determines its new altitude, marked by sticking the nose of the bomber into a metal stand at the appropriate height. And the third sets the number of spaces it moves forward. Many spaces are marked with numbers from 1 to 14, increasing with the length of the journey.
rules: After a player's bomber is moved, he gets a shot with the AA gun by choosing a spoke (from 1 to 30) and making two spins. These show its aiming point by lane and altitude. If the result matches a bomber's location, it is shot down, and the shooting player scores points based on the value in that space. The game ends when all bombers either have been shot down or have reached the finish line. The player with the most points wins.

Title: War of Nations
description: This early-war American game shows a naive view of WWI realities, with German cavalry charging British artillery with the gunners still in red uniforms. Only a few strings of barbed wire, gutted houses and early aircraft are signs of the brutal trench warfare to come. The board, however, shows a strategic view of the Western Front, running from England and France to Germany and Austria-Hungary. There are five lines with movement points, each connecting an Allied city with a Central Powers one.
rules: One army piece starts on each city. In turn, players use a spinner and advance any one piece by the exact number. Starred spaces are forts: landing on a friendly costs a turn; landing on an enemy one grants an extra spin. Landing right in front of an enemy army pushes it back 3; on top of it, captures it. Armies may not move beyond an enemy piece. An army capturing its opponent can go another line where a friendly army has been captured. Winner is first to eliminate all opposing pieces.

Title: Sea Raider
description: This game is about the dangers posed in the Atlantic by Germany's surface raiders and recreates the kind of chase that occurred when the Bismarck burst into the North Atlantic in 1941.. The board has a web of lines connecting dots. Some wavy lines are usable only by the Raider player.
rules: The raider piece starts at the entrance to the open sea and spins first. The cruisers start six spaces behind. each one spins and moves before the raider's next turn. A piece can start in any direction but must move the full number of spaces spun without reversing course.  The raider wins by reaching Home Base. The cruisers win if any one of them lands by exact count on the raider.

Title: Welthandel
description:
Players move their ships along tracks around the world to pick up and deliver cargoes.

The pre-war first edition included a Nazi flag among those on the box cover. The post-war version shown here has replaced the red flag's white/swastika center with a yellow center.
rules: Players aim to land at Ports, where they get a card for the Port and one for its export commodity. The game ends when any player returns to home port. That player gets a bonus of 10,000 points. Players then add up the value of their commodity cards plus 2,000 for each port card. Player with the highest point total wins.

Title: Klar zum Gefecht!
description: This WWII re-issue of WWI's Deutsch-Englischer Seekrieg has metal rather than wooden pieces. The mix of pieces remains the same, with each player getting a 22-ship fleet made up of a single flagship, three cruisers, six destroyers, 10 torpedo boats and two U-boats.
rules: Starting positions are in opposing corners, as in Halma. However, movement and range for attacks vary with the piece. The player who eliminates the opposing fleet wins.

Title: Running the Blockade
description: This game illustrates naval actions during the US Civil War.  These include the Trent Affair in 1861, in which a Union ship boarded the Trent, a British steamer, and seized two Confederate diplomats on their way to Europe. The English were so mad they considered allying with the South. The board also illustrates the Confederate commerce raider Nashville, and the sinking of the Alabama in September 1864.
rules: The game is a competitive maze. In turn, each player tries to move a wooden pin quickly from the entrance to the harbor of Wilmington SC in the middle. Players also may place bets on the various battle scenes. A player's turn ends when hitting a dead end. A player entering one of the battle scenes must pay an amount equal to the stakes present to those who placed them there. The first to Wilmington collects all stakes remaining on the board.

Title: Europa Rundreise
description: This routine "touring Europe" game includes an eerie premonition of what was to come in WWII. The furthest point east that German travelers can reach is Stalingrad. This is a penalty space, with an inscription that reads: “While visiting the city of Tractor Factories, the traveler decides to stay a while. Lose two turns.”
rules: First to complete the circuit around Europe wins.

Title: Ein Flug im Aeroplan
description: This pre-WWI game is focused on the many early types of aircraft. Only the modern warship playing the waters below hints at the military uses of air power soon to come.
rules: First to the end of the 85-space track wins.

Title: Menuette
description: This is a card game where players try to assemble the best meal they can in Britain's still rationed post-war economy.

Title: Objective: Moscow
description: This monster covers a hypothetical invasion of the Soviet Union in the late 1970s. Individual scenarios include a NATO counter-attack in the West, a Chinese invasion in the East and Iranian trouble-making in the south. A grand campaign set at a later involves the United States, Europe, China and Iran all piling on, along with other minor allies. There are 1,200 counters representing 30 countries in all. The map is huge, with 4 sections each of 22" by 34" (55 cm x 85 cm).
rules: Hex movement and combat.

Title: Hornet
description: This target game shows an overhead view of a port with circles around each possible target, each with a point value. These point values range from 1,000 to 5,000 for military targets, and negative points for hitting homes, church, school or hospital.  Wooden disks are launched by a wooden see-saw device. There is a roll-and-move game with a similar theme on the back of the game board.
rules: The player with the highest total points after an agreed number of shots wins.

Title: Modern Military Science
description: Rocket-powered flying gunboats, motorized water-skis, lasers shooting down planes: all are part of this pre-WWII Japanese game featuring military technologies past, present and future.
rules: This is an example of tobi-sugoroku, where each space is marked with the names of six other spaces, and a single die roll determines the space to which a player’s piece moves next. This makes progress toward the goal in the middle space much more erratic.

Title: Warship Game
description: This fairly simple game was included in Boys Club magazine in 1925. The titled Battleships are relegated to small silhouettes in the background. Most of the artwork focuses on civilian vessels at sea and aircraft and an airship in the sky. It shows pilots voluntarily parachuting from planes - a very new practice at the time.
rules: A variant of "16 Musashi": 16 small battleships try to surround the single large one. Each player moves a single piece one space in turn. The big battleship starts in the middle; the small ones on the edges of the square. The small battleships win by surrounding the large one so that it cannot move. The large one captures (removes) two of its attackers by moving between them, and wins by destroying enough attackers that it cannot be surrounded.

Title: Yankee Trader
description: Players compete to pick up and deliver cargoes around the world.

Title: Bunkerspiel, Das
description: The small chess-type board is 16 spaces wide by 12 deep, with 2 x 8 bunkers on top and bottom. Each player gets 12 pieces: 8 Soldiers (small wooden cylinders), 2 Machine Guns (flat disks), 1 Sergeant (small pawn) and 1 Officer (large pawn).
rules: As in chess, each piece has distinct powers. The objective is to capture the opponent's bunker.

Title: L'Attaque (US and German armies)
description: The first edition of L'Attaque featured a French Army facing off against its traditional enemy, the English. During WWI, new editions were issued that included pieces for both the American and German armies.
rules: Same as first edition

Title: Siegeslauf der Deutschen nach Paris
description: This game is a tiny box enclosing a miniature pinball game. It shows the German expectations of a quick victory over the French, following an invasion path through Belgium, across Northern France and directly to Paris.
rules: In this handheld pinball game, players tilt the box to move a small ball. Starting at the German-Belgian border, ball first must get past the fortresses of Luttich, Huy, Namur and Dinant. The ball must cross the Belgian-French border and pass the seven French cities. After crossing the Aisne and Oise rivers a fortified belt from La Fere to Verdun, it must assault the forts before the capital city, “after the conquest of which there is nothing in the way of the victorious invasion of Paris.

Title: Quartette Deutsches Heer
description: One card in each set of four shows "modern" army troops and technology, with the rest showing WWI equivalents. Examples include supply trains using trucks rather than horse-drawn wagons, transport troops with an armored car rather than depending on railway building, and the latest garb for engineers and ski-equipped scouts compared with their WWI peers. However, the latest "innovation" for communications appears to be troops on bicycles with a bag of carrier pigeons!
rules: Standard Authors

Title: Schlacht bei Tannenberg
description: This game about Germany's unexpected and triumphant battle on the Eastern Front is the earliest German game about a battle of WWI. Germany planned to win quickly in the West and had only holding forces in the East. After two defeats, Paul von Hindenburg was pulled out of retirement to take charge, and won a massive victory. The board shows scenes of both battle and of life in the field.
rules: The game is a simple die-rolling race from the initial patrol by Uhlans (German lancers) toward the climax of the battle in the central space 75. As in most such games, players put money in the pot to start. They then add more money to the pot or win some back as they land on various spaces along the way.

Title: Quartette (WWI Heroes)
description: This card deck features portraits and bios of WWI national leaders, front commanders and military heroes. The latter include aerial pioneers Max Immelmann and Oswald Boelke, U-boat commanders Otto Hersing and Otto Weddigen, naval captains Karl von Muller and Graf Nikolaus zu Dohna-Schlodien and Austrian submarine captain Georg von Trapp, better known for later inspiring the movie Sound of Music.
rules: Standard Quartette (Authors, Happy Families)

Title: Unterm Roten Kreuz
description: This game paid tribute to the medics of WWI – the people who risked their own lives to rescue those wounded on the field of battle. It illustrates the full range of activities of the medical corps: recruitment, use of horse-drawn and motorized ambulances, rescue dogs, hospital trains and hospital care. The box also has a title in Dutch, (Onder de afdeeling het roode Kruis) suggesting that the publisher felt it could sell in the neutral Netherlands.
rules: First to complete the 70-space track wins.

Title: Salvo
description: This version of Battleships was issued as a promotional item by Signal Oil. It features an image of two "under-sea-men" playing the game aboard a submarine.
rules: Each player in turn announces 10 shots at once. After the salvo is complete, the defender announces how many hits and which ships were hit, but not the squares of the scoring hits.

Title: Tiddly Winks Barrage Game
description: Tiddly Winks Go to War! The board inset in the box has a river chasm in the middle. Each side has the battery from which players fire their winks and four targets: Air Field, Sea Port, Munitions Works and Dumps, and Capital (the smallest and hardest to hit).
rules: Players are allowed to shoot winks as fast as they can, simultaneously. Winner is first to land a wink on the Capital or to land winks in all three of the other targets.

Title: Quebec 1759
description: This and War of 1812 are the first two games to use the modern block system. Each piece is a square thick enough to stand on its side. The blank side is kept toward the opponent. The friendly side shows the identity of the unit and its strength, which can vary from 1 to 4. The current strength of a unit is shown by the number of pips on its uppermost side. The map shows various areas within Quebec, with the St. Lawrence River dividing the board.
rules: Each turn, a player may move all or some of the blocks in one area to an adjacent area. If ships are present in the right river zone, pieces can move across to an area on the far shore (max 1 per ship). Battles occur when opponents occupy the same area. Players roll a number of dice equal to their current strength and remove an opposing pip for each "6". The English must capture Quebec City within 16 turns with 20 strength points remaining. Otherwise, the French win.

Title: Jeu de la Libération
description: Each of the 52 spaces around edges of this Parchisi-style board is numbered and labelled with the name and national flag of a city on the path to liberation. The outer path is linear neither in terms of date or location. The flags show 17 French spaces, 12 Russian and 10 Italian, along with two each from the U.K. and Belgium, and one each in Poland, Romania, Greece, Yugoslavia and Estonia. Playing pieces are wooden castles topped with national flags.
rules: The playing pieces start from the lowest-numbered space beside their corner, proceed clockwise all the way around the outside of the board, and then up an alley from their national flag toward Berlin in the middle of the board. First to reach Berlin wins.

Title: West Point Cadet
description: This game shows what cadets experience in passing through the West Point Military Academy. It is partly a race game, but victory is determined by the number of tokens held by the end. Good behavior and performance grant tokens; poor grades and discipline have costs. Finishing first gives bonus tokens but a round does not end until all have completed the course.
rules: Players must spin 1 or 2 to start, and reach exam by exact spin and then choose one of three tracks from each (some faster, some more rewarding). Various spaces grant tokens or take them away. First to graduation gets the most tokens there. Game is played in five rounds from start to finish. Winner is the player with the most tokens at the end.

Title: Bombers Over Europe
description: This somber brown-paper map paints a depressingly accurate picture of the horrendous casualties faced by British nighttime bombers. Players undertake bombing missions across Europe. Each target city has a point value, with higher value targets usually more difficult and dangerous to reach. Landing on a dark brown space means the bomber is shot down, and there are 40 of these among the 179 total spaces on the network of movement tracks.
rules: To start a mission, each player chooses a target city and then rolls diceto reach their target and return. Points are scored for reaching the target, but landing on a dark brown space in either direction means the plane is lost. Players get 10 missions in a game. The winner is the player to score the most points for targets hit -- but about half the planes will perish on a typical mission.

Title: Export
description: This is a game about international trade in the sense of commercial control. The board is divided into 16 numbered areas, each with three main (red) cities and other minor ones. Each city is a point of movement along a web of tracks. The objective is to control as many major cities as possible, with points multiplied for all 3 in an area (control) and adjacent controlled areas. Players can move by sea or land, but only one on a turn.
rules: Players must roll a 1 or 2 to leave Berlin, then roll again for actual movement. On a 1, 3 or 5, a piece may move that many dots (cities) in any direction. If landing on or passing over a red city, a token of the player's color is placed. Players may not move through a city with another player's token, except by offering to exchange that token for one of his own (other's choice). Play continues until all major cities are controlled. Player with the most points wins.

Title: Defence
description: The board is focused on the North Sea, with England on one side and northwestern Europe from Brittany to Norway on the other. Across the North Sea lie a series of dotted lines that intersect at multiple points. Each side has five naval bases that mark the end of a dotted line. There is a Minefield hazard dot 1-3 spaces out from each base. There also are two Mutiny dots at intersections and three Fog patches covering multiple dots around major intersections.
rules: Each side starts moving one ship from each base. On each roll or spin, the player must move one ship that many dots forward or backward. A player captures an enemy ship or base by landing on it by exact roll. Ships in fog cannot be captured, while landing on a Minefield or Mutiny dot destroys the ship. The winner is the first to capture an enemy base.

Title: World Culture
description: This colorful sugoroku celebrates the 20th anniversary of Shounen (The Boys) magazine in 1923. It records a host of major events from Marie Curie's discovery of radium in 1904 through the Great Kanto Earthquake in 1923. The board covers both military and economic events, and pays special attention to British as well as Japanese royalty.
rules: Sugoroku: first player to reach the middle space wins.

Title: Warzon
description: This is a fairly complex roll and move game in which each player either takes the side of Britain or Germany. Each player gets 18 units (6 land, 6 sea and 6 air) of varying values (between $100,000 and $1 million). The board has three tracks, for land sea and air. Each track is divided into red (British and allies), grey (German and allies) and green (Neutral) spaces. Each space has a designated result (for instance, collect $x from each other player). Pieces on friendly or neutral territory are safe; those on enemy territory may be captured.
rules: In turn, each player rolls 2 dice three times, and must move a unit on each column (land, sea, air) once. To start, the first roll may be assigned to any column, the second to one remaining and the third with no choice. In future turns, rolls are applied in reverse order of value. The game lasts 6 rounds and ends when all pieces have either completed their tracks or been destroyed. The winner is the player with the highest total value (money plus value of surviving units).

Title: Het Nieuwe Koerierspel
description: This Dutch race game that highlights the difficulties facing couriers during WWI as they moved between the neutral Netherlands, Germany and occupied Belgium.  In particular, it shows that while the Dutch kept their usual, relatively open, border with Germany itself, the long wartime border between the Netherlands and occupied territory was guarded by an electric fence, presumably to keep Belgians from escaping to neutral territory.
rules: Players race to deliver mail to destinations across the Netherlands, western Germany and occupied Belgium.

Title: Prikliucheniya na Lune (Приключения на Луне)
description: This kids' game asks players to fast-forward to the year 2179 and set out across the lunar surface in search of fame and fortune. The glossy paper map shows a portion of the lunar surface, marked with a 7 x 7 grid. The playing pieces are plastic spaceships. There are 7 exploration cards (placed randomly face down on designated spaces), and 7 adventure spaces marked with an exclamation point. These have a matching deck of cards. There also is a sheet of currency tokens.
rules: Two to four players roll a single die in turn. They may move only orthogonally, starting at Port Nubium in the lower left. Play continues until all unknown spaces have been reached and the corresponding rewards or penalties applied. Winner is the player to accumulate the most currency tokens.

Title: Navy Bobs
description: Players try to sink enemy ships in one of two ways: with a small pool cue through the end of the box or via a tilted "torpedo tube" ramp. In either case, a wooden ball is propelled down a long narrow trench in the box, with indentations along the way. Raised on either side are images of ships with small holes next to each of the target indentations. Brown and red wooden pegs are used to mark hits.
rules: Players choose a method of shooting and then take turns propelling the ball down the trench. Wherever the ball lands, the player marks the corresponding ship with a peg of his colour. Multiple hits on the same space have no effect. The first player to place pegs in all of the targets wins.

Title: Bomb Berlin
description: This snakes-and-ladders variant features a Halifax heavy bomber on the box and Bristol Blenheim medium bombers on the board. The 100-space playing surface is placed at an angle, as a diamond. Red single-engine planes act as ladders, swooshing upward, while black twin-engine German planes plunge downward trailing smoke. (These appear to be Me-110 heavy fighters, but are drawn with a single rather than double tail.)
rules: First to reach Berlin at space 100 wins.

Title: Wings
description: This skeet-shooting game features a patented multi-shot gun. It shoots elastic bands stretched from the front of the barrel back over the release mechanism. This is a cogged wheel that allows multiple elastics to be loaded and fired in sequence. Up to six shots are possible. The target is a vertical wooden board with two open windows. Targets are cardboard planes on a wooden base that slide down ramps behind the board.
rules: One player places the planes at the top of a ramp and then releases it. The other player aims the gun and tries to shoot the target when it appears in a window. As in skeet-shooting, players can agree to make the game more difficult by releasing upper and lower planes at random or simultaneously.

Title: Trench War (Teuber)
description: This untitled game shows whimsical scenes of everyday life in the trenches. The designer, Wilhelm Teuber of Berlin, graduated from grammar school in 1912 and almost certainly served as a soldier on the Western Front.
rules: Players advance along a 90-space track representing a tour of duty, from the rear areas to and through the front lines and back again.

Title: Alarm
description: The game board is 10 x 10, with the first and last rows being the players' camps. Each player gets 10 "runners" (planes with white stripe) in camp, and 4 "flyers" starting just forward. Pieces move one at a time, runners orthogonally into or through empty spaces and flyers in any direction and able to jump over friendly and enemy pieces.
rules: The game has two phases: the first lasts until one player loses a sixth runner. Pieces are captured by surrounding them so they cannot move. In the first phase, players may leave but not return to camp, and cannot move into the enemy camp. In the second phase, the enemy camp may be occupied. Winner is the first to fully occupy the enemy camp with 6 runners and 4 flyers, or to leave the opponent without enough units to win.

Title: Victory Bomber
description: This simple target games uses a metal spring launcher to fire wooden bombs. The target grid is a cardboard insert in the game box. The holes have point values ranging from 5 to the black bordered “Victory” space worth 100.  The launcher is placed from 3 to 10 inches from the bottom of the box.
rules: Players take turns shooting a single projectile. These have a wide end and a narrow end. If the narrow end lands in the hole, the point value is doubled. A player who lands the narrow end in the Victory space wins the game instantly. Otherwise play continues until one player reaches a total of 500 points or more.

Title: Champion European War Game
description: This game offers a simple but strategically realistic look at the European theatre of WWI. Movement tracks run from Germany in four directions, to England, Belgium, France and Russia. Player pieces start in their home countries and move toward the enemy. Since the battle results are biased against the attacker, the game tends to bog down into a stalemate.
rules: Players use a spinner to advance along a track of their choice. The spinner's inner ring is used until the enemy piece on the track is reached. Once pieces are adjacent, the outer-ring results are used. Winner is the side that advances into the opposing fortress(es) along three of the four tracks.

Title: Trencho
description: This war game from Australia is a game of Nine Men’s Morris dressed up in wartime camouflage. The points on which the playing pieces sit are shown as artillery positions. The lines along which they move have become trenches filled with troops.
rules: Standard Nine Men's Morris with one minor exception: Artillery allows diagonal moves from the outside corners, but not from the middle ring to the inner.
